The Pi stores values in SQLite (local storage) and runs a rule-based analyzer: *"if soil moisture < 30% AND temperature > 35°C for 5 consecutive readings"*, then it pushes an alert message to AWS IoT. The cloud triggers Twilio to SMS the farmer.

**Why Level 4 (not Level 5)?** Because SMS sending requires an **internet/telephony service (Twilio)**, which is most reliably invoked from the cloud, not from a constrained edge device. The decision (dry soil detected) is local; the actuation (send SMS) is cloud-mediated.

*(1 mark for component identification, 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for hardware table, 2 marks for working + justification.)*

---

### 📗 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **six IoT deployment templates (T1 to T6)**. How does the **distribution of intelligence** change from T1 to T6? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company wants to deploy a **drone swarm for forest-fire detection** where drones must **coordinate among themselves** to form a perimeter and avoid colliding, **without depending on the cloud**. Identify the appropriate **IoT Level and Deployment Template**. Justify with a **functional diagram** and **hardware recommendation**. **(7 marks)**

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q(B):

**Solution (a): The Six Deployment Templates**

A deployment template specifies **which physical device** hosts which functional component.

| Template | Host of S | Host of P | Host of L | Host of A | Host of Act | Host of C | Hardware |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-----------|----------|
| **T1** | S/A | Cloud | Cloud | Cloud | Cloud | Cloud | 1 sensor + 1 cloud |
| **T2** | S/A | S/A | Cloud | Cloud | Cloud | Cloud | 1 sensor + 1 cloud |
| **T3** | S/A | S/A | Gateway | Cloud | Cloud | Cloud | 1 sensor + 1 GW + 1 cloud |
| **T4** | S/A | S/A | Gateway | Gateway | Cloud | Cloud | 1 sensor + 1 GW + 1 cloud |
| **T5** | S/A | S/A | Gateway | Gateway | Gateway | Cloud | 1 sensor + 1 GW (no cloud) |
| **T6** | S/A | S/A | Gateway | Gateway | Gateway | Gateway (mesh) | Multiple GWs (peer-to-peer) |

**Distribution of intelligence from T1 → T6:**
- In **T1**, the sensor node is a **"thin" device** — it has only a transducer and a transmitter. The cloud is "fat" with all four other components.
- From **T2 to T5**, intelligence **migrates from cloud to edge in a strict stepwise fashion** — pre-processing first (T2), then local storage (T3), then analysis (T4), then actuation (T5).
- In **T6**, the cloud disappears entirely and intelligence is **horizontally distributed** across gateways that coordinate via mesh protocols.
- This migration is **monotonic and non-reversible** — you cannot go from T5 back to T1 without re-architecting.

*(1 mark per template × 6 = 6 marks, 1 mark for migration narrative.)*

---

**Solution (b): Drone Swarm → Level 6 + Template T6**

**Step 1 — Analyze the requirements:**
1. Multiple drones (≥ 3) must cooperate.
2. They must **avoid cloud** (forest areas have poor connectivity).
3. They must **form a perimeter** (collective action).
4. They must **avoid collision** (coordination).

**Step 2 — Identify the Level:**

Since **coordination among multiple autonomous edge devices, with no cloud**, the system must support **S + P + L + A + Act + C** locally. This is **Level 6** (all six components local). The corresponding **Deployment Template is T6**.

**Step 3 — Functional Diagram:**

```
[Drone 1: S → P → L → A → Act → C]  <--mesh-->  [Drone 2: S → P → L → A → Act → C]
              |                                                   |
              +<---------------mesh---------------->+---------------+
                                                               |
                                                       [Drone N: S → P → L → A → Act → C]
```

**Step 4 — Hardware Recommendation:**

| Drone Component | Recommended Hardware |
|-----------------|----------------------|
| Sensing (thermal, smoke) | FLIR Lepton thermal camera + MQ-2 smoke sensor |
| Pre-processing | Onboard GPU/NPU (e.g., Jetson Nano) — image noise reduction, ROI cropping |
| Local Storage | 32 GB microSD or NVMe SSD |
| Analysis | Jetson Nano running TensorRT for fire detection ML model |
| Action | Flight controller (Pixhawk) controlling motors + gimbal camera |
| Coordination | 802.11s mesh radio or LoRa + MAVLink protocol |

**Step 5 — Justification for Level 6:**
- **Why not Level 5?** Because Level 5 supports only **one** autonomous device. A **swarm** requires **peer-to-peer coordination**, which is the defining 6th component.
- **Why not the cloud?** Forest areas have **intermittent or no internet**; the drones cannot depend on a master server.
- **Why template T6 specifically?** T6 is the only template where **Coordination is hosted on the edge (mesh)**, satisfying the swarm requirement.

*(2 marks for Level 6 + T6 identification, 2 marks for functional diagram, 2 marks for hardware table, 1 mark for justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "Levels" with "Layers" of the OSI/IoT protocol stack** — Levels here mean **distribution of intelligence**, not protocol layers. (Penalty: 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Forgetting that Level 6 is uniquely defined by Coordination** — students often write "Level 5 has all components" and skip the peer-to-peer aspect. Always mention **coordination** for Level 6.
> 3. **Writing "cloud is not required" for Level 4** — In Level 4, the **action is in the cloud**, so cloud is still required. Only Levels 5 and 6 are fully cloud-free.
> 4. **Mixing up Deployment Templates with IoT Levels** — A Level is *logical*, a Template is *physical*. Examiners expect both to be discussed distinctly.
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without labeling** — Unlabeled blocks fetch **0 marks** for the diagram portion. Always write "S", "P", "L", "A", "Act", "C" inside the boxes.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **6 IoT Levels (L1–L6)** + **6 Deployment Templates (T1–T6)** — one-to-one mapping.
- ✅ **Five core components** (S, P, L, A, Act) + **one extension (C)** for Level 6.
- ✅ **Cloud dependency decreases** from **L1 (100%) → L6 (0%)** following $D_L = \frac{6-L}{5}$.
- ✅ **Prefix property:** once a component is moved to the cloud, all later components must also be in the cloud — except for the addition of C in L6.
- ✅ **L1–L2:** Cloud-centric; cheap; offline-unsafe.
- ✅ **L3:** Edge logger pattern; survives outages; no local decision.
- ✅ **L4:** Local decision + cloud-mediated action.
- ✅ **L5:** Fully autonomous edge; no cloud at all.
- ✅ **L6:** Level 5 + **inter-edge coordination**; drone swarms, smart grids, V2V.
- ✅ **Template T_N** maps: S/A node hosts first 1–2 components, Gateway hosts middle components, Cloud hosts the rest (except in T5/T6).
- ✅ **Real-time** + **offline** requirements → push toward L5/L6; **cost** + **simplicity** requirements → push toward L1/L2.
- ✅ **KTU keyword triggers:** "edge computing", "offline", "real-time", "low-latency", "autonomous", "swarm", "federated" — these all point to higher levels.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for memory:** **"SPLAAC"** = Sense, Pre-process, Local-store, Analyze, Act, Coordinate.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for migration:** "PLAAC" moves from cloud to edge as level increases by 1.
- ✅ **Draw functional diagrams in exams** — examiners expect them and award 2–3 marks for them.
- ✅ **Mention hardware in deployment templates** — Arduino/RPi/Jetson/Cloud for S, GW, Cloud respectively.
- ✅ **Always cite a use case** for the Level you describe — it earns the "application" cognitive level marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Domain Specific IoT - Home Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Domain Specific IoT - Home Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Home Automation (a.k.a. Smart Home / Domotics)** refers to the domain-specific application of the Internet of Things wherein residential appliances, lighting, HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning), security systems, and entertainment units are interconnected through a common network, enabling **remote monitoring, intelligent control, and autonomous decision-making** via sensors, actuators, communication protocols, and cloud/edge computing platforms.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the IoT reference model, Home Automation is the **consumer-grade instantiation** of the IoT ecosystem — it forms the **"Things Layer"** plus the **"Network & Application Layer"** rolled into a single user-facing vertical.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Smart Butler" Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Smart Butler" House:**
> Imagine your home as a traditional house with a very attentive butler. You say, *"I'm cold"* — the butler turns on the heater, closes the window, and dims the warm lights. You leave for office — the butler locks doors, switches off AC, and arms the burglar alarm. You return — the geyser is hot, lights are on, and your favourite playlist is queued. **Home Automation is essentially embedding this "butler" (sensors + microcontrollers + cloud logic) into the building itself, and connecting him to your phone.**

The butler’s **eyes & ears** = **Sensors** (temperature, motion, light, gas).
The butler’s **hands** = **Actuators** (relays, motors, smart switches).
The butler’s **brain** = **Microcontroller/Gateway** (ESP32, Raspberry Pi).
The butler’s **telephone** = **Network protocols** (Wi-Fi, Zigbee, MQTT).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Classification (KTU Board Keywords)

| Metric | Standard / Typical Value | KTU Board Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Communication Range** | **< 100 m (indoor)** | Differentiates PAN/LAN-based home IoT from WAN IoT |
| **Power Consumption** | **< 5 W per node (typical)** | Battery-operated sensor nodes |
| **Latency Tolerance** | **< 1 s (real-time control)** | Critical for security/locks |
| **Data Rate** | **250 kbps (Zigbee) – 54 Mbps (Wi-Fi)** | Protocol selection criterion |
| **Device Density** | **50–200 devices per home (by 2030)** | Scalability metric |

### 1.4 KTU Syllabus Tagging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Mapping:** This topic falls under *"Domain Specific IoTs"* — vertical sub-domain illustration. It is a **high-frequency 14-mark question topic** in KTU ESE, often appearing as a "Design a smart home system for XYZ" question.
> **CO Mapping:** CO1 — *Understand the concepts and terminology of IoT systems and domain-specific applications.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Home Automation System

A Home Automation IoT follows the **standard 4-layer IoT architecture** customized for residential deployment:

#### **Layer 1 — Perception / Sensor Layer (The "Things")**
- Physical hardware that *senses* and *acts*.
- **Sensors:** Temperature (DHT11/22), Humidity, PIR (motion), Gas (MQ-2/MQ-135), Light (LDR), Door/Window reed switch, Smoke, Water-leak.
- **Actuators:** Relays (5V/12V coil), Servo motors (for curtains/locks), Solenoid valves (for gas/water cutoff), Smart dimmers, Buzzer/Siren.
- **Identity:** Each device carries a unique **UUID/MAC address** — this is the "Thing" in IoT.

#### **Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer (The "Path")**
- **Short-range wireless:** Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n), Bluetooth/BLE, Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4), Z-Wave, Thread.
- **Wired legacy:** KNX, X10 (power-line communication), Ethernet, RS-485.
- **Edge Gateway:** Raspberry Pi / ESP32 / Smart-Home Hub (Samsung SmartThings, Google Nest Hub) aggregating protocols.
- **Backbone to cloud:** MQTT (lightweight publish-subscribe), HTTP/REST, CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol).

#### **Layer 3 — Middleware / Processing Layer (The "Brain")**
- Runs on **Cloud (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT)** or **Edge (local gateway)**.
- Functions: data aggregation, rule engine (IF-THEN logic), ML-based prediction, device shadow management.
- Example rule: `IF (motion == TRUE) AND (time > 22:00) THEN (turn_on_light) AND (send_phone_alert)`.

#### **Layer 4 — Application Layer (The "Face")**
- **Mobile Apps** (Blynk, MIT App Inventor, proprietary vendor apps).
- **Voice Assistants** (Alexa Skills, Google Assistant Actions).
- **Dashboards** (Grafana, custom Web UI).
- **Notification channels** (SMS, Email, Push).

---

### 2.2 Core Operating Logic — "Sense → Compute → Actuate" Loop

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental control loop in any home automation node is a closed feedback loop. Mastering this loop is **mandatory for KTU 14-mark design questions.**

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Sense (Data Acquisition):** A sensor samples a physical quantity (e.g., DHT22 reads room temperature every 5 seconds) and converts it to a digital signal via an **ADC** (Analog-to-Digital Converter).
2. **Pre-process:** Microcontroller (ESP8266/ESP32) applies filtering (e.g., moving average) to remove sensor noise.
3. **Transmit:** Sensor reading is published over MQTT topic `home/livingroom/temperature` to the broker.
4. **Decide:** Cloud rule engine or local edge controller evaluates the rule `IF temperature > 30°C THEN turn_on_AC`.
5. **Command:** An actuation command is published on topic `home/livingroom/ac/cmd` with payload `{"state":"ON"}`.
6. **Actuate:** The smart plug / relay receives the command and closes the circuit driving the AC.
7. **Feedback:** The new state is confirmed back to the dashboard (device shadow update).
8. **Log:** The event is timestamped and stored for analytics/history.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Protocols & Standards

| Protocol | Standard | Frequency | Range | Data Rate | Power | Typical Use in Home |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi** | IEEE 802.11 | **2.4 / 5 GHz** | ~50 m | **54–1200 Mbps** | High | Cameras, hubs, high-bandwidth |
| **Bluetooth/BLE** | IEEE 802.15.1 | **2.4 GHz** | ~10 m | **1–2 Mbps** | Low | Wearables, beacons, door locks |
| **Zigbee** | IEEE 802.15.4 | **2.4 GHz** | ~30 m | **250 kbps** | Very Low | Mesh of lights/sensors |
| **Z-Wave** | Proprietary | **908.42 MHz** | ~30 m | **100 kbps** | Very Low | Smart locks, thermostats |
| **Thread** | IEEE 802.15.4 | **2.4 GHz** | ~30 m | **250 kbps** | Very Low | Nest, Apple HomeKit |
| **LoRa** | Semtech | **868/915 MHz** | km-scale | **0.3–50 kbps** | Ultra Low | Not typical (long-range) |
| **MQTT** | OASIS | TCP/IP-based | N/A | Lightweight | Low | **Messaging backbone (app layer)** |
| **CoAP** | IETF RFC 7252 | UDP-based | N/A | REST-like | Very Low | Constrained devices |
| **KNX** | ISO/IEC 14543 | Wired/Powerline | Building | 9.6 kbps | Medium | Premium wired homes |
| **X10** | Legacy PLC | Powerline | Home | 50 bps | Low | Legacy retrofitting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** "Compare Zigbee and Wi-Fi for home automation" is a **favourite 7-mark sub-question**. Always state: *Wi-Fi = high bandwidth, high power, point-to-master; Zigbee = low bandwidth, mesh, ultra-low power, ideal for 50+ battery sensors.*

---

### 2.4 Mathematical Foundations Used in Home Automation

Although Home Automation is largely systems-design, two quantitative relations frequently appear in KTU papers.

#### **A. Power Consumption of a Sensor Node (Battery Life Estimation)**

Let a node consume current $I_{active}$ when awake for time $t_{active}$, and $I_{sleep}$ when asleep for time $t_{sleep}$ over a duty cycle of period $T = t_{active} + t_{sleep}$.

The **average current** is:

$$\bar{I} = \frac{I_{active} \cdot t_{active} + I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}}{T}$$

The **battery life (in hours)** for a battery of capacity $C$ (in mAh) is:

$$L = \frac{C}{\bar{I}} \quad [\text{hours}]$$

#### **B. Network Latency for Real-Time Control**

For a message traversing $N$ hops in a mesh network (e.g., Zigbee), each hop contributing delay $d_{hop}$:

$$D_{total} = N \cdot d_{hop} + d_{prop} + d_{queue}$$

For KTU: this is used to justify protocol choice — a security door-lock must have $D_{total} < 500$ ms, ruling out long-range, low-priority protocols.

#### **C. Smart Lighting Energy Saving (Often a 3-mark Part-A question)**

Let baseline consumption be $P_{base}$ (W) for duration $H$ hours/day. After automation, occupancy sensor enables lights for fraction $f$ of the time.

$$\text{Daily Energy Saved} = P_{base} \cdot H \cdot (1 - f) \quad [\text{Wh}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** A smart home typically achieves **20–30% energy reduction** in lighting and **15–25% in HVAC** — this justifies ROI calculations often asked in viva/practical exams.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utilities of Home Automation

1. **Energy Management** — Smart meters + AI scheduling reduce bills.
2. **Elderly/Disabled Assistance** — Fall detection, automated medication reminders, voice control.
3. **Security & Surveillance** — Smart locks, IP cameras, motion-triggered alarms.
4. **Convenience & Lifestyle** — Scene control (e.g., "Good Morning" scene = blinds open + geyser on + news play).
5. **Predictive Maintenance** — Sensor data (e.g., refrigerator compressor current) fed to ML model predicts failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical — Battery Lifetime of a Zigbee Window Sensor

**Problem:** A Zigbee-based window sensor node operates as follows:
- Active current $I_{active} = 30 \text{ mA}$ for $t_{active} = 50 \text{ ms}$ (transmit a packet every 10 minutes).
- Sleep current $I_{sleep} = 5 \mu\text{A}$ for the rest of the period.
- Powered by a CR2032 coin cell of capacity $C = 220 \text{ mAh}$.
- **Find the expected battery lifetime in days.**

#### **Step 1 — Identify the duty cycle period.**
$$T = 10 \text{ minutes} = 600 \text{ s}$$
$$t_{active} = 50 \text{ ms} = 0.050 \text{ s}$$
$$t_{sleep} = T - t_{active} = 600 - 0.050 = 599.95 \text{ s}$$

#### **Step 2 — Express sleep current in compatible units.**
$$I_{sleep} = 5 \, \mu\text{A} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mA} = 0.005 \text{ mA}$$

#### **Step 3 — Apply the average current formula.**
$$\bar{I} = \frac{I_{active} \cdot t_{active} + I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}}{T}$$

Substituting values:
$$\bar{I} = \frac{(30 \text{ mA})(0.050 \text{ s}) + (0.005 \text{ mA})(599.95 \text{ s})}{600 \text{ s}}$$

#### **Step 4 — Compute the active contribution.**
$$(30)(0.050) = 1.5 \text{ mA·s}$$

#### **Step 5 — Compute the sleep contribution.**
$$(0.005)(599.95) = 2.99975 \text{ mA·s}$$

#### **Step 6 — Sum and divide.**
$$\bar{I} = \frac{1.5 + 2.99975}{600} = \frac{4.49975}{600} \text{ mA}$$

$$\bar{I} \approx 0.0075 \text{ mA} = 7.5 \, \mu\text{A}$$

#### **Step 7 — Compute battery lifetime in hours.**
$$L = \frac{C}{\bar{I}} = \frac{220 \text{ mAh}}{0.0075 \text{ mA}}$$

$$L \approx 29{,}333 \text{ hours}$$

#### **Step 8 — Convert to days.**
$$L_{days} = \frac{29{,}333}{24} \approx 1{,}222 \text{ days} \approx 3.35 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This justifies why Zigbee is the de-facto choice for battery-powered home sensors — multi-year lifetimes are achievable. CRITICAL: Show unit conversion explicitly in KTU exams — unit marks are easy to score.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Energy Saved by Smart Lighting

**Problem:** A 60 W incandescent bulb in a corridor is replaced by an LED bulb (9 W). An occupancy sensor ensures the light is ON for only 40% of the time. The corridor is used 12 hours/day. **Calculate the annual energy saved in kWh and the cost saved at ₹8/kWh.**

#### **Step 1 — Compute original daily energy.**
$$E_{old} = P_{old} \cdot H = 60 \text{ W} \times 12 \text{ h} = 720 \text{ Wh/day}$$

#### **Step 2 — Compute new daily energy (with both LED + occupancy).**
$$E_{new} = P_{new} \cdot H \cdot f = 9 \text{ W} \times 12 \text{ h} \times 0.40 = 43.2 \text{ Wh/day}$$

#### **Step 3 — Compute daily energy saved.**
$$E_{saved/day} = 720 - 43.2 = 676.8 \text{ Wh/day}$$

#### **Step 4 — Convert to annual kWh.**
$$E_{saved/year} = 676.8 \times 365 = 247{,}032 \text{ Wh} = 247.03 \text{ kWh}$$

#### **Step 5 — Compute annual monetary savings.**
$$\text{Savings} = 247.03 \times 8 = \text{₹}1{,}976.25 \text{ per year per bulb}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** When extrapolated to 50 lights in a building, this becomes **₹98,800/year** — a powerful argument that KTU examiners love to see in design questions.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Protocol Selection (14-Mark Ready)

| Criterion | Wi-Fi | Zigbee | Z-Wave | BLE | KNX (Wired) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Topology | Star | **Mesh** | **Mesh** | Star/P2P | Bus/Tree |
| Max Nodes | ~32 per AP | **65,000** | 232 | ~7 simultaneous | ~58,000 |
| Battery Life | Days–Weeks | **Years** | **Years** | Months | Mains-powered |
| Installation | Easy | Medium (hub) | Medium | Easy | Complex (rewire) |
| Cost per node | Low | Low | Medium | Low | High |
| Interference | High (shared) | Moderate | **Low (sub-GHz)** | High (2.4 GHz) | None (wired) |
| Best For | Cameras, hubs | Sensors mesh | Locks, plugs | Beacons | Premium retrofits |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** **"Zigbee for Sensing, Wi-Fi for Streaming, KNX for Permanence, BLE for Beacons, Z-Wave for Locks."**

---

### 3.4 Decision Tree for KTU Design Questions

When a KTU question says *"Design a smart home system for [scenario]"*, follow this hierarchy:

1. **Identify user requirements** → e.g., "elderly living alone, mobility-impaired, 2BHK flat, ₹30,000 budget".
2. **Map requirements to sensors/actuators** → fall detector, gas leak sensor, voice-controlled lights, automatic medication box.
3. **Select communication protocol** → Wi-Fi for camera, Zigbee for sensor mesh, BLE for wearable.
4. **Choose gateway/processor** → Raspberry Pi 4 / ESP32 hub.
5. **Decide cloud vs edge** → cloud for analytics, edge for safety-critical decisions.
6. **Define control logic** → IF-THEN rules + ML anomaly detection.
7. **Address security & privacy** → TLS, device authentication, local data.
8. **Justify cost-benefit** → energy savings + safety premium vs ₹cost.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Home Automation IoT System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENS["PERCEPTION LAYER - SENSE and ACT"]
        S1["Temp Sensor DHT22"]
        S2["PIR Motion Sensor"]
        S3["Gas Sensor MQ2"]
        S4["LDR Light Sensor"]
        S5["Reed Switch Door Window"]
        A1["Relay Module 5V"]
        A2["Servo Motor Lock"]
        A3["Smart Dimmer LED"]
        A4["Buzzer Siren"]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["EDGE LAYER - GATEWAY MICROCONTROLLER"]
        MC["ESP32 / Raspberry Pi - Local Decision Hub"]
    end

    subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER - PROTOCOLS"]
        P1["Wi-Fi 802.11"]
        P2["Zigbee Mesh"]
        P3["BLE 5.0"]
        P4["MQTT over TLS"]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["MIDDLEWARE CLOUD LAYER"]
        BR["MQTT Broker HiveMQ"]
        RE["Rule Engine IFTTT Custom"]
        DB["TimeSeries DB InfluxDB"]
        ML["ML Anomaly Detector"]
    end

    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER - USER INTERFACE"]
        U1["Mobile App Blynk"]
        U2["Voice Assistant Alexa Google"]
        U3["Web Dashboard Grafana"]
        U4["Push Notification SMS Email"]
    end

    S1 --> MC
    S2 --> MC
    S3 --> MC
    S4 --> MC
    S5 --> MC
    MC --> A1
    MC --> A2
    MC --> A3
    MC --> A4

    MC --> P1
    MC --> P2
    MC --> P3
    P1 --> P4
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> BR

    BR --> RE
    BR --> DB
    DB --> ML
    RE --> BR
    ML --> U4

    BR --> U1
    BR --> U2
    BR --> U3
```

### 4.2 Sequence Flow — "Intruder Detection" Scenario

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PIR as PIR Motion Sensor
    participant ESP as ESP32 Gateway
    participant MQT as MQTT Broker
    participant CLD as Cloud Rule Engine
    participant CAM as IP Camera
    participant APP as Mobile App
    participant BUZ as Buzzer Siren

    PIR->>ESP: Motion Detected HIGH
    ESP->>MQT: Publish home/entrance/motion {"value":1}
    MQT->>CLD: Forward message
    CLD->>MQT: Publish home/camera/snapshot_cmd
    MQT->>CAM: Trigger snapshot
    CAM->>CLD: Upload image to cloud storage
    CLD->>MQT: Publish home/buzzer/cmd {"state":"ON"}
    MQT->>ESP: Forward to gateway
    ESP->>BUZ: Activate siren
    CLD->>APP: Push Notification "Motion Detected at Entrance"
    APP-->>CLD: User acknowledges
```

### 4.3 Comparative Mesh Topology Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    HUB["Smart Home Hub / Coordinator"] --- Z1["Zigbee Node - Living Room Light"]
    HUB --- Z2["Zigbee Node - Bedroom Sensor"]
    HUB --- Z3["Zigbee Node - Kitchen Plug"]
    Z1 --- Z2
    Z2 --- Z3
    Z3 --- Z1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The triangular links between Z1, Z2, Z3 represent **self-healing mesh** — if HUB-Z3 link fails, Z3 still reaches the hub via Z2 → Z1 → HUB. This is the **single most important differentiator** between Zigbee and Wi-Fi for KTU answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define Home Automation. List any four smart home applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Home Automation is the domain-specific IoT application that integrates sensors, actuators, communication networks, and intelligent controllers to enable **remote and automatic** monitoring and control of household appliances and environments.

**Four applications:**
1. **Smart Lighting** — automatic on/off and dimming using motion and LDR sensors.
2. **Climate Control** — smart thermostats (e.g., Nest) regulating HVAC.
3. **Security & Surveillance** — smart locks, IP cameras, motion-triggered alarms.
4. **Energy Management** — smart meters and appliance scheduling for bill reduction.

*(1 mark for definition, 0.5 mark each for 4 applications, 0.5 for the keyword "remote/automatic").*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between Zigbee and Wi-Fi for home automation. Mention one limitation of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Zigbee | Wi-Fi |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | IEEE 802.15.4 | IEEE 802.11 |
| Power | Ultra-low (years on coin cell) | High (days on battery) |
| Topology | Mesh | Star (AP-centric) |
| Data Rate | 250 kbps | up to 1.2 Gbps |

**Limitation of Zigbee:** Low data rate — unsuitable for video streaming.
**Limitation of Wi-Fi:** High power consumption — unsuitable for battery sensors.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Never confuse "automation" with "remote control alone"** — KTU specifically expects *autonomous decision-making* using sensors and rules. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Do not list IoT layers without explaining one example per layer** — generic textbook lists fetch only 50% marks. (–2 to –3 marks)
> 3. **For numerical battery-life questions, ALWAYS show the duty-cycle formula explicitly** — silent skipping = –2 marks.
> 4. **Always justify protocol choice with a trade-off** — "Zigbee because low-power mesh" alone is incomplete; add *"because the system has 50+ battery sensors that need multi-year life"*.
> 5. **Sketching a block diagram is COMPULSORY in design questions** — missing diagram = –2 marks guaranteed.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model Paper 2024, ESE Module 1, Set B]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-layer architecture** of a Home Automation IoT system. List **two sensors and two actuators** used in each of the following subsystems: *Lighting, Security, Climate Control*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **MQTT and HTTP** as application-layer protocols for smart home communication. Justify why MQTT is preferred in resource-constrained home IoT deployments. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Block Diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 above — student should redraw with proper labelled blocks). **(2 marks for diagram, 5 marks for explanation)**

**Architecture Explanation:**

1. **Perception Layer (1 mark):** Contains physical sensors and actuators that interface directly with the environment. *Example:* DHT22 (temp), MQ-2 (gas), PIR (motion), Relay (actuator).
2. **Network Layer (1 mark):** Provides wired/wireless communication using Wi-Fi, Zigbee, BLE, or KNX. The gateway (ESP32/RPi) aggregates sensor traffic.
3. **Middleware/Processing Layer (1 mark):** Hosts the rule engine (e.g., Node-RED), data storage (InfluxDB), and analytics/ML modules. Decision-making and event correlation occur here.
4. **Application Layer (1 mark):** User-facing interfaces — mobile app, voice assistant, web dashboard, push notifications.

**Sensor/Actuator Table (1 mark):**

| Subsystem | Sensor 1 | Sensor 2 | Actuator 1 | Actuator 2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lighting | LDR | PIR | Relay | Smart Dimmer |
| Security | PIR | Reed Switch | Solenoid Lock | Buzzer/Siren |
| Climate | DHT22 | Thermostat | Compressor Relay | Smart Fan |

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | MQTT | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | **Publish/Subscribe** | **Request/Response** |
| Transport | TCP | TCP |
| Header Size | **2 bytes (fixed)** | Hundreds of bytes |
| Power Use | **Very Low** | High |
| Suitable Device | Constrained sensors | Smartphones, browsers |
| Push Capability | **Native (broker pushes)** | Polling required |
| Example | home/livingroom/temp | GET /api/temperature |

**Why MQTT is preferred (4 marks):**
1. **Lightweight footprint** — 2-byte header suits ESP8266/Zigbee nodes with limited RAM/flash.
2. **Asynchronous push** — a sensor publishes once; multiple subscribers (app, cloud, history DB) receive without polling, reducing bandwidth.
3. **Decoupled architecture** — sensor does not need to know about clients; the broker handles fan-out.
4. **Quality of Service levels (QoS 0/1/2)** — guarantees message delivery for critical events like intrusion alerts.
5. **Last-Will-and-Testament feature** — detects offline devices, vital for security sensors.

**Example topic hierarchy (for 1 extra mark):**
`home/{room}/{device}/{metric}` — e.g., `home/kitchen/gas/value`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Module 1, Set A]*

**(a)** Design a **Home Automation system for an elderly person living alone** in a 2BHK flat. List the sensors, actuators, communication protocol, and gateway you would use. Justify each choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **PIR-based motion sensor** with a Zigbee radio operates in the following duty cycle: active at $I_{act} = 25 \text{ mA}$ for $t_{act} = 100 \text{ ms}$, and sleep at $I_{sleep} = 3 \, \mu\text{A}$ for the remainder of a 5-minute period. The sensor is powered by a **CR2477 coin cell (1000 mAh)**. Calculate the **battery lifetime in months**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**System Design Table (5 marks):**

| Component | Selected Device | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Fall detection | **MPU6050 accelerometer (waist-worn)** | 6-axis, low-power, detects orientation change |
| Gas leak | **MQ-2** | Detects LPG/smoke, audio-visual alarm |
| Door open | **Reed switch** | Binary, ultra-low power, reliable |
| Activity monitor | **PIR + BLE beacon** | Detects room occupancy without cameras (privacy) |
| Voice control | **Google Nest Mini** | Hands-free, ideal for mobility-impaired |
| Smart lighting | **Zigbee bulbs + PIR** | Auto-on at night for bathroom visits |
| Medication reminder | **Smart pillbox with RTC + buzzer** | Time-based alert, local operation |
| Emergency button | **Wearable BLE panic button** | One-touch alert to family + hospital |
| Gateway | **Raspberry Pi 4 + Zigbee dongle + Wi-Fi** | Aggregates Zigbee mesh, runs Node-RED, sends alerts |
| Cloud | **AWS IoT / Firebase** | Family notifications, event log |

**Communication Protocol Justification (2 marks):**
- **Zigbee** for sensors — mesh ensures coverage across flat, multi-year battery life.
- **Wi-Fi** for gateway-to-cloud — high bandwidth for video calls/alerts.
- **BLE** for wearable — short-range, ultra-low power.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert all currents to mA.**
$$I_{act} = 25 \text{ mA}, \quad I_{sleep} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — Define duty cycle period.**
$$T = 5 \text{ min} = 300 \text{ s}, \quad t_{act} = 0.1 \text{ s}, \quad t_{sleep} = 299.9 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply average current formula.**
$$\bar{I} = \frac{(25)(0.1) + (0.003)(299.9)}{300}$$

$$= \frac{2.5 + 0.8997}{300} = \frac{3.3997}{300}$$

$$\bar{I} = 0.01133 \text{ mA} = 11.33 \, \mu\text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Compute lifetime in hours.**
$$L = \frac{C}{\bar{I}} = \frac{1000 \text{ mAh}}{0.01133 \text{ mA}} = 88{,}261 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to months.**
$$L_{months} = \frac{88{,}261}{24 \times 30} = \frac{88{,}261}{720} \approx 122.6 \text{ months} \approx 10.2 \text{ years}$$

**Answer: ≈ 122 months (≈ 10.2 years).**

This is realistic for a Zigbee PIR sensor — multi-year battery life is a **key selling point of Zigbee** for home automation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Rapid Recap — Home Automation:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Home Automation = residential IoT for *remote + autonomous* control of appliances, lighting, HVAC, security.
- [ ] **Four Layers:** Perception → Network → Middleware → Application. **Always diagram this.**
- [ ] **Sense-Compute-Actuate loop** is the universal control flow.
- [ ] **Protocols:** Wi-Fi (high BW, high power), Zigbee/Z-Wave (low power mesh), BLE (short range), KNX (wired premium).
- [ ] **MQTT over HTTP** for constrained devices — 2-byte header, pub/sub, QoS, Last-Will.
- [ ] **Battery life formula:** $\bar{I} = \frac{I_{act} t_{act} + I_{slp} t_{slp}}{T}$, $L = C / \bar{I}$.
- [ ] **Energy saving formula:** $E_{saved} = P \cdot H \cdot (1 - f)$.
- [ ] **Mesh topology = self-healing** — KTU's favourite differentiator.
- [ ] **Design questions** = list sensors, actuators, protocol, gateway, cloud, security, **then justify each**.
- [ ] **Always include a labelled block diagram** in 14-mark answers.
- [ ] **Security/Privacy** — TLS, authentication, local edge processing for sensitive data.
- [ ] **Standards** to remember: IEEE 802.15.4 (Zigbee/Thread), IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi), IEEE 802.15.1 (BLE), OASIS MQTT, IETF CoAP.
- [ ] **Typical numbers:** Zigbee 250 kbps / 2.4 GHz, Wi-Fi 54+ Mbps / 2.4–5 GHz, PIR ~5 m range, DHT22 ±0.5°C.
- [ ] **Realistic battery life** of Zigbee sensor = 3–10 years with CR coin cells.
- [ ] **Energy savings of smart home** = 20–30% lighting, 15–25% HVAC.

> [!WARNING]
> **One-Line Punchline for KTU Viva:** *"Home Automation is the consumer-grade IoT vertical that uses low-power mesh protocols like Zigbee/BLE for sensing, MQTT for messaging, and a cloud/edge gateway for autonomous rule-based control, achieving energy savings of 20–30% and supporting elderly-care and security use-cases."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, **Energy** refers to the electrical power required to operate the heterogeneous network of constrained devices, sensors, actuators, communication modules, and processing units that constitute an IoT ecosystem. More formally, energy in IoT encompasses the entire lifecycle of power generation, storage, conversion, management, and consumption that enables the autonomous operation of resource-constrained edge devices, often deployed in inaccessible, hazardous, or remote environments where battery replacement or mains-power connectivity is impractical.

The energy subsystem in an IoT node typically includes:
- **Energy Source** (battery, harvested ambient energy, mains supply)
- **Energy Storage** (supercapacitors, Li-ion cells, thin-film batteries)
- **Power Management Unit (PMU)** (DC-DC converters, regulators, switching circuits)
- **Load** (sensors, MCU, radio transceiver)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Energy is one of the three foundational pillars of IoT (along with *Sensing* and *Connectivity*). Without an efficient energy strategy, an IoT deployment is economically and operationally infeasible — this is often quoted in board questions as the *“Energy Trilemma of IoT.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small remote village in the hills of Kerala where neither the government electricity board nor fuel delivery services reach. To keep a weather-monitoring station alive 24/7, the villagers must:
1. **Generate** their own electricity (solar panel, micro-wind turbine).
2. **Store** it for cloudy/rainy days (a battery).
3. **Use it frugally** — switching off the radio transmitter when no one is listening, dimming displays, sleeping when idle.
4. **Harvest** any "free" energy around them — a vibrating bridge, the warmth of the sun, ambient Wi-Fi signals.

An IoT node faces the *exact same constraints*. It is a tiny, unattended computer that must **scavenge, store, budget, and ration** every joule of energy. The discipline that makes this possible is called **Energy-Aware Computing** in IoT.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (Board-Exam Friendly):**
> - **Energy (E):** Measured in **Joules (J)** or **Watt-hours (Wh)**. $1\,\text{Wh} = 3600\,\text{J}$.
> - **Power (P):** Rate of energy consumption, in **Watts (W)**. $P = \dfrac{dE}{dt}$.
> - **Energy Harvesting:** Capturing ambient energy (solar, RF, thermal, vibration) and converting it to electrical energy.
> - **Duty Cycling:** Repeatedly switching a device between *active* and *sleep* states to minimize average power draw.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in IoT Energy Analysis

- **Battery Capacity (Q):** Measured in **mAh** (milliampere-hours) or **Coulombs (C)**.
- **Energy Density:** **J/kg** (gravimetric) or **J/m³** (volumetric).
- **Average Power Consumption (P_avg):** $P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{mission}}}$.
- **Node Lifetime:** $L = \dfrac{E_{\text{stored}}}{P_{\text{avg}}}$.
- **Power-Delay Product (PDP):** $PDP = P_{\text{active}} \times t_{\text{active}}$ (used to compare processor efficiencies).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Energy Stack of an IoT Node

An IoT node's energy budget is consumed by **four subsystems**, in typical descending order of demand:

1. **Radio / Communication Module** (60–80% of total energy) — Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, Zigbee transceivers.
2. **Microcontroller / Processor (MCU)** (10–20%) — ARM Cortex-M, ESP32, etc.
3. **Sensors & Actuators** (5–15%) — temperature, humidity, accelerometers.
4. **Power Management Circuitry** (1–5%) — losses in regulators and converters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the radio dominate?** Transmitting a single bit over the air can consume more energy than executing thousands of CPU instructions — this is the famous *"Transmit Energy vs. Compute Energy"* trade-off that drives IoT protocol design.

## 2.2 Energy Sources for IoT Devices

### A. Conventional (Stored) Energy
- **Primary Batteries:** Non-rechargeable (e.g., alkaline, Li-thionyl chloride). High energy density but finite.
- **Secondary Batteries:** Rechargeable (Li-ion, Li-Po, NiMH). Used with energy harvesting.
- **Supercapacitors:** Very high power density, low energy density, millions of charge cycles.

### B. Ambient (Harvested) Energy
| Harvesting Source | Conversion Principle | Typical Power Density |
|---|---|---|
| **Solar / Photovoltaic** | Photovoltaic effect | $10$–$100\,\text{mW/cm}^2$ (outdoor) |
| **Thermal / Thermoelectric** | Seebeck effect | $10$–$100\,\mu\text{W/cm}^2$ |
| **Vibration / Piezoelectric** | Mechanical stress → voltage | $4$–$200\,\mu\text{W/cm}^3$ |
| **RF / Electromagnetic** | Rectifying ambient RF | $0.1$–$1\,\mu\text{W/cm}^2$ |
| **Wind / Flow** | Micro-turbine | $1$–$10\,\text{mW/cm}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seebeck Effect:** When two dissimilar conductors are joined and their junctions are held at different temperatures, an EMF is generated. This is the principle behind **Thermoelectric Generators (TEGs)**, used in body-heat-powered wearables.

## 2.3 Energy Management Strategies

1. **Duty Cycling:** Run the node only $x\%$ of the time.
   $$P_{\text{avg}} = x \cdot P_{\text{active}} + (1-x) \cdot P_{\text{sleep}}$$
2. **Data Reduction (In-Network Processing):** Aggregate, compress, or filter data at the edge before transmission.
3. **Energy-Efficient Routing:** Multi-hop routing where nodes closer to the gateway transmit with less power.
4. **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS):** Lower the clock frequency of the MCU when full speed is not required (since $P \propto V^2 f$).
5. **Radio Optimization:** Use low-power protocols (BLE, LoRa, Zigbee), adaptive transmit power, and sleep modes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy = Power × Time | J (Joules) |
| 2 | $Q = I \times t$ | Charge / Battery capacity | C or mAh |
| 3 | $E_{\text{batt}} = V_{\text{nom}} \times Q$ | Energy stored in a battery | Wh |
| 4 | $L = \dfrac{E_{\text{batt}}}{P_{\text{avg}}}$ | Node operational lifetime | seconds/hours/years |
| 5 | $P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sum P_i t_i}{\sum t_i}$ | Time-weighted average power | W |
| 6 | $P_{\text{CMOS}} = \alpha C V_{\text{dd}}^2 f$ | Dynamic power in CMOS circuits | W |
| 7 | $E_{\text{TX}}(d) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{\text{amp}} \cdot k \cdot d^n$ | Energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$ | J |
| 8 | $P_{\text{harvested}} = \eta \cdot P_{\text{ambient}}$ | Usable power after conversion efficiency $\eta$ | W |
| 9 | $V_{\text{out,TEG}} = S \cdot \Delta T$ | Seebeck EMF ($S$ = Seebeck coefficient) | V |
| 10 | $E_{\text{cycle}} = P_{\text{active}} \cdot t_{\text{on}}$ | Energy used in one active cycle | J |

> **Memory Trick for the Board:** "*Energy is what you store, Power is the rate at which you spend it.*"

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensors powered by small solar panels + supercapacitor buffers in paddy fields of Kuttanad.
- **Structural Health Monitoring:** Piezoelectric harvesters fitted on the cables of the **Wellington Bridge, Cochin** to sense vibration and self-report damage.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** TEG-based fitness bands powered by human body heat ($\approx 37^\circ\text{C}$ skin vs. $25^\circ\text{C}$ ambient).
- **Smart Logistics:** BLE asset trackers on shipping containers, powered by harvested RF from port-side Wi-Fi hotspots.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Node Lifetime from Battery Capacity

**Problem Setup:** An IoT sensor node operates in two states:
- *Active:* current $I_{\text{on}} = 15\,\text{mA}$, duration $t_{\text{on}} = 100\,\text{ms}$.
- *Sleep:* current $I_{\text{sleep}} = 5\,\mu\text{A}$, duration $t_{\text{sleep}} = 900\,\text{ms}$.

Total cycle period: $T = t_{\text{on}} + t_{\text{sleep}} = 100\,\text{ms} + 900\,\text{ms} = 1000\,\text{ms} = 1\,\text{s}$.

Battery: $V_{\text{nom}} = 3\,\text{V}$, capacity $Q = 2400\,\text{mAh}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the average current drawn per cycle.**
$$I_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{on}} \cdot t_{\text{on}} + I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot t_{\text{sleep}}}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**
$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg}} &= \dfrac{(15 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A})(100 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}) + (5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{A})(900 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s})}{1\,\text{s}} \\
&= \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} + 4.5 \times 10^{-6}}{1} \\
&= 1.5045 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A} \\
&\approx 1.5045\,\text{mA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the operational lifetime in hours.**
$$L_{\text{hours}} = \dfrac{Q}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \dfrac{2400\,\text{mAh}}{1.5045\,\text{mA}} \approx 1595.2\,\text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to days and years.**
$$L_{\text{days}} = \dfrac{1595.2}{24} \approx 66.47\,\text{days}, \quad L_{\text{years}} \approx 0.182\,\text{years}$$

**Step 5 — Key insight.**
If we increased the duty cycle by reducing $t_{\text{on}}$ to $50\,\text{ms}$:
$$I_{\text{avg}}' = \dfrac{15 \times 50 + 0.005 \times 950}{1000} = 0.7547\,\text{mA}$$
Lifetime **more than doubles** — proving the leverage of duty cycling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman Model)

This is the standard model used in LEACH, PEGASIS, and similar WSN/IoT routing protocols.

**Step 1 — Energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$:**
$$E_{\text{TX}}(k,d) = \begin{cases} k \cdot E_{\text{elec}} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot d^2, & d < d_0 \quad \text{(free-space)} \\ k \cdot E_{\text{elec}} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot d^4, & d \geq d_0 \quad \text{(multipath)} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Energy to receive $k$ bits:**
$$E_{\text{RX}}(k) = k \cdot E_{\text{elec}}$$

**Step 3 — Threshold distance $d_0$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot d_0^{\,2} &= \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot d_0^{\,4} \\
\Rightarrow d_0 &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{\text{fs}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{mp}}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**
Take $E_{\text{elec}} = 50\,\text{nJ/bit}$, $\varepsilon_{\text{fs}} = 10\,\text{pJ/bit/m}^2$, $\varepsilon_{\text{mp}} = 0.0013\,\text{pJ/bit/m}^4$, $k = 2000\,\text{bits}$, $d = 80\,\text{m}$.

Since $d_0 = \sqrt{10 \times 10^{-12} / 0.0013 \times 10^{-12}} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7\,\text{m}$, we are in the free-space regime.
$$E_{\text{TX}} = 2000 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 2000 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 80^2$$
$$\begin{aligned}
&= 100\,\mu\text{J} + 2000 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 6400 \\
&= 100\,\mu\text{J} + 0.128\,\text{mJ} \\
&= 100\,\mu\text{J} + 128\,\mu\text{J} = 228\,\mu\text{J}
\end{aligned}$$
$$E_{\text{RX}} = 2000 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 100\,\mu\text{J}$$

Total link energy = $328\,\mu\text{J}$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Energy Sources

| Parameter | Primary Battery (Li) | Rechargeable (Li-ion) | Supercapacitor | Solar Harvester | RF Harvester |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Density** | Very High | High | Low | Source-dependent | Very Low |
| **Power Density** | Moderate | Moderate | Very High | High (daylight) | Very Low |
| **Recharge Cycles** | 1 (single use) | $500$–$2000$ | $>10^6$ | Indefinite | Indefinite |
| **Self-Discharge** | Very Low | Moderate | High | None | None |
| **Predictability** | Excellent | Good | Moderate | Weather-dependent | Environment-dependent |
| **Form Factor** | Bulky | Bulky | Compact | Needs surface area | Compact antenna |
| **Best Use Case** | Long-term remote | Mobile + charging | Pulse loads | Outdoor fixed | Urban indoor |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Energy Subsystem of an IoT Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC1[Ambient Energy Source] --> HARV[Energy Harvester]
    BAT[Battery / Supercapacitor] --> HARV
    HARV --> PMU[Power Management Unit]
    PMU -->|3.3V Rail| MCU[Microcontroller MCU]
    PMU -->|3.3V Rail| SENS[Sensor Array]
    PMU -->|3.3V Rail| RADIO[Radio Transceiver]
    PMU -->|5V Rail| ACT[Actuator Bank]
    MCU --> CTRL[Control Signals]
    CTRL --> PMU
    MCU <--> SENS
    MCU <--> RADIO
    RADIO -. Wireless Link .-> GW[Gateway / Cloud]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Harvested ambient energy and stored battery energy are *conditioned* by the PMU into stable DC rails that feed the digital and analog loads. The MCU can send *control signals* back to the PMU to enable/disable specific rails, which is the hardware basis of duty cycling.

## 4.2 Energy Harvesting System — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AMB[Ambient Source: Sun, Heat, Vibration, RF] --> TRANS[Transducer PV, TEG, Piezo, Antenna]
    TRANS --> RECT[Rectifier and Conditioning Circuit]
    RECT --> REG[Voltage Regulator and MPPT]
    REG --> STOR[Storage Element Battery or Supercapacitor]
    STOR --> LOAD[IoT Load MCU, Sensor, Radio]
    LOAD --> MON[Energy Monitor ADC]
    MON --> CTRL[Controller Firmware]
    CTRL -->|Adjust sleep cycles| LOAD
    CTRL -->|Update transmission interval| RADIO_CTRL[Radio Duty Control]
```

## 4.3 Duty Cycling State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DeepSleep
    DeepSleep --> WakeUp : Timer IRQ
    WakeUp --> Sense : Read sensors
    Sense --> Process : Run algorithm
    Process --> Transmit : Send data packet
    Transmit --> DeepSleep : Ack received
    Process --> DeepSleep : No event detected
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** When asked to *draw and explain energy management in IoT*, present the state machine above and label each state's typical current draw (e.g., *Deep Sleep: 1 μA, Sense: 5 mA, Transmit: 80 mA*). This is a high-scoring visual answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define *Energy Harvesting* in the context of IoT. List four ambient sources from which energy can be harvested.**

**Model Answer:**
Energy harvesting is the process by which an IoT device captures and converts small amounts of ambient energy from its surroundings into usable electrical energy, enabling self-sustained, battery-less or battery-augmented operation.

Four ambient sources:
1. **Solar / Photovoltaic** (light energy)
2. **Thermal / Thermoelectric** (Seebeck effect)
3. **Vibration / Piezoelectric** (mechanical strain)
4. **RF / Electromagnetic** (ambient radio waves)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing only "solar" alone. The examiner expects *at least four* distinct sources for full marks. Listing only 1–2 will attract a partial deduction.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**What is *Duty Cycling*? Why is it the most widely used energy-saving technique in IoT?**

**Model Answer:**
Duty cycling is a power-management strategy in which an IoT device alternates between an *active* (high-power) state and a *sleep* (low-power) state such that the active period occupies only a small fraction ($x\%$, the duty cycle) of the total operational time.

It is the most widely used technique because:
1. The **average power** scales linearly with the duty cycle, often yielding **2–3 orders of magnitude** reduction.
2. It requires **no specialized hardware** — it is implemented in firmware.
3. It is **compatible with all subsystems** (MCU, radio, sensors).
4. It provides a tunable trade-off between **latency** and **energy savings**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[MODULE 1 — INTERNAL CHOICE — FULL 14 MARK PACKAGE]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an IoT energy harvesting system with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IoT node uses a 3 V, 2400 mAh Li-ion battery. Its active current is 20 mA for 50 ms and sleep current is 8 μA for 950 ms in every cycle. Calculate:
   (i) Average current
   (ii) Battery lifetime in days
   (iii) Total energy consumed in 30 days. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The IoT energy harvesting system consists of five functional blocks:

1. **Energy Source / Transducer:** Captures ambient energy. Examples — solar cell (light), TEG (heat), piezoelectric cantilever (vibration), antenna (RF).
2. **Rectification & Conditioning:** Converts AC (from RF, vibration) to DC; smooths ripple using diodes, filters.
3. **Voltage Regulation / MPPT:** Maintains a constant DC output despite input variations. *Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)* is used in solar harvesters.
4. **Energy Storage:** Battery or supercapacitor buffers energy for periods when harvesting is insufficient (nighttime, cloudy days).
5. **Load / Power Management Unit:** Distributes regulated power to the MCU, sensor, and radio. Implements *sleep* and *wake-up* sequencing.

(Include the **mermaid block diagram from Section 4.1** here for the 2-mark diagram allocation.)

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Average current per cycle:**

$$T = 50\,\text{ms} + 950\,\text{ms} = 1000\,\text{ms} = 1\,\text{s}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg}} &= \dfrac{I_{\text{on}} t_{\text{on}} + I_{\text{sleep}} t_{\text{sleep}}}{T} \\
&= \dfrac{(20 \times 10^{-3})(50 \times 10^{-3}) + (8 \times 10^{-6})(950 \times 10^{-3})}{1} \\
&= \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-3} + 7.6 \times 10^{-6}}{1} \\
&= 1.0076 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A} \\
&\approx 1.008\,\text{mA}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Battery lifetime in days:**

$$L_{\text{h}} = \dfrac{Q}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \dfrac{2400\,\text{mAh}}{1.008\,\text{mA}} \approx 2381\,\text{h}$$

$$L_{\text{days}} = \dfrac{2381}{24} \approx 99.2\,\text{days}$$

**(iii) Energy consumed in 30 days:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg,30}} &= 1.008\,\text{mA} \times 24 \times 30 = 725.76\,\text{mAh} \\
E &= V \times Q = 3\,\text{V} \times 725.76 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Ah} \\
  &= 2.177\,\text{Wh} = 2.177 \times 3600\,\text{J} \\
  &\approx 7838\,\text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare primary batteries, rechargeable batteries, supercapacitors, and energy harvesters as energy sources for IoT. Discuss the suitability of each for (i) a remote forest fire sensor, (ii) a wearable fitness band. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the first-order radio energy model, derive the threshold distance $d_0$ and compute the transmission energy for 4000 bits over a distance of 50 m, given $E_{\text{elec}} = 50\,\text{nJ/bit}$, $\varepsilon_{\text{fs}} = 10\,\text{pJ/bit/m}^2$, $\varepsilon_{\text{mp}} = 0.0013\,\text{pJ/bit/m}^4$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Source | Reusability | Maintenance | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Battery | No (single use) | Replacement every 1–5 years | Long-term, inaccessible, low-power sites |
| Rechargeable Battery | Yes (500–2000 cycles) | Periodic charging | Mobile, wearable, semi-accessible nodes |
| Supercapacitor | Yes (>10⁶ cycles) | Very low | Pulse loads, fast charge/discharge needs |
| Energy Harvester | Indefinite | None | Self-sustaining, green deployments |

**(i) Forest Fire Sensor:**
A remote forest sensor must operate unattended for years. **Primary battery** (e.g., Li-SOCl₂) is most suitable — long shelf life, high energy density, no maintenance. Energy harvesting (solar) is unreliable in dense canopy.

**(ii) Wearable Fitness Band:**
A band is recharged nightly by the user. **Rechargeable Li-ion or Li-Po** is ideal — moderate size, good energy density, supports continuous BLE/heart-rate monitoring.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation of $d_0$:**

At the threshold, the two energy models give equal power loss per bit:
$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot d_0^{\,2} &= \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot d_0^{\,4} \\
\Rightarrow d_0 &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{\text{fs}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{mp}}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} \\
&= \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $d = 50\,\text{m} < d_0 = 87.7\,\text{m}$, the **free-space** model applies.

**Transmission energy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{TX}} &= k \cdot E_{\text{elec}} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot d^2 \\
&= 4000 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 4000 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 50^2 \\
&= 200 \times 10^{-6} + 4000 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 2500 \\
&= 200\,\mu\text{J} + 100\,\mu\text{J} \\
&= 300\,\mu\text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — $E_{\text{elec}}$ is in nJ, $\varepsilon_{\text{fs}}$ is in pJ. Convert *both* to the same unit (Joules) before computing, or you will lose **2 marks** silently.
> 2. **Forgetting to square $d$** in the free-space model is a recurring 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Not stating the regime** (free-space vs multipath) is also penalized — always mention which model you selected and *why*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this on your wall the night before the exam):**
>
> ✅ **Energy vs Power:** Energy is *capacity to do work* (J, Wh); Power is *rate* (W).
> ✅ **CMOS Power:** $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ — voltage has the **largest** leverage (squared).
> ✅ **Duty Cycling Equation:** $P_{\text{avg}} = x P_{\text{on}} + (1-x) P_{\text{sleep}}$.
> ✅ **Lifetime Equation:** $L = E_{\text{battery}} / P_{\text{avg}}$.
> ✅ **Radio Energy:** Free-space ($d^2$) vs multipath ($d^4$); $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{fs}} / \varepsilon_{\text{mp}}}$.
> ✅ **Energy Sources in IoT:** Primary cell, rechargeable cell, supercapacitor, harvested (solar, TEG, piezo, RF).
> ✅ **Seebeck Effect** → TEG; **Photovoltaic Effect** → Solar cell; **Piezoelectric Effect** → Vibration harvester.
> ✅ **Duty Cycling** is the **single most effective** software-level energy technique.
> ✅ **Radio consumes 60–80%** of node energy — transmit less, transmit shorter.
> ✅ **DVFS** reduces $P$ dramatically by lowering $V$ and $f$.
> ✅ **Energy Neutral Operation (ENO):** $E_{\text{harvested}} \geq E_{\text{consumed}}$ over a duty cycle.
> ✅ **Supercapacitors** complement batteries for pulse loads and fast charge cycles.
> ✅ **MPPT** maximizes power extraction from variable sources like solar.
> ✅ **Always carry units** in numerical answers; examiners reward explicit unit tracking.
> ✅ **For 14-mark numericals:** state formula → substitute values → compute → give answer in requested units. **No skipped steps.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agriculture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Agriculture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Smart Agriculture (also called Precision Agriculture or Smart Farming)** is the application of **Internet of Things (IoT)** technologies, embedded sensors, wireless communication networks, cloud/edge computing, and data analytics to monitor, control, and optimize agricultural production processes — including crop cultivation, livestock management, irrigation, and post-harvest handling — with the goal of improving yield, reducing resource wastage, and enabling data-driven decision making.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCAMT501 – Introduction to IoT)**, the agricultural domain is treated as a canonical **use-case / application vertical** of IoT, illustrating how the generic IoT reference model (Perception → Network → Middleware → Application → Business layers) is instantiated for a real-world problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCAMT501 – Module 1):**
> IoT in Agriculture is studied as an *application domain* demonstrating how physical sensors (soil, climate, crop, livestock) are interfaced to the internet through resource-constrained wireless networks (typically **LoRaWAN, ZigBee, NB-IoT, or Wi-Fi**) to enable autonomous decision-making.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Think of a traditional farm as a patient who can only tell the doctor (farmer) that "something hurts" — by the time the farmer walks the field, the damage is already done.

**IoT-based Smart Agriculture** is like giving every plant and every animal a tiny wearable health monitor (a sensor node) that continuously reports its heartbeat (moisture), temperature, pH, and activity level to a central cloud doctor (the IoT platform). The doctor compares the readings against historical baselines and, when something is abnormal, automatically dispenses water, fertilizer, or alerts the farmer — often before the plant even shows visible stress.

In one sentence:
> *Smart Agriculture = Sensors + Connectivity + Intelligence + Actuators, all working 24×7 across the field.*

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics (Used Throughout the Module)

The following quantitative terms are standardized across the IoT-in-Agriculture literature and the **KTU 2024 Scheme marking scheme**:

- **Soil Moisture Content (θ)** — Volumetric Water Content, expressed as a **percentage (%)** in the range **0% – 100%**.
- **Field Capacity (θ_FC)** ≈ **30%** for loamy soils (a benchmark figure).
- **Wilting Point (θ_WP)** ≈ **10%** for loamy soils.
- **Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR)** — measured in **μmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹**.
- **Reference Evapotranspiration (ET₀)** — measured in **mm/day**.
- **Typical LoRaWAN Spreading Factor (SF)** — **SF7 to SF12**.
- **Typical Soil-Moisture Sensor Accuracy** — **±2% to ±3% VWC**.
- **Node Battery Target Lifetime** — **2 to 5 years** (on 2× AA / 1× Li-SOCl₂ cell).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in the exam:** KTU examiners frequently award marks for quoting realistic, industry-standard values (e.g., "SF = 7 to 12", "duty cycle ≤ 1%"). Memorize the bold constants above.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Smart Agriculture

| Pillar | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing** | Measuring the physical world | Soil-moisture, NPK, DHT22, LDR, pH probes |
| **Communication** | Transporting data to the cloud | LoRaWAN gateways, NB-IoT modems, ZigBee mesh |
| **Actuation / Intelligence** | Closing the loop | Solenoid valves, motor pumps, ML-based irrigation decisions |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 5-Layer IoT Reference Model — Applied to Agriculture

The generic IoT architecture is mapped onto an agricultural deployment as follows:

1. **Perception / Sensor Layer (Layer 1)** — The "eyes and ears" of the field.
   - *Soil sensors:* moisture, temperature, pH, NPK (Nitrogen-Phosphorus-Potassium), EC (Electrical Conductivity).
   - *Atmospheric sensors:* air temperature, relative humidity, rainfall, wind speed, PAR/light intensity, CO₂.
   - *Imaging sensors:* RGB cameras, multispectral / hyperspectral cameras, drones.
   - *Livestock sensors:* RFID ear-tags, accelerometers (rumination/activity), GPS collars, body-temperature boluses.
2. **Network / Transmission Layer (Layer 2)** — Carries the data to a sink.
   - Short-range: **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)**, **BLE**, **Wi-Fi (802.11)**.
   - Long-range / LPWAN: **LoRaWAN**, **NB-IoT**, **Sigfox**, **LTE-M**.
3. **Middleware / Edge Layer (Layer 3)** — Pre-processing, filtering, time-stamping, security.
   - Edge gateways (e.g., Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson Nano) running **MQTT brokers**, **Apache Kafka**, or **AWS Greengrass**.
4. **Application Layer (Layer 4)** — End-user dashboards and decision logic.
   - Mobile apps, web dashboards, SMS/IVR alerts, decision-support systems (DSS).
5. **Business Layer (Layer 5)** — Analytics, prediction, billing, supply-chain integration.
   - Crop-yield prediction models, market-price advisory, insurance integration.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of a Smart-Irrigation Use-Case (Step-by-Step)

Below is the **canonical closed-loop irrigation workflow** that KTU examiners love to draw as a block diagram:

- **Step 1:** Soil-moisture sensor samples θ at a fixed rate (e.g., every **15 min**).
- **Step 2:** Microcontroller (MCU — e.g., ESP32, Arduino Uno, STM32) reads the analogue/digital value.
- **Step 3:** MCU compares θ against the **lower threshold (θ_min ≈ 20%)** and **upper threshold (θ_max ≈ 35%)**.
- **Step 4:** If **θ < θ_min**, MCU energizes a relay → solenoid valve → water pump turns **ON**.
- **Step 5:** If **θ > θ_max**, the valve is **closed** (preventing over-irrigation and root rot).
- **Step 6:** Data packet (sensor ID, θ, timestamp, battery voltage) is uplinked via LoRaWAN/NB-IoT to the gateway → cloud → dashboard.
- **Step 7:** Cloud platform updates the historical database and triggers any SMS/app notification.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are in the KTU 2024 Scheme high-yield list for Module-1 application verticals.** Learn the meaning of each symbol, not just the formula.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_v = \dfrac{V_{water}}{V_{soil}} \times 100\%$ | Volumetric water content | % | Soil-moisture sensor calibration |
| 2 | $ET_c = K_c \times ET_0$ | Crop evapotranspiration | mm/day | Irrigation scheduling |
| 3 | $P_{tx} \;[\text{dBm}] = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{tx}\;[W]}{1\text{ mW}}\right)$ | Tx power conversion | dBm | RF link budget |
| 4 | $L_{fs}(d) = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (dB) | Free-space path loss (d in km, f in MHz) | dB | LoRa/NB-IoT link budget |
| 5 | $L_{total} = L_{fs} + L_{veg} + L_{atm} + L_{misc}$ | Total path loss | dB | Rural field coverage |
| 6 | $E_b/N_0 = \text{SNR} - 10\log_{10}(R/B)$ | Energy per bit over noise spectral density | dB | Modulation analysis |
| 7 | $T_{lifetime} \;[\text{years}] = \dfrac{C_{battery}\;[mAh] \times V}{I_{avg}\;[mA] \times 8760}$ | Battery lifetime | years | Sensor-node design |
| 8 | $D_{duty} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{sleep}} \times 100\%$ | Duty cycle | % | Energy optimization |
| 9 | $SF, \; R_b = \dfrac{SF \times BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR$ | LoRa bit-rate approximation | bit/s | LoRaWAN payload sizing |
| 10 | $A_{coverage} = \pi r^2 \times N_{gateways}$ | Approx. coverage area | m² or km² | Network planning |
| 11 | $W_{water} \;[\text{L}] = A_{field} \times (θ_{FC} - θ_{current}) \times d_{root} \times 10$ | Litres of water needed | L | Smart-irrigation logic |
| 12 | $R_{yield} = \dfrac{Y_{treated} - Y_{control}}{Y_{control}} \times 100\%$ | % yield improvement | % | Justification of IoT farming |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all LaTeX expressions above, the absolute-value bars and "divides" symbols have been written as `\vert` (or omitted) so that the markdown table layout is **never broken**. The vertical pipe `\|` would have terminated the column prematurely.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water conservation:** Smart irrigation has been shown (FAO studies) to reduce water use by **30% – 50%** compared to flood irrigation.
- **Yield improvement:** Precision fertilization and pest-detection can raise yield by **15% – 25%**.
- **Labour cost reduction:** Autonomous monitoring cuts manual scouting by up to **80%**.
- **Carbon-footprint tracking:** IoT-enabled methane/CO₂ sensors help in **carbon-credit** accounting for agri-corporates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are written for **maximum exam marks**: every algebraic transition is expanded and every constant is named, exactly as a KTU 2024 Scheme model-answer script expects.

### 3.1 Derivation-1: Free-Space Path Loss for an Agricultural LoRa Link

**Problem statement (typical KTU sub-question):** *A LoRaWAN gateway is placed at the edge of a 2 km² farm. The transmission frequency is 868 MHz (the Indian/European ISM band). Calculate the free-space path loss at a distance of 1.5 km from the gateway.*

**Starting point — Friis free-space transmission equation:**

$$
\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t \, G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^{\!2}
$$

**Step 1 — Take 10·log₁₀ of both sides (convert to dB):**

$$
P_r\;[\text{dBm}] = P_t\;[\text{dBm}] + G_t\;[\text{dBi}] + G_r\;[\text{dBi}] - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — The bracket term on the right is the path loss L_fs:**

$$
L_{fs}\;[\text{dB}] = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\lambda = c/f$:**

$$
L_{fs} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d \, f}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Use the standard logarithmic identity $20\log(ab) = 20\log a + 20\log b$ and plug in $4\pi/c$ (which evaluates to a constant) to obtain the KTU-friendly form:**

$$
L_{fs}\;[\text{dB}] = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44
$$

where **$d$ is in km, $f$ is in MHz**, and **32.44 = 20·log₁₀(4π·10³/3×10⁸)** when those units are used.

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:**

- $d = 1.5$ km → $20\log_{10}(1.5) = 20 \times 0.1761 = 3.52$ dB
- $f = 868$ MHz → $20\log_{10}(868) = 20 \times 2.9385 = 58.77$ dB

$$
L_{fs} = 3.52 + 58.77 + 32.44 = 94.73\;\text{dB}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:**

$$
\boxed{L_{fs} \approx 94.7\;\text{dB at 1.5 km, 868 MHz}}
$$

A typical LoRa SX1276 transceiver with $P_t = +14$ dBm and $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi will still receive the packet with a healthy link margin of $>15$ dB, confirming that **LoRaWAN is suitable for 1.5 km agricultural fields**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation-2: Battery Lifetime of a Soil-Moisture Sensor Node

**Problem statement:** *A soil-moisture node transmits a 20-byte LoRaWAN packet every 15 minutes. The transmit current is 120 mA (at 3.3 V) for 1.2 s; the sleep current is 8 μA. The node is powered by a 2.4 Ah (2400 mAh) Li-SOCl₂ primary cell. Compute the lifetime in years.*

**Step 1 — Charge consumed per transmission:**

$$
Q_{tx} = 120\;\text{mA} \times 1.2\;\text{s} = 144\;\text{mC} = \frac{144}{3600}\;\text{mAh} = 0.04\;\text{mAh}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of transmissions per day:**

$$
N = \frac{24 \times 60}{15} = 96\;\text{transmissions/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily charge for transmissions:**

$$
Q_{day,tx} = 96 \times 0.04 = 3.84\;\text{mAh/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily charge for sleep mode:**

$$
Q_{day,sleep} = 8\;\mu\text{A} \times 24\;\text{h} = 0.192\;\text{mAh/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Total daily consumption:**

$$
Q_{day} = 3.84 + 0.192 \approx 4.03\;\text{mAh/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Lifetime:**

$$
T_{life} = \frac{2400\;\text{mAh}}{4.03\;\text{mAh/day}} = 595.5\;\text{days} \approx 1.63\;\text{years}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation:** To reach the KTU-quoted target of **2–5 years**, the node must (i) lower SF, (ii) increase sleep time, or (iii) use a **solar-energy-harvesting** front-end.

---

### 3.3 Derivation-3: Smart-Irrigation Water-Volume Calculation

**Problem statement:** *A 1-hectare (10 000 m²) field has a root-zone depth of 30 cm. Current soil moisture is 15% VWC; field capacity is 30% VWC. How many litres of water must be supplied to bring the root zone to field capacity?*

**Step 1 — Volume of root zone per m²:**

$$
V_{rz} = 1\;\text{m}^2 \times 0.30\;\text{m} = 0.30\;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Moisture deficit (per m², per m³ of soil):**

$$
\Delta \theta = \theta_{FC} - \theta_{current} = 30\% - 15\% = 15\% = 0.15
$$

**Step 3 — Water volume needed per m²:**

$$
V_w = V_{rz} \times \Delta\theta = 0.30 \times 0.15 = 0.045\;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Scale to 1 hectare (10 000 m²):**

$$
V_{total} = 0.045 \times 10\,000 = 450\;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to litres (1 m³ = 1000 L):**

$$
\boxed{V_{total} = 450\,000\;\text{L} = 4.5 \times 10^{5}\;\text{L}}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering check:** With a 10 L/min drip-emitter system, the run-time would be **45 000 min ≈ 31 days of continuous operation** — clearly justifying a sensor-driven, on-demand irrigation controller.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Communication Technologies for Agriculture

| Parameter | **ZigBee** | **LoRaWAN** | **NB-IoT** | **Wi-Fi (HaLow)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standard | IEEE 802.15.4 | LoRa Alliance | 3GPP Release 13 | IEEE 802.11ah |
| Range (open field) | 30 – 100 m | 2 – 10 km | 1 – 10 km | 1 – 2 km |
| Data rate | 250 kbps | 0.3 – 50 kbps | 26 kbps (DL) / 62 kbps (UL) | up to 4 Mbps |
| Power | Low | Very low | Medium (PSM/eDRX) | Medium-high |
| Battery life | 1 – 2 yr | 5 – 10 yr | 5 – 10 yr | < 1 yr |
| Best use | Greenhouse | Open field crops | Rural telemetry | Video / drone |
| Cost / node | Lowest | Low | Medium (SIM) | High |

> **Exam Tip:** For an *open-field* deployment, **LoRaWAN is the KTU-recommended default**; for *high-bandwidth drone imagery*, Wi-Fi HaLow or 5G is preferred.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use **Mermaid** syntax with **purely-alphanumeric node IDs** and **no markdown formatting inside labels**, strictly per the V10 safeguards.

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture — IoT for Smart Agriculture (5-Layer View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1["PERCEPTION LAYER - Soil moisture, DHT22, NPK, pH, LDR, Camera, RFID, GPS collar"]
    layer2["NETWORK LAYER - ZigBee Mesh, LoRaWAN Gateway, NB-IoT Modem, Wi-Fi AP"]
    layer3["MIDDLEWARE LAYER - MQTT Broker, Edge Gateway, Time Series DB, Security"]
    layer4["APPLICATION LAYER - Mobile App, Web Dashboard, SMS Alert, DSS Engine"]
    layer5["BUSINESS LAYER - Yield Prediction, Market Advisory, Insurance, Carbon Credit"]

    layer1 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer5

    layer3 -. feedback actuation .- layer1
    layer4 -. feedback actuation .- layer1
```

### 4.2 Closed-Loop Smart Irrigation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Soil Moisture Sensor samples theta"]
    stepB["MCU reads and digitizes value"]
    stepC{"theta less than theta_min threshold"}
    stepD["Actuate Relay - Solenoid Valve - Pump ON"]
    stepE["Pump OFF - theta above theta_max threshold"]
    stepF["Packet Encoded - LoRaWAN Uplink"]
    stepG["Cloud Updates Dashboard and History"]
    stepH["Alert to Farmer if Anomaly"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC
    stepC -- YES --> stepD
    stepC -- NO --> stepE
    stepD --> stepF
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG --> stepH
```

### 4.3 Component-Level Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sensorBank["SENSOR BANK - Soil Moisture, DHT22, NPK, pH, LDR, Rain Gauge, Camera"]
    mcu["MICROCONTROLLER - ESP32 or STM32 - ADC, GPIO, I2C, SPI"]
    pwr["POWER SUBSYSTEM - LiSOCl2 Battery, Solar Panel, MPPT, LDO 3V3"]
    radio["RADIO MODULE - SX1276 LoRa or NB-IoT modem - Antenna"]
    gw["GATEWAY - Raspberry Pi - MQTT broker, TLS, Local cache"]
    cloud["CLOUD PLATFORM - AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, ThingsBoard"]
    dash["DASHBOARD - Mobile App, Web UI, Voice IVR, SMS"]
    actuator["ACTUATOR BANK - Relay, Solenoid Valve, Motor, Heater, Fan"]

    sensorBank --> mcu
    pwr --> mcu
    mcu --> radio
    radio --> gw
    gw --> cloud
    cloud --> dash
    cloud --> actuator
    actuator -. affects .- sensorBank
```

### 4.4 Use-Case Coverage Map of IoT in Agriculture

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Smart Agriculture IoT))
    Crop Monitoring
      Soil sensing
      Drone imaging
      Weather station
    Smart Irrigation

      Water budgeting
    Livestock Monitoring

      Geo fencing

      Lighting

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students **draw a sensor and write "IoT"** — that is *not* an IoT system. You must show **sensing + communication + intelligence + actuation** to earn full marks.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse ZigBee with Z-Wave**, and **do NOT confuse LoRa (the physical layer) with LoRaWAN (the MAC layer)**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 3. When asked to "design" a system, always include **power budget, link budget, and duty-cycle analysis** — without these, a Part-B answer is incomplete.
> 4. **State numerical assumptions** (frequency, distance, battery capacity). A solution without numbers is treated as vague and capped at 60% marks.

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define Smart Agriculture. List any four sensors commonly used in IoT-based agriculture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Smart Agriculture is the integration of IoT sensors, communication networks, and data analytics to monitor and control farming operations in real time with minimal human intervention, improving yield and resource-use efficiency. Four commonly used sensors are:

1. **Soil-moisture sensor** (e.g., capacitive or resistive type) — measures volumetric water content (θ).
2. **DHT22 / DHT11** — measures air temperature and relative humidity.
3. **NPK sensor** — measures soil Nitrogen, Phosphorus and Potassium ion concentration.
4. **LDR / PAR sensor** — measures incident light intensity for photosynthesis studies.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each × 4 = 1 mark for sensors = 3 marks. Add a labelled sketch for half-mark bonus.)*

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between LoRaWAN and NB-IoT for agricultural deployments (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses **unlicensed ISM bands** (868/915 MHz) — no SIM, no recurring fee. | Uses **licensed cellular spectrum** — requires SIM and operator subscription. |
| 2 | Very low data rate (**0.3 – 50 kbps**), suited for periodic soil/climate telemetry. | Higher data rate (**~26 kbps DL / 62 kbps UL**), can carry firmware updates. |
| 3 | Star-of-stars topology with **8-channel gateways**; long battery life (5–10 yr). | Star topology via eNodeB; PSM/eDRX power-saving modes for similar battery life. |

*(1 mark per valid, contrasting point. Tabular format preferred for clarity.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from the choice given. Each full question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 + (b) 7.*

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **5-layer IoT reference architecture** as applied to a **smart irrigation system** in a 2-hectare farm. Identify **at least one specific sensor and one communication technology per layer**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LoRaWAN end-device is placed **2.5 km** from a gateway. The system operates at **868 MHz** with $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi and a transmit power of **+14 dBm**. Calculate the **free-space path loss** and the **received signal strength (RSSI)**. State whether the link is viable assuming a sensitivity of **−137 dBm (SF12, BW=125 kHz)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Layer | Block in Diagram | Specific Component |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Perception | Soil-moisture probe, DHT22, rain-gauge, NPK sensor | Capacitive v1.2 soil sensor (0–100% VWC) |
| 2. Network | LoRaWAN SX1276 radio + 868 MHz antenna | Dragino LG01-N gateway |
| 3. Middleware | Raspberry Pi running **Mosquitto MQTT broker** + TLS | Local caching & JSON payload formatting |
| 4. Application | Blynk/ThingsBoard dashboard with crop-watering scheduler | Mobile app with push notification |
| 5. Business | Crop-water-saving report, insurance telemetry | AWS IoT Analytics for ET₀-based yield prediction |

*Block diagram (1 mark), each layer × correct component (1 mark each) = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for labelling the closed-loop arrow back to actuation. Total = 7.*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Free-space path loss formula:**

$$
L_{fs}\;[\text{dB}] = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44
$$
