4. **Interoperability** across heterogeneous vendors (Cisco, Huawei, Bosch, Siemens) because the MIB is standardized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** In **v1 / v2c**, "community strings" act as plaintext passwords. In **v3**, SNMP added **authentication (MD5/SHA)** and **privacy (DES/AES)** — making it suitable for **industrial IoT and critical infrastructure** where security is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 SNMP Architecture — The Four Logical Components

The SNMP framework is built on a **manager–agent model** and consists of four interacting entities:

### 🔹 1. Network Management Station (NMS) — *The Boss*
- A software application (e.g., **PRTG, Zabbix, Nagios, SolarWinds, OpenNMS**).
- Runs on a network administrator's workstation or server.
- Issues **GET / SET / GET-NEXT / GET-BULK** requests.
- Receives **TRAP / INFORM** messages.
- Hosts the user interface, dashboards, and analytics.

### 🔹 2. Managed Device — *The Worker*
- A **node** that implements an SNMP interface (e.g., router, switch, server, IoT gateway, smart meter).
- Identified uniquely by an **IP address**.
- Must run an **SNMP agent** software process.

### 🔹 3. SNMP Agent — *The Translator*
- A small software module running **inside the managed device**.
- Maintains a local copy of management variables defined in the **MIB**.
- Responds to manager requests and emits **unsolicited TRAPs** when anomalies occur.
- Listens on **UDP port 161**.

### 🔹 4. Management Information Base (MIB) — *The Dictionary*
- A **structured, hierarchical database** (essentially a virtual namespace) that defines every manageable object.
- Written in **ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation One)** syntax defined by **SMI (Structure of Management Information — RFC 2578)**.
- Objects are addressed using **Object Identifiers (OIDs)** — a dotted-decimal sequence like a phone number for variables.

> [!NOTE]
> **The OID Tree:** Every OID traces back to the root `{ iso(1) identified-organization(3) dod(6) internet(1) }`. The standard prefix for IETF-managed objects is **`1.3.6.1.2.1`**, often abbreviated as **`iso.org.dod.internet.mgmt.mib-2`**.

---

## 2.2 SNMP Protocol Operations (PDU Types)

SNMPv1 defined **five PDU types**. SNMPv2c added two more.

| PDU Type | Code | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GetRequest** | `0` | Manager → Agent | Retrieve the value of a specific OID |
| **GetNextRequest** | `1` | Manager → Agent | Retrieve the *next* OID in the MIB tree (used for table walking) |
| **Response** | `2` | Agent → Manager | Reply carrying requested values or error code |
| **SetRequest** | `3` | Manager → Agent | Modify / write a value on the device |
| **Trap** | `4` | Agent → Manager (async) | Asynchronous alert (link down, auth failure) |
| **GetBulkRequest** | `5` | SNMPv2c+ | Bulk retrieval — efficient for large tables |
| **InformRequest** | `6` | SNMPv2c+ | Acknowledged trap — manager must reply with Response |

---

## 2.3 SNMP Message / PDU Structure (Exhaustive)

A generic SNMP message follows the **BER (Basic Encoding Rules)** ASN.1 format:

```
+--------------------+--------------------+----------------------+
| Version (Integer)  | Community (OCT STR)| PDU (Sequence)       |
| 0, 1, or 3         | "public"/"private" | Type + ReqID + 0 +   |
|                    |                    | VarBindList           |
+--------------------+--------------------+----------------------+
```

- **Version:** `0` (SNMPv1), `1` (SNMPv2c), `3` (SNMPv3).
- **Community String:** Acts as a *cleartext* password. If the manager sends `public`, the agent allows **read-only** access. `private` enables **read-write**. A mismatch produces the **`noSuchName`** / **`authorizationError`** response.
- **PDU Fields:**
  - **Type** (1 byte): the operation code (e.g., `0xA0` for GetRequest, `0xA1` for GetNextRequest, `0xA3` for SetRequest, `0xA2` for Response, `0xA6` for Trap, `0xA5` for GetBulkRequest, `0xA8` for InformRequest).
  - **Request-ID** (Integer): matches a request to its response.
  - **Error-Status** (Integer): `0=noError`, `1=tooBig`, `2=noSuchName`, `3=badValue`, `4=readOnly`, `5=genErr`, etc.
  - **Error-Index** (Integer): points to the offending variable in VarBindList.
  - **VarBindList:** A sequence of `name–value` pairs (e.g., `[OID 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0 = "Cisco Router"]`).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following comparison table — it appears in almost every KTU Module 2 exam.**

### 🔸 Comparison of SNMP Versions

| Feature | **SNMPv1** | **SNMPv2c** | **SNMPv3** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RFC** | 1157, 1155 | 1901 – 1908 | 3411 – 3418 |
| **Security** | Community string (plaintext) | Community string (plaintext) | **USM (User-based Security Model)** with MD5/SHA + DES/AES |
| **Bulk Retrieval** | ❌ No | ✅ GetBulkRequest | ✅ GetBulkRequest |
| **Informs** | ❌ No | ✅ InformRequest (acknowledged) | ✅ InformRequest |
| **64-bit Counters** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Encryption** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (Privacy) |
| **Use in IoT** | Legacy | Common in LANs | **Industrial IoT / Critical infra** |

### 🔸 OID Reference Examples (Frequently Asked)

| OID | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0` | **sysDescr** — system description |
| `1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0` | **sysUpTime** — time since last boot (timeticks) |
| `1.3.6.1.2.1.1.5.0` | **sysName** — device hostname |
| `1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10` | **ifInOctets** — bytes received on interface |
| `1.3.6.1.2.1.25.1.1.0` | **hrSystemUptime** | 

### 🔸 Trap Destination Port Equation
$$
\text{Manager's listening port} = \mathbf{162}_{\text{UDP}}
$$
$$
\text{Agent's request listening port} = \mathbf{161}_{\text{UDP}}
$$

### 🔸 Maximum OID Naming Convention
$$
\text{OID} = \underbrace{1}_{\text{ISO}}.\underbrace{3}_{\text{Org}}.\underbrace{6}_{\text{DoD}}.\underbrace{1}_{\text{Internet}}.\underbrace{4}_{\text{Private}}.\underbrace{1}_{\text{Enterprise}}.
$$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Telecom Networks (ISPs):** Monitor backbone routers (Cisco, Juniper) for BGP session uptime, interface errors.
- **Smart Grids:** Poll smart meters every 15 minutes for kWh consumption via SNMP.
- **Data Centers:** Track server rack temperature, PSU status, fan RPM using SNMP traps.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** SCADA systems use **SNMPv3** over encrypted channels for PLC monitoring.
- **Enterprise Wi-Fi:** Wireless controllers poll APs for client counts, channel utilization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation in IoT:** For **battery-constrained, lossy networks (LLNs)** like 6LoWPAN or NB-IoT, full SNMP is often too heavy. Lighter alternatives include **CoAP, LWM2M (Lightweight M2M), and MQTT-SN**. However, SNMP remains the **de-facto standard for IP-based IoT gateways and edge routers**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: A Complete SNMP GetRequest → Response Exchange

Let us derive **exactly what happens** when an NMS operator queries the `sysUpTime` (OID `1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0`) of a managed router.

### Step 1 — Manager Constructs the GetRequest PDU

$$
\text{PDU}_{\text{Type}} = 0\text{x}A0 \quad (\text{GetRequest tag in BER})
$$

$$
\text{Request-ID} = 12345 \quad (\text{matches manager's transaction table})
$$

$$
\text{Error-Status} = 0 \quad (\text{always 0 on request, ignored by agent})
$$

$$
\text{Error-Index} = 0 \quad (\text{always 0 on request})
$$

### Step 2 — VarBindList Construction

The manager wraps the OID in a VarBind:

$$
\text{VarBindList} = \{ \text{OID}: 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0,\; \text{Value}: \text{NULL} \}
$$

The `NULL` placeholder is required by BER even though the manager doesn't know the value yet.

### Step 3 — Full Message Encapsulation

$$
\text{SNMP Message} = \begin{cases} \text{Version} = 0 \; (\text{SNMPv1}) \\ \text{Community} = \text{"public"} \\ \text{PDU} = \langle 0\text{x}A0,\; 12345,\; 0,\; 0,\; \text{VarBindList} \rangle \end{cases}
$$

### Step 4 — UDP Encapsulation & Transmission

$$
\text{IP Header: Src} = 192.168.1.10, \; \text{Dst} = 192.168.1.1
$$
$$
\text{UDP Header: Src Port} = 49152, \; \text{Dst Port} = \mathbf{161}
$$

The packet is sent **without handshake** (UDP = connectionless).

### Step 5 — Agent Receives & Validates

The agent:
1. **Decodes** BER → recovers OID.
2. **Walks** its local MIB tree → finds `sysUpTime` (a `TimeTicks` value, e.g., `345,678,901` timeticks).
3. **Checks** the community string: `"public"` allows **read** → access **granted**.

### Step 6 — Agent Constructs Response PDU

$$
\text{PDU}_{\text{Type}} = 0\text{x}A2 \quad (\text{Response})
$$

$$
\text{Request-ID} = 12345 \quad (\text{echoes manager's ID for correlation})
$$

$$
\text{Error-Status} = 0 \quad (\text{noError})
$$

$$
\text{Error-Index} = 0
$$

$$
\text{VarBindList} = \{ \text{OID}: 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0,\; \text{Value}: 345678901 \}
$$

### Step 7 — Conversion Logic (timeticks to human-readable)

$$
\text{Time in seconds} = \frac{\text{Timeticks}}{100}
$$

$$
T_{\text{seconds}} = \frac{345678901}{100} = 3456789.01 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{days}} = \frac{3456789.01}{86400} \approx 40.01 \text{ days}
$$

**Interpretation:** The router has been up for **40 days, 0 hours, 13 minutes, 9 seconds** since last reboot.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the OID Walk Mechanism (Table Traversal)

SNMP **cannot return a full table** in one packet. Instead, the manager uses a sequence of `GetNextRequest` calls — this is called **MIB walking**.

$$
\text{Manager sends: GetNextRequest}(1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10)
$$

$$
\text{Agent returns: } (1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10.1, \text{ifInOctets for interface 1})
$$

The agent returns the **lexicographically NEXT** OID in the MIB tree.

$$
\text{Manager sends: GetNextRequest}(1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10.1)
$$

$$
\text{Agent returns: } (1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10.2, \text{ifInOctets for interface 2})
$$

The walk **terminates** when the agent returns an OID **outside the requested subtree** (`endOfMibView` / `noSuchObject`).

---

## 3.3 SNR for Trap Polling vs. Polling Interval (Analytical Model)

Let us derive the **average manager load** $\lambda$ in PDUs per second:

$$
\lambda = \frac{N_{\text{devices}} \times N_{\text{OIDs\_per\_poll}}}{T_{\text{poll\_interval}}}
$$

**Example derivation:**
- Suppose $N_{\text{devices}} = 500$ routers.
- Each poll retrieves $N_{\text{OIDs\_per\_poll}} = 20$ variables.
- Polling interval $T_{\text{poll\_interval}} = 60$ s.

$$
\lambda = \frac{500 \times 20}{60}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{10000}{60} \approx 166.67 \; \text{PDUs / second}
$$

This tells the NMS designer how much CPU and bandwidth the management channel will consume. KTU problems may ask you to size the management VLAN accordingly.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — *Which SNMP Version to Choose?*

| Deployment Scenario | Recommended Version | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Home LAN, lab experiments | **v2c** | Simpler, supports GetBulk, no encryption needed |
| Cross-internet exposure (public cloud) | **v3 with authNoPriv** | Authentication prevents spoofed traps |
| SCADA / Industrial control | **v3 with authPriv** | AES privacy mandatory for compliance |
| Legacy 1990s equipment | **v1** | Old firmware; cannot be upgraded |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why UDP and not TCP?** SNMP is intentionally built over UDP because:
> 1. UDP is **stateless** → the agent does not need to maintain session tables, saving memory on embedded IoT devices.
> 2. **Retransmission is the manager's job** — if no Response arrives within $\approx 5$ s, the manager retries up to 3 times before flagging the agent as `down`.
> 3. **Trap messages** are fire-and-forget; TCP's handshake is wasted overhead.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SNMP Architectural Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NMS[Network Management Station]
        direction TB
        op[Network Administrator] --> ui[Management UI Dashboard]
        ui --> nmsCore[NMS Software e.g. Zabbix, PRTG]
        nmsCore --> mibCache[Local MIB Cache]
    end

    subgraph Cloud[Managed IoT Network - LAN/WAN]
        direction TB
        r1[Router 1 - Agent: UDP 161]
        r2[Switch 1 - Agent: UDP 161]
        r3[IoT Gateway - Agent: UDP 161]
        s1[Smart Sensor Node - Agent]
        s2[Smart Meter - Agent]
    end

    nmsCore -- "GetRequest : UDP 161" --> r1
    nmsCore -- "GetBulkRequest : UDP 161" --> r2
    nmsCore -- "SetRequest : UDP 161" --> r3
    s1 -- "TRAP : UDP 162" --> nmsCore
    s2 -- "INFORM : UDP 162" --> nmsCore

    r1 -. "Response : UDP 49152" .-> nmsCore
    r2 -. "Response : UDP 49153" .-> nmsCore
```

---

## 4.2 SNMP PDU Flow — GetRequest → Response (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Mgr as NMS Manager
    participant Agt as SNMP Agent
    participant MIB as Local MIB

    Mgr->>Agt: 1. UDP GetRequest (OID 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.5.0)
    Note over Agt: 2. Decode BER ASN.1
    Agt->>MIB: 3. Look up sysName
    MIB-->>Agt: 4. Return "EdgeRouter-XYZ"
    Agt->>Agt: 5. Validate community = "public"
    Agt-->>Mgr: 6. UDP Response (OID, value, noError)
    Note over Mgr: 7. Display in dashboard
```

---

## 4.3 SNMP OID Naming Hierarchy (Tree Structure)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root((1 iso)) --> org((3 identified-org))
    org --> dod((6 dod))
    dod --> internet((1 internet))
    internet --> mgmt((2 mgmt))
    internet --> private((4 private))
    internet --> snmpV2((6 snmpV2))
    mgmt --> mib2((1 mib-2))
    mib2 --> system((1 system))
    mib2 --> interfaces((2 interfaces))
    mib2 --> ip((4 ip))
    system --> sysDescr((1.1.0 sysDescr))
    system --> sysUpTime((1.3.0 sysUpTime))
    system --> sysName((1.5.0 sysName))
    interfaces --> ifTable((2.1 ifTable))
    ifTable --> ifInOctets((2.1.10 ifInOctets))
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: SNMPv3 Security Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app[Application PDU e.g. GetRequest] --> msgProc[Message Processing Subsystem]
    msgProc --> usm[User-based Security Model USM]
    usm --> auth[Authentication: HMAC-MD5 or HMAC-SHA]
    usm --> priv[Privacy: DES CBC or AES CFB]
    auth --> ber[BER Encoding]
    priv --> ber
    ber --> udp[UDP Layer - Port 161/162]
    udp --> ip[IP Layer - IPv4/IPv6]

    auth -. "Auth Key: 16/20 bytes" .-> usm
    priv -. "Priv Key: 16 bytes" .-> usm
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**List any three components of the SNMP architecture and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SNMP Architecture Components (Any 3 of 4):**
>
> 1. **Network Management Station (NMS):** A software application that runs on the administrator's machine, issues SNMP requests, receives responses/traps, and provides the management interface.
> 2. **Managed Device:** A network node such as a router, switch, server, or IoT gateway that has an SNMP agent installed.
> 3. **SNMP Agent:** A software module embedded in the managed device that maintains local management variables (MIB) and responds to NMS requests on **UDP port 161**.
> 4. **Management Information Base (MIB):** A standardized, hierarchical collection of manageable objects defined in ASN.1, addressed via Object Identifiers (OIDs).

---

### ❓ Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]
**Differentiate between SNMPv1, SNMPv2c, and SNMPv3 in terms of security.**

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | SNMPv1 | SNMPv2c | SNMPv3 |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Authentication** | Community string (plaintext) | Community string (plaintext) | **HMAC-MD5 / HMAC-SHA** |
> | **Encryption** | None | None | **DES / AES (Privacy module)** |
> | **Authorization** | Single community | Single community | **Per-user access control (VACM)** |
> | **Verdict** | Insecure over public networks | Insecure — community sniffable | **Secure — suitable for IIoT and cloud** |

> SNMPv3 is the only version offering **confidentiality, integrity, and authentication** — it is mandatory in modern IoT and enterprise deployments.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — SNMP Questions:**
> 1. **Do not confuse ports.** Agents listen on **161**, managers listen on **162** for traps. Reversing these is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 2. **State the SNMP version** when discussing security — answers that ignore v1/v2c/v3 differences score poorly.
> 3. **OIDs are written in dotted-decimal, not as raw integers.** `1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0` is correct; `4367492` is not.
> 4. **MIB ≠ database.** Explain that the MIB is a **schema/namespace**, not a physical database table.
> 5. **Trap vs. Inform** confusion is common — Inform requires a Response acknowledgment; Trap does not.

---

### ❓ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **SNMP architecture**. List the various **SNMP operations (PDU types)** supported by SNMPv1. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **SNMP message format** in detail. Explain the role of the **community string** and **VarBindList**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a)

The SNMP architecture follows a **manager–agent** model comprising **four components**:

1. **Network Management Station (NMS):** The central console (e.g., PRTG, SolarWinds) that issues management commands.
2. **Managed Devices:** Network elements (routers, switches, servers, IoT gateways) being monitored.
3. **SNMP Agent:** A daemon process on the managed device that maintains the MIB and responds on **UDP 161**.
4. **Management Information Base (MIB):** A standardized namespace of objects written in ASN.1.

**Reference diagram (must be drawn in exam):**

```
[ NMS Manager ] ---Get/Set/Trap---> [ Agent @ UDP 161 ]
       ^                                    |
       |----------- Response / Trap --------|
                                          [MIB]
```

**SNMPv1 PDU Types (5 operations):**

$$
\text{GetRequest} + \text{GetNextRequest} + \text{SetRequest} + \text{Response} + \text{Trap}
$$

- **GetRequest:** Manager retrieves a specific variable's value.
- **GetNextRequest:** Manager retrieves the next OID in the MIB tree (used for table walking).
- **SetRequest:** Manager writes/modifies a value on the agent.
- **Response:** Agent replies with the requested value or error code.
- **Trap:** Agent asynchronously alerts the manager of an event (e.g., link down) without being polled.

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b)

**SNMP Message Format (BER-encoded ASN.1 sequence):**

$$
\text{SNMPv1 Message} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{Version (INTEGER)} \\ \text{Community (OCTET STRING)} \\ \text{PDU (SEQUENCE)} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Field-by-field breakdown:**

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Version** | INTEGER | `0` for SNMPv1 |
| **Community** | OCTET STRING | Acts as a **plaintext password**; e.g., `"public"` (read), `"private"` (read-write). If mismatched, the agent returns `authorizationError`. |
| **PDU Type** | Tag | `0xA0` GetRequest, `0xA1` GetNextRequest, `0xA2` Response, `0xA3` SetRequest, `0xA4` Trap (older) / `0xA6` (newer Trap) |
| **Request-ID** | INTEGER | Matches a request to its response (transaction correlation) |
| **Error-Status** | INTEGER | `0` noError, `1` tooBig, `2` noSuchName, `3` badValue, `4` readOnly, `5` genErr |
| **Error-Index** | INTEGER | Index into VarBindList for the offending variable |
| **VarBindList** | SEQUENCE OF | List of (OID, value) pairs — the **actual payload** |

**Role of Community String:**
The community string functions as a **lightweight authentication mechanism**. The default `public` community grants read-only access, while `private` allows configuration writes. **However, it travels in plaintext**, making it vulnerable to sniffing — a major security weakness addressed in SNMPv3.

**Role of VarBindList:**
The VarBindList is a **sequence of (name, value) tuples** that carry the actual management data. For example, a single GetRequest can ask for the OID `1.3.6.1.2.1.1.5.0` (sysName), and the agent populates the value in its Response.

---

### ❓ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain **SNMPv3 architecture** with emphasis on its security features. How does it differ from SNMPv1/v2c? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With an example, explain the **MIB and OID structure**. How are objects addressed in SNMP? **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a)

SNMPv3 (RFC 3411–3418) introduces a **modular architecture** with three logical subsystems:

1. **Message Processing Subsystem (MPD):** Handles message format, encoding, and communication with the dispatcher.
2. **User-based Security Model (USM):** Provides **authentication** and **privacy**.
3. **View-based Access Control Model (VACM):** Decides **which MIB objects** a user is allowed to read/write.

**Security Features of SNMPv3:**

| Feature | Mechanism | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Authentication** | HMAC-MD5-96 or HMAC-SHA-96 | Verifies message integrity and source identity — prevents spoofing |
| **Privacy (Encryption)** | CBC-DES or CFB128-AES | Encrypts the PDU payload — prevents eavesdropping |
| **Timeliness Check** | Engine-time synchronization | Prevents **replay attacks** |
| **User Keys** | 16-byte (MD5) / 20-byte (SHA) | Per-user secret keys, not shared plaintext |

**Comparison:**

> - **SNMPv1/v2c:** Community string is sent **in cleartext**, offering **no confidentiality, no integrity, no per-user authentication**. Anyone capturing packets can read or replay them.
> - **SNMPv3:** Every message is **digitally authenticated** and optionally **encrypted**. Each user has a unique key, and VACM enforces **fine-grained MIB access** (e.g., user A can read interface stats, user B can also reset them).

**Why This Matters in IoT:** In **Industrial IoT and smart city deployments**, SNMPv1/v2c traffic is trivially sniffable on shared Wi-Fi or compromised switches. **SNMPv3 is mandatory** for compliance with IEC 62443 and similar industrial security standards.

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b)

**Management Information Base (MIB):**
The MIB is a **standardized collection of manageable object definitions** for a device. It is **not a database** — rather, it is a **schema** written in **ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation One)** that describes the structure of management data. The current standard is **MIB-II** (RFC 1213).

**Object Identifier (OID):**
Every manageable object is uniquely addressed by an OID, which is a **sequence of integers separated by dots**, forming a path from the root of the global OID tree.

**Example OID Walk:**

$$
1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0 \rightarrow \texttt{sysDescr}
$$

- `1` = **iso**
- `3` = **identified-organization**
- `6` = **U.S. Department of Defense (DoD)**
- `1` = **internet**
- `2` = **mgmt**
- `1` = **MIB-2**
- `1` = **system group**
- `1` = **sysDescr object**
- `0` = **scalar instance (always 0 for non-tabular objects)**

**OID Tree Visualization:**

```
iso(1) → org(3) → dod(6) → internet(1) → mgmt(2) → mib-2(1) → system(1)
                                                            ├── sysDescr(1)
                                                            ├── sysUpTime(3)
                                                            └── sysName(5)
```

**How objects are addressed:**
- **Scalar objects** (single value) end in `.0` (e.g., `sysName.0`).
- **Tabular objects** use **two indices** — the column index and the row index. For example, `ifInOctets` for interface 2 = `1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10.2`.
- The manager's request must specify the **exact OID**; wildcards are not allowed in standard GetRequest (a limitation addressed by GetBulk in v2c).

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these points before walking into the KTU exam hall:**
>
> ✅ **SNMP** is an **application-layer (Layer 7)** protocol defined by the **IETF**, used for **monitoring and managing** IP networks.
>
> ✅ Default ports: **Agent = UDP 161**, **Manager (for traps) = UDP 162**.
>
> ✅ Transport = **UDP (connectionless, stateless)**. Retries are the manager's responsibility.
>
> ✅ **Four architecture components:** NMS, Managed Device, Agent, MIB.
>
> ✅ **SNMPv1 PDU types (5):** GetRequest, GetNextRequest, SetRequest, Response, Trap.
>
> ✅ **SNMPv2c additions:** GetBulkRequest, InformRequest (acknowledged).
>
> ✅ **SNMPv3 security stack:** USM (HMAC-MD5/SHA + DES/AES) + VACM (access control).
>
> ✅ **Community string** = plaintext password in v1/v2c; default `public` (RO) and `private` (RW).
>
> ✅ **OIDs** are dotted-decimal paths: `1.3.6.1.2.1.1.x.0` = system group in MIB-2.
>
> ✅ **MIB walking** uses sequential **GetNextRequest** to traverse tables.
>
> ✅ **Trap = unacknowledged alert**, **Inform = acknowledged trap** (requires Response).
>
> ✅ **Error-Status codes:** 0=noError, 1=tooBig, 2=noSuchName, 3=badValue, 4=readOnly, 5=genErr.
>
> ✅ **SNMP in IoT:** Ideal for IP-based gateways & edge devices; for LLN/6LoWPAN, prefer **CoAP / LWM2M**.
>
> ✅ **Key RFCs:** 1157 (v1), 1901–1908 (v2c), **3411–3418 (v3)**.
>
> ✅ **Standard prefix for IETF-managed objects:** `1.3.6.1.2.1` (`iso.org.dod.internet.mgmt.mib-2`).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NETCONF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** NETCONF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of NETCONF

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NETCONF (Network Configuration Protocol)** is a session-based, XML-encoded network management protocol standardized by the **IETF in RFC 6241**, used to *install, manipulate, and delete the configuration* of network devices. It provides a robust mechanism for programmatic configuration management using a layered architecture comprising the **Content Layer, Operations Layer, Messages Layer, and Secure Transport Layer**.

In the context of **IoT (PCAMT501)**, NETCONF is classified as a *device-management and orchestration protocol* that operates alongside (or above) protocols like **SNMP, CoAP, and YANG**, providing **transactional, configuration-centric, and capability-aware** device control — which makes it indispensable for SDN, NFV, and large-scale IoT gateway management.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Remote-Control Chef" 🍳**
>
> Imagine a chef (the **network/IoT device**) locked inside a kitchen. You (the **controller/manager**) cannot enter the kitchen, but you can pass a **structured, signed work-order (XML <rpc>)** through a dedicated, encrypted pneumatic tube (**Secure Transport — SSH/TLS**). The chef reads the order, performs the action, and drops a **signed receipt (XML <rpc-reply>)** back into the tube. If the order is malformed, the chef rejects it *atomically* — meaning the kitchen is not left half-cooked.
>
> This is **NETCONF** — a precise, secure, transactional work-order system for managing devices.

### Key Properties (Memorize These!)
- **XML-encoded** — Human-readable, hierarchical data format.
- **Session-oriented** — Persistent, stateful connection (not query-based like SNMP).
- **Transactional** — Operations are *atomic* (all-or-nothing) on the **<candidate>** datastore.
- **Capability-negotiated** — Client and server exchange `<hello>` messages to agree on supported features.
- **Transport-agnostic** — Defined over **SSH (RFC 6242), TLS, HTTP/HTTPS**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Always mention that NETCONF was designed as the **successor to SNMP** for configuration management (SNMP is fine for *monitoring*, NETCONF is better for *configuration*).

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Constants
- **Default SSH Port for NETCONF:** **TCP port 830** (subscriber port).
- **Standard for YANG Data Modeling:** **RFC 7950** (YANG 1.1).
- **NETCONF Protocol Standard:** **RFC 6241** (replaces the older RFC 4741).
- **NETCONF over SSH:** **RFC 6242** (mandatory to implement).
- **Base Capability URI:** `urn:ietf:params:netconf:base:1.1`

## 1.4 Where NETCONF Fits in the IoT Stack

| Layer of IoT Stack | Role of NETCONF |
|---|---|
| **Application / Management Plane** | SDN controllers, orchestration dashboards, IoT gateways |
| **Service Layer** | YANG models describe device capabilities |
| **Network Layer** | Manages routers, switches, base stations, IoT gateways |
| **Perception Layer** | Indirectly via edge devices/gateways |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Layer Architecture of NETCONF (RFC 6241)

NETCONF is structured into **four strictly-separated, protocol-agnostic layers**. This separation is a *favourite KTU question* (3-mark / 7-mark).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why layered?** Each layer can evolve *independently*. The transport can change (e.g., from SSH to QUIC) without breaking YANG models, and YANG data models can change without breaking the transport.

### Layer 1 — Content Layer
- Contains the **configuration data** and **notification data**.
- Modeled using **YANG** (Yet Another Next Generation data modeling language).
- Independent of encoding and transport.

### Layer 2 — Operations Layer
- Defines a fixed set of **base protocol operations** exchanged as RPCs.
- Includes: `<get>`, `<get-config>`, `<edit-config>`, `<copy-config>`, `<delete-config>`, `<lock>`, `<unlock>`, `<close-session>`, `<kill-session>`.
- New operations can be added via **capabilities**.

### Layer 3 — Messages Layer
- Encodes operations and notifications into a machine-independent format.
- NETCONF **mandates XML** for this encoding (the only valid option in RFC 6241).
- Defines the framing: `<hello>`, `<rpc>`, `<rpc-reply>`, `<notification>`.

### Layer 4 — Secure Transport Layer
- Provides **confidentiality, integrity, and authentication**.
- Default: **SSH** (RFC 6242 — Mandatory to Implement).
- Optional: **TLS** (RFC 7589), **HTTP** (RFC 8071).
- Default port: **TCP 830**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (Key Terms)

| Concept | Symbol / Keyword | Standard / Value | Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Protocol RFC | $\text{NETCONF}_{RFC}$ | **RFC 6241** | "6241 = NET-work-CON-fig" |
| Default Port | $P_{830}$ | **TCP 830** | "8-3-0: SSH subscriber port" |
| Data Modeling | $\mathcal{M}$ | **YANG (RFC 7950)** | "YANG = Yet Another Next Gen" |
| Transport (Mandatory) | $T_{SSH}$ | **SSH (RFC 6242)** | "SSH is the MTI" |
| Encoding | $E$ | **XML** | "eXtensible Markup Language" |
| Hello Exchange | $\langle hello \rangle$ | Capability negotiation | "Hello first, talk later" |
| Datastore | $\mathcal{D}$ | running / candidate / startup | "Run, Can, Start" |
| Transactional Unit | $TX$ | All-or-nothing on candidate | "Atomic edits" |
| Capability URI | $U_{cap}$ | `urn:ietf:params:netconf:capability:<name>:1.x` | "URN = Uniform Resource Name" |

## 2.3 The Base Operations — Deep-Dive (Most Asked)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus:** Memorize the **9 base operations** — they appear almost every semester.

### 2.3.1 `<get-config>`
Retrieves *all or part* of a specified configuration datastore. **Does not** retrieve state data.

### 2.3.2 `<get>`
Retrieves **both configuration AND state data** from the device.

### 2.3.3 `<edit-config>`
Modifies a configuration datastore using one of **four operations**:
- `merge` (default) — Combines new config with existing.
- `replace` — Replaces configuration entirely.
- `create` — Creates a new entry, fails if it exists.
- `delete` — Deletes the specified entry.

### 2.3.4 `<copy-config>`
Copies one datastore to another (e.g., `<running>` ← `<candidate>`). **Replaces** target entirely.

### 2.3.5 `<delete-config>`
Deletes a datastore. **Exception:** `<running>` cannot be deleted (only `<candidate>` and `<startup>`).

### 2.3.6 `<lock>` and `<unlock>`
Acquires a **configuration lock** to prevent concurrent updates — analogous to a database row lock.

### 2.3.7 `<close-session>` and `<kill-session>`
Graceful vs. forceful session termination. `<kill-session>` is initiated *by a third party* forcibly aborting another manager's session.

## 2.4 The Four Datastores

| Datastore | Purpose | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **`<running>`** | Active config in RAM | Always exists, cannot be deleted |
| **`<candidate>`** | Holding buffer for edits | Atomic commit via `<commit>` |
| **`<startup>`** | Persistent boot config | Loaded on device restart |
| **`<intended>`** | Vendor-specific target | Optional, not in base spec |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SDN (Software-Defined Networks)**: NETCONF + YANG are the *de-facto* southbound APIs for OpenDaylight, ONOS, and Cisco NSO.
- **5G Core & RAN**: Used for NFV orchestration (ETSI MANO).
- **IoT Gateways**: Managing thousands of LoRaWAN/NB-IoT gateways remotely.
- **Cloud Networking**: AWS Transit Gateway, Azure Virtual WAN use NETCONF internally.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Configuring PLCs and industrial switches in factories.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Protocol Flow & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since NETCONF is a *protocol* (not a numerical formula), the "derivation" here is the **end-to-end protocol exchange flow** that you must be able to write on paper for full marks.

## 3.1 The Complete NETCONF Session — Step-by-Step Derivation

We derive the exact sequence of XML messages exchanged when a manager first connects, retrieves running config, modifies the candidate, and commits.

### Step 1 — TCP Connection Establishment
- Manager initiates **SSH connection** to the NETCONF server (default **TCP port 830**).
- TLS handshake (if using TLS) occurs here.

### Step 2 — Capability Exchange (⟨hello⟩)
Both sides send a `<hello>` message listing their **capabilities** (supported operations, data models, and protocol versions). Capabilities are advertised as **URIs**.

**Manager's ⟨hello⟩ (XML):**
```xml
<hello xmlns="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0">
  <capabilities>
    <capability>urn:ietf:params:netconf:base:1.1</capability>
    <capability>urn:ietf:params:netconf:capability:writable-running:1.0</capability>
    <capability>urn:ietf:params:netconf:capability:candidate:1.0</capability>
    <capability>urn:ietf:params:netconf:capability:startup:1.0</capability>
    <capability>urn:ietf:params:netconf:capability:validate:1.1</capability>
  </capabilities>
</hello>
```

**Server replies with its own `<hello>`** containing *its* capabilities. The common set is the **negotiated capability set** — features neither side supports are *not* usable in the session.

### Step 3 — Retrieve Current Running Config (⟨get-config⟩)
The manager requests the full `<running>` configuration filtered to a specific subtree (e.g., interfaces):

```xml
<rpc message-id="101" xmlns="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0">
  <get-config>
    <source><running/></source>
    <filter type="subtree">
      <interfaces xmlns="urn:example:interfaces:1.0"/>
    </filter>
  </get-config>
</rpc>
```

The server replies with an `<rpc-reply>` containing the matching XML subtree.

### Step 4 — Lock the Candidate Datastore (⟨lock⟩)
To prevent race conditions, the manager locks `<candidate>`:

```xml
<rpc message-id="102">
  <lock>
    <target><candidate/></target>
  </lock>
</rpc>
```

### Step 5 — Edit the Candidate Datastore (⟨edit-config⟩)
Modifications are sent to `<candidate>` (atomic buffer). The `operation` attribute can be `merge`, `replace`, `create`, or `delete`.

```xml
<rpc message-id="103">
  <edit-config>
    <target><candidate/></target>
    <config>
      <interfaces xmlns="urn:example:interfaces:1.0">
        <interface>
          <name>eth0</name>
          <enabled>true</enabled>
          <mtu>1500</mtu>
        </interface>
      </interfaces>
    </config>
  </edit-config>
</rpc>
```

The server responds with `<ok/>` if accepted, or `<rpc-error>` if validation fails.

### Step 6 — Validate the Candidate (Optional, capability-dependent)
If the `validate:1.1` capability was negotiated, the manager can run `<validate>` before committing.

### Step 7 — Commit the Candidate (⟨commit⟩)
This is what makes the change **active**. The entire `<candidate>` is **atomically merged** into `<running>`.

```xml
<rpc message-id="104">
  <commit/>
</rpc>
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If `<commit>` fails, the **entire transaction is rolled back** — the running configuration is unchanged. This is the *atomicity* property KTU examiners love to test.

### Step 8 — Persist to Startup (⟨copy-config⟩)
To survive a reboot, copy running → startup:

```xml
<rpc message-id="105">
  <copy-config>
    <source><running/></source>
    <target><startup/></target>
  </copy-config>
</rpc>
```

### Step 9 — Unlock & Close Session
```xml
<rpc message-id="106">
  <unlock>
    <target><candidate/></target>
  </unlock>
</rpc>
<rpc message-id="107">
  <close-session/>
</rpc>
```

## 3.2 YANG Modeling — The Schema Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **YANG = "Yet Another Next Generation"** data modeling language. It is to NETCONF what **JSON Schema** is to REST APIs.

### The YANG Module Skeleton (Critical for 7-mark answers)

```yang
module example-interfaces {
  yang-version 1.1;
  namespace "urn:example:interfaces:1.0";
  prefix ex-if;

  import ietf-interfaces { prefix if; }

  organization "Example Corp";
  contact "support@example.com";
  description "An example interface model for KTU exam purposes.";

  revision 2024-01-15 {
    description "Initial revision.";
    reference "RFC XXXX";
  }

  container interfaces {
    list interface {
      key "name";
      leaf name { type string; }
      leaf enabled { type boolean; default true; }
      leaf mtu { type uint16; default 1500; }
    }
  }
}
```

**Why this is testable:** A KTU 7-mark question may give you a YANG model and ask you to draw the resulting XML data tree, or vice versa.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: NETCONF vs. SNMP

| Feature | NETCONF | SNMP |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Use | **Configuration management** | **Monitoring & monitoring-based config** |
| Data Encoding | **XML** (verbose, structured) | **BER/ASN.1** (compact) |
| Transport | SSH / TLS / HTTP | UDP (v1, v2c) / TCP (v3) |
| Transactional | **Yes** (atomic commits) | No (single-PDU) |
| Data Modeling | **YANG (rich, typed)** | SMI / MIB (limited) |
| Scalability for Configs | **High** (filtering via XPath) | Low |
| Standardized | **RFC 6241** | RFC 3411–3418 |
| Best For | SDN, NFV, complex config | Polling, traps, alerts |

## 3.4 Failure / Error Handling Logic

The server returns `<rpc-error>` with structured sub-elements:
- `error-type`: `transport`, `rpc`, `protocol`, `application`
- `error-tag`: e.g., `invalid-value`, `operation-failed`, `data-exists`
- `error-severity`: `error` or `warning`
- `error-info`: additional structured data

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The NETCONF Four-Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["CONTENT LAYER (Configuration and Notification Data)"] --> B["OPERATIONS LAYER (9 base operations as RPCs)"]
    B --> C["MESSAGES LAYER (XML encoding with rpc, rpc-reply, hello, notification)"]
    C --> D["SECURE TRANSPORT LAYER (SSH RFC 6242, TLS, HTTP)"]

    A1["Modeled by YANG (RFC 7950)"] -.-> A
    B1["get, get-config, edit-config, copy-config, delete-config, lock, unlock, close-session, kill-session"] -.-> B
    C1["Default port TCP 830 (SSH subscriber)"] -.-> D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End NETCONF Session Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as MANAGER (Client)
    participant S as NETCONF SERVER (Device)

    M->>S: 1. SSH TCP Handshake (Port 830)
    M->>S: 2. hello (capabilities list)
    S-->>M: 2. hello (server capabilities)
    Note over M,S: Capability negotiation complete

    M->>S: 3. rpc - get-config (running, filter interfaces)
    S-->>M: rpc-reply (XML data)

    M->>S: 4. rpc - lock (candidate)
    S-->>M: rpc-reply - ok

    M->>S: 5. rpc - edit-config (candidate, merge interface)
    S-->>M: rpc-reply - ok

    M->>S: 6. rpc - validate (candidate) [optional]
    S-->>M: rpc-reply - ok

    M->>S: 7. rpc - commit (atomic merge to running)
    S-->>M: rpc-reply - ok

    M->>S: 8. rpc - copy-config (running to startup)
    S-->>M: rpc-reply - ok

    M->>S: 9. rpc - unlock (candidate)
    S-->>M: rpc-reply - ok

    M->>S: 10. rpc - close-session
    S-->>M: rpc-reply - ok
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Datastore & Transaction Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[("startup Datastore")] -->|Boot load| B[("running Datastore")]
    B <-->|atomic commit| C[("candidate Datastore")]
    M["Manager"] -->|edit-config| C
    M -->|validate| C
    M -->|lock or unlock| C
    M -->|copy-config| B
    M -->|copy-config| A
    M -->|get and get-config| B
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — NETCONF Position in IoT Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AppLayer["Application Layer (Cloud, SDN Controllers)"]
        App["Orchestrator and Dashboard"]
    end
    subgraph SrvLayer["Service Layer"]
        Yang["YANG Models (RFC 7950)"]
    end
    subgraph NetLayer["Network Layer (Routers, Gateways, Switches)"]
        Netc["NETCONF Agent (RFC 6241)"]
    end
    subgraph PercLayer["Perception Layer (Sensors, Actuators)"]
        Sens["Sensors and IoT End Devices"]
    end

    App <-->|NETCONF over SSH or TLS| Netc
    Yang -.-> Netc
    Netc --> Sens
    Sens --> Netc
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme assessment pattern** (Bloom's Levels L1–L4: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze). Marks are split as per the KTU model: Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks with internal choice.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / L1-Remember]`
**Define NETCONF. State its default transport protocol and port number.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
**NETCONF (Network Configuration Protocol)** is an IETF-standardized, **XML-based, session-oriented network management protocol** defined in **RFC 6241**, used for **installing, manipulating, and deleting configuration** of network devices.
- **Default transport:** **SSH** (Secure Shell), as per **RFC 6242**.
- **Default port:** **TCP 830** (the IANA-registered "netconf-ssh" subscriber port).

> [!TIP]
> **Mark split:** Definition (1) + Transport (1) + Port (1).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / L2-Understand]`
**Differentiate between `<get>` and `<get-config>` operations of NETCONF.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Aspect | `<get>` | `<get-config>` |
|---|---|---|
| Data Returned | **Configuration + State data** | **Only configuration data** |
| Datastore | Implied `<running>` | Any specified datastore |
| Use Case | Live status, counters, runtime values | Backup, audit, verification of configs |

> [!TIP]
> The key phrase to remember: **"`<get>` = Stats + Config; `<get-config>` = Config only."**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Choice 1: Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / L3-Apply]`
**(a)** Explain the **four-layer architecture of NETCONF** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe the **YANG data modeling language** and show how a YANG model is rendered as a NETCONF XML configuration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 marks

The four layers, from top to bottom, are:

1. **Content Layer (Top):** Carries the actual **configuration data** and **notification data**. It is fully decoupled from encoding and transport. YANG models define the schema for this content.

2. **Operations Layer:** Defines the **9 base protocol operations** (RPCs) such as `<get>`, `<edit-config>`, `<copy-config>`, etc. Operations are abstract — they are not bound to XML yet.

3. **Messages Layer:** Encodes the operations as **XML messages**: `<hello>`, `<rpc>`, `<rpc-reply>`, and `<notification>`. RFC 6241 mandates **XML** as the only valid encoding.

4. **Secure Transport Layer (Bottom):** Provides **confidentiality, authentication, integrity**. **SSH (RFC 6242)** is Mandatory-to-Implement. Default TCP port is **830**.

**Why this layering matters:** Each layer can be updated independently — e.g., switching SSH → QUIC does not affect YANG data models.

**Diagram (reproduced from Section 4.1):**
```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["CONTENT LAYER"] --> B["OPERATIONS LAYER"] --> C["MESSAGES LAYER"] --> D["SECURE TRANSPORT LAYER"]
```

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 marks

**YANG (Yet Another Next Generation, RFC 7950)** is a **hierarchical, typed, schema-validation language** used to describe the structure, syntax, semantics, and constraints of NETCONF configuration and state data.

**Key YANG Constructs:**
- `module` / `submodule` — top-level containers.
- `container` — groups related nodes.
- `list` — a sequence of records (with `key`).
- `leaf` / `leaf-list` — single or multi-valued data nodes.
- `type` — built-in (int, string, boolean, enumeration) or derived.
- `rpc` / `notification` — defines operations/events.
- `revision` — version control for the model.

**Example YANG Module (as above in Section 3.2):** The model defines a container `interfaces` containing a list of `interface` entries keyed by `name`, with leaves for `enabled` and `mtu`.

**Equivalent XML rendered as a NETCONF `<config>`:**
```xml
<config xmlns="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0">
  <interfaces xmlns="urn:example:interfaces:1.0">
    <interface>
      <name>eth0</name>
      <enabled>true</enabled>
      <mtu>1500</mtu>
    </interface>
  </interfaces>
</config>
```

**Why this matters:** The YANG model acts as a **contract** between the device and the manager. Validators can reject any XML that does not conform to the schema, providing **strong type safety** that SNMP/SMI lacks.

---

### Choice 1: Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / L4-Analyze]`
**(a)** With a neat **sequence diagram**, illustrate the complete **NETCONF session** showing capability exchange, `<lock>`, `<edit-config>`, `<commit>`, `<copy-config>`, and `<close-session>`. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compare **NETCONF and SNMP** on at least 6 technical parameters and state **two practical use-cases** where NETCONF is preferred. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 marks

The complete flow (as derived in Section 3.1) is:

1. **SSH TCP handshake** to port 830.
2. **Mutual `<hello>` exchange** for capability negotiation.
3. **`<lock>` on `<candidate>`** datastore.
4. **`<edit-config>`** on `<candidate>` with `merge` semantics.
5. **`<validate>`** (if supported).
6. **`<commit>`** to atomically push candidate → running.
7. **`<copy-config>`** from running → startup for persistence.
8. **`<unlock>` on `<candidate>`**.
9. **`<close-session>`**.

> Refer to Section 4.2 for the full sequence diagram. Examiner's expectation: every arrow must be labeled with the actual `<rpc>` and `<rpc-reply>` exchanged.

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 marks

| Parameter | NETCONF | SNMP |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | RFC 6241 | RFC 3411–3418 |
| **Encoding** | XML | BER/ASN.1 |
| **Transport** | SSH / TLS / HTTP (TCP-based) | UDP (v1, v2c) / TCP (v3) |
| **Configuration Model** | YANG (typed, rich) | MIB-II (SMI, limited) |
| **Transactions** | Atomic `<commit>` on `<candidate>` | None (single-PDU) |
| **Best Suited For** | Configuration orchestration | Monitoring, traps, polling |
| **Security** | Strong (SSH/TLS mandatory) | Weak in v1/v2c (community string) |
| **Data Retrieval** | Subtree / XPath filtering | Full or OID walks |

**Two Use-Cases Where NETCONF is Preferred:**

1. **SDN / NFV Orchestration:** Configuring thousands of virtual switches and routers requires **transactional, schema-validated, programmatic** operations — exactly NETCONF's strength. OpenDaylight and ONOS use NETCONF/YANG as the southbound API.

2. **Large-Scale IoT Gateway Management:** Managing firmware versions, security policies, and routing across 10,000+ IoT gateways benefits from **atomic configuration commits** and **YANG-validated rollbacks**, which SNMP cannot provide reliably.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse NETCONF ports:** Port **830 = SSH**; port **6513 = TLS**; port **433 = HTTPS** (alternative transports). Mixing these up = −1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention atomicity:** When describing `<commit>`, always state that it is an **all-or-nothing** operation. This is a favourite 1–2 mark follow-up.
> 3. **YANG ≠ XML:** YANG is a *modeling language*; XML is the *encoding*. They are not the same thing — writing the wrong pairing costs marks.
> 4. **Capability URIs:** Many students write "hello" without showing the capability exchange. KTU expects at least the **base:1.1** capability URI in the answer.
> 5. **`<running>` cannot be deleted:** A frequent conceptual error. Only `<candidate>` and `<startup>` can be deleted by `<delete-config>`.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **NETCONF** = Network Configuration Protocol, **RFC 6241**, **XML-based, session-oriented**, used for **device configuration management**.
- **Default transport = SSH (RFC 6242)**, **Port 830**.
- **YANG (RFC 7950)** = the data modeling language for NETCONF.
- **4 layers:** Content → Operations → Messages → Secure Transport.
- **9 base operations:** `get`, `get-config`, `edit-config`, `copy-config`, `delete-config`, `lock`, `unlock`, `close-session`, `kill-session`.
- **3 main datastores:** `<running>` (active), `<candidate>` (atomic buffer), `<startup>` (persistent boot).
- **`<edit-config>` operations:** `merge`, `replace`, `create`, `delete` (default is `merge`).
- **Atomicity** = all-or-nothing; failure during `<commit>` leaves `<running>` unchanged.
- **Capability exchange** happens in the initial `<hello>` — common capabilities include `base:1.1`, `writable-running:1.0`, `candidate:1.0`, `startup:1.0`, `validate:1.1`.
- **NETCONF vs. SNMP:** NETCONF wins for *configuration* (transactional, YANG-validated, secure); SNMP wins for *monitoring* (lightweight, trap-based).
- **Used in:** SDN (OpenDaylight, ONOS), NFV/5G, IoT gateway management, industrial automation.
- **Hello flow rule:** The first child of `<hello>` must be `<capabilities>`, followed by `<session-id>` (server only).
- **Errors:** Server returns `<rpc-error>` with `error-type`, `error-tag`, `error-severity`, `error-info`.
- **YANG constructs to memorize:** `module`, `container`, `list` (with `key`), `leaf`, `leaf-list`, `type`, `rpc`, `notification`, `revision`.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "N-E-T-C-O-M-F":**
> **N**amed RFC 6241, **E**ncoded in XML, **T**ransport is SSH/830, **C**onfig-centric, **O**perations = 9, **M**odeled in YANG, **F**rames in XML.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: YANG
LPWAN Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LPWAN Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LPWAN (Low-Power Wide-Area Network)** is a category of wireless communication technology designed to enable **long-range communication** (typically **2 km to 10 km** in urban areas and up to **50 km** in rural line-of-sight conditions) at **low data rates** (typically **0.3 kbps to 50 kbps**), while consuming extremely low power, allowing battery-operated end devices to function for **5 to 15 years** on a single battery charge.

The term **"LPWAN Applications"** refers to the diverse real-world use cases, deployment scenarios, and vertical industries where LPWAN technologies (such as **LoRa/LoRaWAN**, **Sigfox**, **NB-IoT**, and **LTE-M**) are deployed to connect a massive number of low-throughput, energy-constrained IoT devices (often called **end nodes**, **motes**, or **end devices**) to a centralized network server or cloud platform.

> [!NOTE]
> According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAMT501 – Module 2)** syllabus, the study of LPWAN applications is essential because it represents the *connectivity backbone* of massive Machine-Type Communications (**mMTC**) — one of the three primary use cases of **5G** networks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slow Mail of the IoT World"

Imagine a **postal service that delivers very tiny letters**:
- Each letter contains only a few sentences (low data rate).
- It can travel hundreds of kilometers to a central post office (long range).
- The sender uses a battery-powered stamp that lasts for years (ultra-low power).
- The delivery is not instant — it may take a few seconds, which is fine.

This is exactly how LPWAN works! It is **not designed** for streaming video or making phone calls. Instead, it is optimized for sending **small, infrequent packets** of sensor data (temperature, location, status updates) over very long distances using minimal energy.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **standardized performance metrics** used in KTU board examinations when describing an LPWAN application:

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Communication Range** | **2 – 10** (urban), up to **50** (rural LoS) | **km** |
| **Data Rate** | **0.3 – 50** | **kbps** |
| **Transmit Power** | **+14 to +20** | **dBm** |
| **Receiver Sensitivity** | **−130 to −142** | **dBm** |
| **Battery Life** | **5 – 15** | **years** |
| **Device Density** | **10,000 – 1,000,000** | **devices/km²** |
| **Latency** | **1 – 30** | **seconds** |
| **Frequency Bands** | **Sub-1 GHz** (e.g., 868 MHz EU, 915 MHz US, 433 MHz Asia) | **MHz** |

## 1.4 Why LPWAN Exists — The "Why" Behind the Technology

Before LPWAN, engineers had only two choices:
1. **Short-range, low-power** technologies like **BLE** or **Zigbee** (range < 100 m).
2. **Long-range, high-power** cellular technologies like **3G/4G** (high battery drain).

LPWAN fills the **gap** by offering **long range + very low power + low cost**, making it ideal for applications where devices are **remote, numerous, and must operate for years without maintenance**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of an LPWAN Application

Every LPWAN application follows a **standardized 4-layer architecture**. Understanding this is critical for KTU 14-mark questions.

### Layer 1 — **End Devices (Sensor Nodes / Motes)**
- Equipped with **sensors** (temperature, humidity, GPS, accelerometer, gas, etc.).
- Contain a **radio transceiver** (LoRa, Sigfox, NB-IoT modem).
- Powered by **batteries** (often **Li-SOCl₂** primary cells rated **2.4 Ah – 18 Ah**).
- Operate in **ALOHA-like** or **slotted** MAC protocols with **duty cycling** to conserve power.

### Layer 2 — **Gateways / Concentrators / Base Stations**
- Act as **transparent bridges** between end devices and the network server.
- Receive RF packets from thousands of devices simultaneously.
- Forward data to the cloud via **IP backhaul** (Ethernet, 4G, satellite).
- A single gateway can typically handle **10,000+ devices** within its coverage radius.

### Layer 3 — **Network Server**
- Performs **deduplication** (multiple gateways may receive the same packet).
- Handles **security** (encryption, frame counters, MIC validation).
- Routes packets to the appropriate application server.
- Manages **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)** algorithms.

### Layer 4 — **Application Server**
- Stores data in **time-series databases** (e.g., InfluxDB, TimescaleDB).
- Performs analytics, triggers alerts, and visualizes dashboards.
- Often hosted on **public clouds** (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "explain the architecture of an LPWAN application", always draw or describe all **four layers** in the order: *End Device → Gateway → Network Server → Application Server*. Examiners explicitly look for these keywords.

## 2.2 Major LPWAN Technologies (Comparative Analysis)

| Technology | Modulation | Spectrum | Data Rate | Range | Key Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **LoRaWAN** | **CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum)** | **Sub-1 GHz ISM** | **0.3 – 50 kbps** | **2 – 15 km** | Agriculture, Smart Cities |
| **Sigfox** | **Ultra-Narrow Band (UNB)** | **Sub-1 GHz ISM** | **100 – 600 bps** | **3 – 50 km** | Asset Tracking |
| **NB-IoT (LTE Cat-NB1/NB2)** | **QPSK (LTE-based)** | **Licensed Cellular** | **26 – 62 kbps** | **1 – 10 km** | Smart Metering |
| **LTE-M (LTE Cat-M1)** | **QPSK/16-QAM** | **Licensed Cellular** | **300 kbps – 1 Mbps** | **1 – 5 km** | Wearables, Vehicles |
| **Weightless (W/N/P)** | Various | **Sub-1 GHz** | **0.025 – 100 kbps** | **2 – 10 km** | Industrial Telemetry |

## 2.3 Core Theoretical Concepts Behind LPWAN Operation

### 2.3.1 **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) — The Heart of LoRa**

LoRa uses **chirps** — frequency sweeps that linearly increase or decrease across the entire channel bandwidth. This makes the signal **resistant to multipath fading**, **Doppler shifts**, and **interference**. The key parameter is the **Spreading Factor (SF)**, which ranges from **SF7 to SF12**.

- **Higher SF** → Longer range, lower data rate, more time on air, more energy consumed.
- **Lower SF** → Shorter range, higher data rate, less energy.

### 2.3.2 **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)**
The network server dynamically adjusts the SF, bandwidth, and transmit power of each end device to:
- **Minimize airtime** (conserve battery).
- **Maximize network capacity** (more devices per gateway).
- **Ensure reliability** (link margin > 0 dB).

### 2.3.3 **Duty Cycling**
To comply with **ETSI regulations** (e.g., **1% duty cycle in Europe** for the 868 MHz band), LPWAN devices transmit for at most **36 seconds per hour**. This is enforced by regional regulations, not the protocol itself.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **routinely tested** in KTU board exams for IoT courses. Memorize the table below.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $FSPL\,(dB) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | **Free-Space Path Loss** | $d$ in **km**, $f$ in **MHz** |
| 2 | $L_{budget} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{TX} - L_{RX} - S_{RX}$ | **Link Budget Equation** | **dB / dBm** |
| 3 | $EIRP\,(dBm) = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{TX}$ | **Effective Isotropic Radiated Power** | **dBm** |
| 4 | $L_{life} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg} \times 24 \times 365}$ | **Battery Life (years)** | $C$ in **mAh**, $I$ in **mA** |
| 5 | $D = \dfrac{1}{T_{frame} \times 3600} \times 100\%$ | **Duty Cycle** (as %) | $T_{frame}$ in **seconds/hour** |
| 6 | $R_b = \dfrac{SF \times BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR$ | **LoRa Bit Rate** | **bps** |
| 7 | $T_{air} = \dfrac{2^{SF}}{BW} \times N_{symbols}$ | **LoRa Time-on-Air** | **seconds** |
| 8 | $N_{max} = \dfrac{1 \text{ hour}}{T_{air} \times N_{channels}}$ | **Max devices per hour per channel** | dimensionless |

> **Note on Symbols:**
> - $P_{TX}$ = Transmit Power (dBm)
> - $G_{TX}, G_{RX}$ = Antenna Gains (dBi)
> - $L_{TX}, L_{RX}$ = Cable/Connector Losses (dB)
> - $S_{RX}$ = Receiver Sensitivity (dBm)
> - $SF$ = Spreading Factor (LoRa)
> - $BW$ = Bandwidth (Hz)
> - $CR$ = Coding Rate (typically $\vert 1 \mid \text{ to } \mid 4 \mid \text{ of } \mid 5 \mid$)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

LPWAN applications are deployed across **every major engineering vertical**:

- **Agriculture:** Soil moisture, weather stations, livestock tracking, irrigation control.
- **Smart Cities:** Streetlight monitoring, waste-bin fill-level sensing, parking sensors, air-quality monitoring.
- **Utilities:** Smart electricity/gas/water metering (automatic meter reading — AMR/AMI).
- **Logistics:** Container tracking, cold-chain monitoring, fleet management.
- **Healthcare:** Remote patient monitoring, fall detection (non-critical vitals).
- **Industrial IoT:** Tank-level monitoring, predictive maintenance of remote pumps, pipeline pressure sensing.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Forest fire detection, flood early-warning, glacier movement, wildlife tracking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

The Friis transmission equation in linear form is:

$$
\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

**Step 1 — Convert to decibel scale.**

$$
P_r\,(dBm) = P_t\,(dBm) + G_t + G_r + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the path loss term** $L_p = -20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)$.

$$
L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ **where** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**.

$$
L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d \cdot f}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in** $c$ **and simplify** for $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz.
Using $d_{km} = d_{m} \div 1000$ and $f_{MHz} = f_{Hz} \div 10^6$:

$$
L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d_{km} \cdot 10^3 \cdot f_{MHz} \cdot 10^6}{3 \times 10^8}\right)
$$

$$
L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \cdot 10^9}{3 \times 10^8}\right) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})
$$

$$
L_p = 20\log_{10}(41.88) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})
$$

$$
\boxed{FSPL\,(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** The constant **32.44** comes from $20\log_{10}(4\pi \cdot 10^9 / 3 \times 10^8)$. If you forget it, just remember the value **32.44** and the formula structure: $\text{Constant} + 20\log(d) + 20\log(f)$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Link Budget for an LPWAN Node

> **Problem (KTU-style):** An LPWAN sensor node uses LoRa in the **868 MHz** ISM band with a transmit power of **+14 dBm**, transmit antenna gain of **2 dBi**, receive antenna gain of **6 dBi**, cable/connector losses of **1 dB** on each side, and a gateway receiver sensitivity of **−137 dBm**. The device must communicate over a distance of **5 km** in a suburban environment with a path loss margin allowance of **+15 dB** for non-line-of-sight conditions. Calculate the **link margin** and determine if the link is feasible.

### **Step A — Compute the Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)**

Using $d = 5$ km, $f = 868$ MHz:

$$
FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(868)
$$

$$
FSPL = 32.44 + 20(0.6990) + 20(2.9385)
$$

$$
FSPL = 32.44 + 13.98 + 58.77
$$

$$
\boxed{FSPL = 105.19 \text{ dB}}
$$

### **Step B — Compute the Received Signal Strength (RSS)**

$$
RSS = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{TX} - L_{RX} - FSPL
$$

$$
RSS = 14 + 2 + 6 - 1 - 1 - 105.19
$$

$$
\boxed{RSS = -85.19 \text{ dBm}}
$$

### **Step C — Compute the Link Margin**

$$
L_{margin} = RSS - S_{RX} - \text{Additional Fade Margin}
$$

$$
L_{margin} = (-85.19) - (-137) - 15
$$

$$
L_{margin} = 51.81 - 15
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{margin} = 36.81 \text{ dB (Link is feasible, with high margin)}}
$$

### **Step D — Engineering Conclusion**

Since the link margin is **strongly positive (+36.81 dB)**, the LPWAN link is **highly reliable** at 5 km. The additional 15 dB margin absorbs the extra attenuation from buildings, vegetation, and weather effects in the suburban environment. The gateway will comfortably decode the LoRa packet at the highest possible spreading factor.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — LPWAN vs. Other Wireless Technologies

| Feature | **LPWAN (LoRa/Sigfox/NB-IoT)** | **Wi-Fi (802.11)** | **Bluetooth / BLE** | **Cellular (4G/5G)** | **Zigbee (802.15.4)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Range** | **2 – 50 km** | **30 – 100 m** | **10 – 100 m** | **1 – 10 km** | **10 – 100 m** |
| **Data Rate** | **0.3 – 50 kbps** | **11 – 1000 Mbps** | **125 kbps – 2 Mbps** | **10 – 1000 Mbps** | **20 – 250 kbps** |
| **Power** | **Ultra-Low** | **High** | **Low** | **High** | **Low** |
| **Battery Life** | **5 – 15 years** | **Hours** | **Days – Months** | **Hours – Days** | **Months – Years** |
| **Cost/Module** | **₹ 400 – 1500** | **₹ 300 – 800** | **₹ 100 – 400** | **₹ 800 – 3000** | **₹ 200 – 600** |
| **Topology** | **Star (Star-of-Stars)** | **Star (AP-centric)** | **Piconet / Mesh** | **Cellular** | **Mesh** |
| **Spectrum** | **Sub-1 GHz ISM** | **2.4 / 5 GHz** | **2.4 GHz** | **Licensed** | **2.4 GHz** |
| **Best For** | **Massive IoT, Remote Sensing** | **High-bandwidth Local** | **Wearables, Beacons** | **Mobile Broadband** | **Home Automation** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End LPWAN Application Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef sensorNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef gatewayNode fill:#7BC8F6,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef serverNode fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef cloudNode fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef userNode fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    sensor1["SOIL MOISTURE SENSOR NODE"]:::sensorNode
    sensor2["TEMPERATURE HUMIDITY NODE"]:::sensorNode
    sensor3["GPS ASSET TRACKER NODE"]:::sensorNode
    sensor4["SMART METER NODE"]:::sensorNode

    gw1["LORA GATEWAY 1 TOWER A"]:::gatewayNode
    gw2["LORA GATEWAY 2 TOWER B"]:::gatewayNode
    gw3["LORA GATEWAY 3 TOWER C"]:::gatewayNode

    ns["NETWORK SERVER DEDUP SECURITY ADR"]:::serverNode

    app1["AGRICULTURE APP SERVER"]:::cloudNode
    app2["UTILITY BILLING SERVER"]:::cloudNode
    app3["LOGISTICS DASHBOARD"]:::cloudNode
    app4["SMART CITY DASHBOARD"]:::cloudNode

    user["FARMER OPERATOR UTILITY OFFICER"]:::userNode

    sensor1 --> gw1
    sensor2 --> gw1
    sensor3 --> gw2
    sensor4 --> gw3
    sensor1 --> gw2
    sensor2 --> gw3

    gw1 --> ns
    gw2 --> ns
    gw3 --> ns

    ns --> app1
    ns --> app2
    ns --> app3
    ns --> app4

    app1 --> user
    app2 --> user
    app3 --> user
    app4 --> user
```

## 4.2 LoRaWAN Communication Flow (Uplink + Downlink)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant NODE as END DEVICE SENSOR NODE
    participant GW as LORAWAN GATEWAY
    participant NS as NETWORK SERVER
    participant AS as APPLICATION SERVER

    NODE->>NODE: 1 READ SENSOR DATA TEMPERATURE 28.5 C
    NODE->>NODE: 2 ENCRYPT PAYLOAD USING APPKEY AND NWSKEY
    NODE->>GW: 3 TRANSMIT LORA PACKET SF10 BW125 KHZ
    GW->>NS: 4 FORWARD PACKET VIA IP BACKHAUL ETHERNET OR 4G
    NS->>NS: 5 CHECK FRAME COUNTER AND MIC VALIDATION
    NS->>NS: 6 DEDUPLICATE IF MULTIPLE GATEWAYS RECEIVED
    NS->>AS: 7 ROUTE DECRYPTED PAYLOAD TO CORRECT APP SERVER
    AS->>AS: 8 STORE IN TIME SERIES DATABASE
    AS-->>NS: 9 SEND DOWNLINK COMMAND IF NEEDED
    NS-->>GW: 10 FORWARD DOWNLINK VIA ANY AVAILABLE GATEWAY
    GW-->>NODE: 11 TRANSMIT DOWNLINK ON RX2 WINDOW 869.5 MHZ
    NODE->>NODE: 12 PROCESS COMMAND AND EXECUTE ACTION
```
