# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define LPWAN. List any four key characteristics of LPWAN technology.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
LPWAN stands for **Low-Power Wide-Area Network**. It is a class of wireless communication technology optimized for long-range, low-power, low-data-rate IoT applications.

**Four key characteristics:**
1. **Long communication range** — 2 to 10 km in urban areas, up to 50 km in rural line-of-sight.
2. **Ultra-low power consumption** — battery life of 5 to 15 years.
3. **Low data rate** — typically 0.3 to 50 kbps.
4. **Massive device density** — supports 10,000 to 1,000,000 devices per square kilometre.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Mention any three major LPWAN technologies and one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Technology | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **LoRa / LoRaWAN** | Smart agriculture — soil moisture monitoring |
| **Sigfox** | Asset tracking — container location |
| **NB-IoT (Narrowband IoT)** | Smart metering — automatic electricity meter reading |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **ESE (End Semester Examination) Format — KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> Each Part B question has **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each carries **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **end-to-end architecture of a LoRaWAN-based LPWAN application** with a neat block diagram. Clearly describe the role of each layer (End Device, Gateway, Network Server, Application Server).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **any four real-world LPWAN applications** in detail, specifying the technology used, sensor type, data rate requirement, and expected battery life.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

A LoRaWAN-based LPWAN application consists of **four distinct layers**:

**1. End Device Layer (Sensor Node):**
This is the field-deployed hardware. It contains **sensors** (temperature, humidity, soil moisture, GPS, accelerometer, etc.), a **microcontroller** (e.g., STM32, Arduino), and a **LoRa transceiver** (e.g., Semtech SX1276 / SX1262). The end device is **battery-powered** (typically a 3.6 V Li-SOCl₂ cell rated 2.4 to 18 Ah). It performs periodic sensor readings, encrypts the payload using the **AppSKey** and **NwkSKey** keys, and transmits the packet using **CSS modulation** on a randomly selected channel. To conserve energy, the device spends **>99% of its time in sleep mode**.

**2. Gateway Layer (Concentrator):**
The LoRa gateway is a **transparent bridge**. It contains an **8-channel LoRa concentrator** that can simultaneously demodulate packets from hundreds of devices using different spreading factors. The gateway forwards the raw LoRa frames to the network server via an **IP backhaul** (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, or 4G). A single gateway can cover a radius of 2 to 10 km and handle 10,000+ devices. Gateways do **not** perform any encryption/decryption or deduplication.

**3. Network Server (NS):**
The NS is the **brain** of the LoRaWAN. It performs:
- **Deduplication** — multiple gateways may receive the same packet; the NS keeps only one.
- **Security validation** — checks the **Message Integrity Code (MIC)** and frame counters to prevent replay attacks.
- **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)** — dynamically adjusts each device's SF, BW, and TX power to balance reliability and airtime.
- **Routing** — sends the decrypted payload to the correct Application Server.

**4. Application Server (AS):**
The AS is where the **business logic** lives. It receives the sensor data, stores it in a **time-series database** (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB), runs analytics, triggers alerts (e.g., SMS to farmer if soil moisture < 20%), and displays data on dashboards (Grafana, custom web/mobile apps). The AS is typically hosted on **public cloud platforms** such as AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, or Google Cloud IoT.

> *(Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.1 for the schematic.)*

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Application 1: Smart Agriculture — Soil Moisture Monitoring**
- **Technology:** LoRaWAN (sub-1 GHz, e.g., 868 MHz in India)
- **Sensor:** Capacitive soil moisture probe (0–100% VWC)
- **Data rate:** ~1 packet per hour, payload 8 bytes
- **Battery life:** 8–10 years on a 2.4 Ah Li-SOCl₂ cell
- **Benefit:** Enables precision irrigation, saving 30–50% water.

**Application 2: Smart Metering — Automatic Meter Reading (AMR)**
- **Technology:** NB-IoT (licensed cellular spectrum) or LoRaWAN
- **Sensor:** Digital electricity/gas/water meter with pulse output
- **Data rate:** 1 reading per day, payload 16–32 bytes
- **Battery life:** 10–15 years (gas and water meters cannot be mains-powered)
- **Benefit:** Eliminates manual meter reading, enables dynamic tariffing.

**Application 3: Smart Parking — Vehicle Detection**
- **Technology:** LoRaWAN or Sigfox
- **Sensor:** Magnetic (AMR) or ultrasonic parking sensor embedded in ground
- **Data rate:** Status update every 1–5 minutes
- **Battery life:** 5–7 years
- **Benefit:** Real-time parking availability reduces traffic congestion by 15–30%.

**Application 4: Cold-Chain Logistics — Vaccine Monitoring**
- **Technology:** LoRaWAN with BLE hybrid
- **Sensor:** Digital temperature sensor (e.g., Texas Instruments TMP117, accuracy ±0.1 °C)
- **Data rate:** 1 packet every 10–15 minutes
- **Battery life:** 3–5 years
- **Benefit:** Ensures vaccine efficacy (must be stored at 2–8 °C), reduces spoilage losses.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - **Do NOT confuse "LoRa" (the physical-layer modulation) with "LoRaWAN" (the MAC-layer protocol).** Writing "LoRa" when you mean "LoRaWAN" costs **1 mark** in the KTU board.
> - **Do NOT mention only LoRa** as the sole LPWAN technology. Always mention **at least 2–3 alternatives** (Sigfox, NB-IoT, LTE-M) to score full marks in the "compare" or "list" type questions.
> - **Do NOT forget to mention ISM band regulations** (e.g., 1% duty cycle in 868 MHz Europe, no duty cycle restriction in the 2.4 GHz band). Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for spectrum/regulatory awareness.
> - **Always include units** (dBm, kbps, km, years) in numerical answers. A correct number without a unit loses 0.5–1 mark.

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With relevant equations, derive the **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** formula in the form $FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ where $d$ is in km and $f$ is in MHz. A LoRaWAN end device operates at **915 MHz** with a transmit power of **+17 dBm**, antenna gains of **2 dBi** (TX) and **8 dBi** (gateway RX), and total feeder losses of **2 dB**. If the receiver sensitivity is **−130 dBm**, calculate the **maximum line-of-sight range** in km.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **LoRaWAN, Sigfox, and NB-IoT** in terms of modulation, spectrum, data rate, range, device density, and business model. Which is most suitable for a **smart city streetlight monitoring** application and why?

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation.)

**Final formula:**

$$
\boxed{FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $P_{TX} = 17$ dBm, $G_{TX} = 2$ dBi, $G_{RX} = 8$ dBi, $L_{total} = 2$ dB, $S_{RX} = -130$ dBm, $f = 915$ MHz.

For **maximum range**, set $RSS = S_{RX}$ (link margin = 0):

$$
P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{total} - FSPL = S_{RX}
$$

$$
17 + 2 + 8 - 2 - FSPL = -130
$$

$$
FSPL = 17 + 2 + 8 - 2 + 130 = 155 \text{ dB}
$$

Now apply the FSPL formula:

$$
155 = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(915)
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d) = 155 - 32.44 - 20(2.9610)
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d) = 155 - 32.44 - 59.22 = 63.34
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d) = 3.167
$$

$$
\boxed{d = 10^{3.167} \approx 1469 \text{ km (theoretical line-of-sight)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** In practice, the range is **2–10 km** due to earth curvature, terrain, buildings, and Fresnel zone obstruction. The 1469 km value assumes **perfect vacuum propagation** with infinite antenna height, which is physically impossible. Examiners expect you to mention this **practical limitation** for full marks.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

| Parameter | **LoRaWAN** | **Sigfox** | **NB-IoT** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Modulation** | CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum) | UNB (Ultra-Narrow Band) | QPSK (LTE-based) |
| **Spectrum** | Sub-1 GHz ISM (unlicensed) | Sub-1 GHz ISM (unlicensed) | Licensed cellular (LTE bands) |
| **Data Rate** | 0.3 – 50 kbps | 100 – 600 bps | 26 – 62 kbps |
| **Range** | 2 – 15 km | 3 – 50 km | 1 – 10 km |
| **Device Density** | ~10,000 per gateway | ~1 million per BS | ~50,000 per cell |
| **Max Messages/Day** | Unlimited (with duty cycle) | 140 UL + 4 DL | Unlimited |
| **Business Model** | Open (deploy your own GW) | Managed (Sigfox operator) | Telecom operator subscription |
| **Module Cost** | ₹ 400 – 1500 | ₹ 600 – 1200 | ₹ 800 – 2500 |

**Best Choice for Smart City Streetlight Monitoring: LoRaWAN**

**Justification:**
1. **Sufficient data rate:** Streetlights only need to report on/off status, dimming level, and fault alerts — 0.3–50 kbps is more than adequate.
2. **Sufficient range:** A single LoRa gateway can cover thousands of streetlights in a 2–10 km radius, drastically reducing infrastructure cost.
3. **Cost-effective deployment:** No recurring operator subscription fee (unlike NB-IoT). City municipalities can deploy and own their network.
4. **Unlimited messages:** Sigfox's 140 messages/day limit may be restrictive for adaptive streetlight dimming that requires frequent updates.
5. **Long battery life for backup:** In areas where streetlights are solar-powered, LoRaWAN's ultra-low power is critical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam**

### **Core Definitions**
- LPWAN = Low-Power Wide-Area Network (range **2–50 km**, data rate **< 50 kbps**, battery life **5–15 years**).
- **LoRa** = Physical layer (CSS modulation by Semtech).
- **LoRaWAN** = MAC/network layer protocol (open standard by LoRa Alliance).
- **CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum)** = Linear frequency sweep modulation; resistant to multipath and interference.
- **SF (Spreading Factor)** = Ranges SF7 to SF12; higher SF = longer range, lower data rate, more time-on-air.
- **ADR (Adaptive Data Rate)** = Network-controlled optimization of SF, BW, and TX power.
- **Duty cycle** = Maximum 1% (EU 868 MHz) per ETSI regulations.

### **Key Technologies to Remember**
- **LoRaWAN** — open, unlicensed, CSS modulation, best for private deployments.
- **Sigfox** — ultra-narrowband, 140 messages/day, managed operator.
- **NB-IoT** — licensed cellular, best for utility metering.
- **LTE-M** — higher data rate than NB-IoT, supports mobility (handover).

### **Key Formulas (Recap)**
- **FSPL:** $32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$
- **Link Budget:** $P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{TX} - L_{RX} - S_{RX}$
- **Battery Life (years):** $\dfrac{C_{mAh}}{I_{mA} \times 24 \times 365}$
- **LoRa Bit Rate:** $\dfrac{SF \times BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR$

### **Architectural Layers (Always list all 4)**
1. **End Device / Sensor Node** (battery-powered, transmits small packets).
2. **Gateway / Concentrator** (transparent bridge, IP backhaul, no decryption).
3. **Network Server** (deduplication, security, ADR, routing).
4. **Application Server** (storage, analytics, dashboards, alerts).

### **Application Domains (Remember 4–5 examples)**
- Smart Agriculture (soil, weather, livestock)
- Smart Cities (parking, lighting, waste)
- Smart Utilities (electricity, gas, water meters)
- Logistics (cold chain, container tracking)
- Environmental (flood, fire, wildlife)
- Industrial (tank level, pipeline pressure)
- Healthcare (remote patient, fall detection)

### **Examiner's Golden Rules**
- ✅ Always mention **frequency band** (868/915/433 MHz).
- ✅ Always mention **modulation technique** (CSS/UNB/QPSK).
- ✅ Always mention **range, data rate, and battery life** with numbers and units.
- ✅ For comparison questions, use a **table format** — it scores extra marks.
- ❌ Never confuse **LoRa** with **LoRaWAN**.
- ❌ Never write "Wi-Fi" or "Bluetooth" as LPWAN — they are PAN/LAN technologies.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Pitfall Callout:** In **14-mark questions**, KTU examiners expect you to write **at least 3 full pages** with a **diagram + table + 4–5 applications**. Short 1-page answers are guaranteed to score below 7 marks. Always include a **neatly labeled block diagram** (can be a hand-drawn sketch in the exam) and a **comparison table** wherever applicable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LPWAN Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LPWAN Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCAMT501 - Module 2)**
> **LPWAN (Low-Power Wide-Area Network)** is a category of wireless communication technology specifically engineered to support **long-range connectivity** (typically **2–5 km in urban** and **10–40 km in rural** environments) for **low-bandwidth, low-power, and low-cost** IoT end devices. LPWAN technologies occupy the sub-1 GHz ISM band (e.g., 868 MHz in Europe, 915 MHz in the USA, 433 MHz in Asia) and are optimized for **massive Machine-Type Communications (mMTC)** — a core use case defined by 3GPP for 5G IoT.

The defining characteristics that distinguish LPWAN from traditional wireless networks (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Cellular) are encapsulated in the **"4-L" model**:

| Acronym | Property | Typical Value |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| **L**ow Power | Battery lifetime | **10–20 years** (on 2× AA batteries) |
| **L**ong Range | Communication distance | **2–40 km** |
| **L**ow Data Rate | Throughput | **0.3 kbps – 50 kbps** |
| **L**ow Cost | Module cost | **< $10** per end node |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Snail-Mail vs Courier" Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Intuition Box**
> Think of the IoT data universe as two extremes:
> - A **Courier (FedEx/UPS)** = Wi-Fi/5G Cellular → Delivers **heavy, urgent, high-definition** parcels (video streams, video calls) over short-to-medium distances, consuming massive fuel (battery).
> - **India Post (Snail Mail)** = LPWAN → Delivers **tiny postcards** ("Temperature = 24.5 °C, Battery = 87%") over **continental distances**, powered by a thin solar trickle or coin cell lasting **a decade**.
>
> LPWAN is the **postal system of IoT** — it sacrifices speed and payload size to achieve unprecedented range and energy efficiency, ideal for sensors reporting small status updates every few minutes/hours.

## 1.3 Why LPWAN is Needed — The Connectivity Gap

Before LPWAN, the wireless IoT landscape had a **massive coverage gap**:

| Technology | Range | Battery Life | Data Rate | Cost |
|------------|-------|--------------|-----------|------|
| Bluetooth/BLE | ~10 m | Days–Weeks | 1–2 Mbps | Low |
| Wi-Fi (802.11) | ~50 m | Hours | 11–600 Mbps | Medium |
| Cellular (4G/5G) | ~5 km | Days | 10–1000 Mbps | High |
| **LPWAN (LoRa/Sigfox/NB-IoT)** | **2–40 km** | **10–20 yrs** | **0.3–50 kbps** | **Very Low** |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Highlight:** LPWAN fills the **"trilemma gap"** — providing **long range + low power + low cost simultaneously**, which was previously impossible with short-range (Wi-Fi/BLE) or power-hungry (cellular) alternatives.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standard values are used throughout LPWAN link-budget calculations:

- Speed of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Free-space path-loss exponent: **n = 2**
- Urban path-loss exponent: **n ≈ 2.7 – 3.5**
- Typical ISM Sub-1 GHz frequencies: **f = 433 / 868 / 915 MHz**
- LoRaWAN regional duty cycle limit (EU 868): **1%**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Core Requirements Driving LPWAN Design (3GPP / IEEE View)

A KTU-mark-worthy answer must cover the **six design constraints** that shape every LPWAN protocol:

1. **Mobility & Roaming** — Devices may move (e.g., livestock tracking, asset trackers on trucks).
2. **Security** — Confidentiality + Integrity + Authenticity at the MAC and Application layers (e.g., LoRaWAN uses **AES-128** with two session keys: **NwkSKey** and **AppSKey**).
3. **Operating Lifetime** — Target: 10+ years on a single battery (energy harvesting friendly).
4. **Range** — Must cover deep indoor (basement meters, parking lots) and outdoor rural.
5. **Scalability / Capacity** — Support **> 50,000 devices per gateway** in dense urban deployments.
6. **Heterogeneity** — Uplink-centric, asymmetric traffic: **> 95% uplink, < 5% downlink**.

## 2.2 LPWAN Architecture — The 4-Layer Functional Stack

LPWAN systems follow a **star-of-stars topology** (vs mesh of Wi-Fi):

```
[End Device (Sensor/Actuator)]
        ↕ (Radio — LoRa/Sigfox/NB-IoT)
   [Gateway / Base Station]
        ↕ (IP Backhaul — Ethernet/4G/Satellite)
   [Network Server]  → handles MAC-layer scheduling, deduplication, ADR
        ↕ (TCP/IP)
   [Application Server] → business logic, dashboard
```

> [!NOTE] **Why "Star-of-Stars" instead of Mesh?**
> Mesh (used by Zigbee/BLE) requires intermediate nodes to **forward** packets, draining their batteries. In LPWAN, **end-devices are "leaf" nodes that transmit directly to the gateway**, preserving battery life. The **gateway is mains-powered**, so it can be the always-on relay.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU Module 2 derivations and numericals.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | **Friis Free-Space Equation** (Received power) | W |
| 2 | $P_r[\text{dBm}] = P_t[\text{dBm}] + G_t + G_r - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.44$ | Log-distance path loss (d in km, f in MHz) | dBm |
| 3 | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$ | **Log-normal shadowing** model | dB |
| 4 | $\text{Max Range} = 10^{\dfrac{P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{\text{sens}} - 32.44 - 20\log_{10}(f)}{20}}$ | Solving Friis for max distance d | km |
| 5 | $R_b = SF \times \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR$ | **LoRa bit rate** (SF = spreading factor, BW = bandwidth, CR = coding rate) | bps |
| 6 | $T_{\text{symbol}} = \dfrac{2^{SF}}{BW}$ | LoRa symbol duration | seconds |
| 7 | $E_b / N_0 = \dfrac{P_r}{N_0 \cdot R_b}$ | **Energy per bit to noise spectral density** (link quality metric) | dB |
| 8 | $P_{\text{link}} = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{\text{cables}} - PL(d)$ | **Link Budget** equation | dBm |
| 9 | $\text{Battery Life} = \dfrac{C_{\text{bat}}[\text{mAh}]}{I_{\text{avg}}[\text{mA}] \times 24 \times 365}$ | Battery lifetime estimation | years |
| 10 | $N_{\text{payload}} = 8 + \max\!\left\lceil \dfrac{(8PL - 4SF + 28 + 16CRC - 20IH)}{4(SF - 2DE)} \right\rceil (CR+4),\, 0$ | LoRa payload symbol count | symbols |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Pitfall:** Never use the **vertical bar `|` symbol** in markdown tables — always use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid breaking the table parser. Examples above are rendered with `\!` and `\max\!\lceil \cdot \rceil` for clean math.

## 2.4 Physical-Layer Techniques Enabling LPWAN

Three orthogonal physical-layer tricks differentiate LPWAN protocols:

### A. Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) — used in **LoRa (by Semtech)**
A chirp is a signal whose **frequency increases (or decreases) linearly with time** over the entire channel bandwidth. Spreading is achieved by multiplying the chirp by a baseband data symbol; the receiver de-chirps using a conjugate chirp, collapsing the energy back to a narrow tone.

> [!NOTE] **Why chirps?** They are **resilient to multipath fading, Doppler shift, and in-band interference** — making them ideal for the noisy, long-distance IoT channels.

### B. Ultra-Narrow Band (UNB) — used in **Sigfox**
Sigfox transmits **100 Hz wide** pulses at very low data rate (100 bps). The extremely narrow bandwidth raises the **energy per bit (Eb)** dramatically, allowing decoding at **−130 dBm** sensitivity (vs −120 dBm for LoRa).

### C. DSSS + Repetition Coding — used in **NB-IoT (LTE Cat-NB1/NB2)**
NB-IoT reuses LTE's physical layer with **subcarrier spacing of 3.75 kHz** (vs 15 kHz in LTE) and applies **up to 128 repetitions** of the same transmission to achieve deep indoor coverage (**MCL = 164 dB**).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Why LPWAN? |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| Smart Agriculture | Soil moisture, weather stations across 1000+ acres | 15-km range, 10-yr battery in fields with no power |
| Smart Cities | Parking sensors, streetlight control, waste-bin level | Deep indoor (basement) + 10-yr battery |
| Asset Tracking | Container / livestock / pallet tracking across continents | Long range + low-cost roaming |
| Utilities | Water/gas metering, grid monitoring | Star topology, no need for repeaters |
| Environmental | Forest fire detection, river flood sensors | Solar-powered, remote deployment |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Range from Friis Equation (High-Yield KTU Problem)

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> A LoRaWAN end-device transmits at **+14 dBm** with a transmit antenna gain of **+2 dBi**. The gateway has an antenna gain of **+8 dBi**, with cable losses of **2 dB**. The receiver sensitivity is **−137 dBm**. Operating frequency is **868 MHz**. Calculate the **maximum free-space range**.

### Step 1 — Recall the Friis equation in logarithmic form
The received power in dBm is given by:

$$P_r[\text{dBm}] = P_t[\text{dBm}] + G_t + G_r - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.44$$

where **d is in km** and **f is in MHz**.

### Step 2 — Identify all given parameters
- $P_t = +14$ dBm
- $G_t = +2$ dBi
- $G_r = +8$ dBi
- $L_{\text{cables}} = 2$ dB
- $P_{\text{sens}} = -137$ dBm
- $f = 868$ MHz

### Step 3 — Apply the link-budget identity
For **successful reception**, the received power must be **at least equal** to the receiver sensitivity:

$$P_r \;\geq\; P_{\text{sens}}$$

Therefore, including cable losses:

$$P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{\text{cables}} - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.44 \;\geq\; P_{\text{sens}}$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate the path-loss term
Move the path-loss term to the right-hand side:

$$20\log_{10}(d) \;\leq\; P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{\text{cables}} - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.44 - P_{\text{sens}}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the right-hand side numerically

$$P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{\text{cables}} = 14 + 2 + 8 - 2 = 22 \text{ dBm}$$

$$20\log_{10}(868) = 20 \times 2.9385 = 58.77 \text{ dB}$$

Substituting:

$$20\log_{10}(d) \;\leq\; 22 - 58.77 - 32.44 - (-137)$$

$$20\log_{10}(d) \;\leq\; 22 - 58.77 - 32.44 + 137$$

$$20\log_{10}(d) \;\leq\; 67.79 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for d

$$\log_{10}(d) = \frac{67.79}{20} = 3.3895$$

$$d = 10^{3.3895} = 2452 \text{ km}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Pitfall:** This is **free-space** range. In **real urban conditions** (path-loss exponent $n = 3$), the range collapses to roughly **20–30 km** — a 100× reduction. Always state the propagation model assumed.

### Step 7 — Recompute for n = 3 (urban)
The general log-distance path loss is:

$$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

For free-space reference at $d_0 = 1$ km: $PL(d_0) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(f) = 91.21$ dB (at 868 MHz).

Maximum allowable path loss for our link:

$$PL_{\max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{\text{sens}} - L_{\text{cables}} = 22 - (-137) = 159 \text{ dBm}$$

Setting $PL(d) = PL_{\max}$:

$$159 = 91.21 + 10(3) \log_{10}(d)$$

$$\log_{10}(d) = \frac{159 - 91.21}{30} = 2.2597$$

$$d = 10^{2.2597} \approx 18.18 \text{ km}$$

**Answer:** Free-space max range ≈ **2452 km**; urban (n=3) realistic range ≈ **18 km** ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LoRa Bit Rate and Time-on-Air

> **Problem Statement:** A LoRa end-node uses **SF = 10**, **BW = 125 kHz**, **CR = 4/5**, and transmits a **20-byte payload**. Compute the **bit rate** and the **time-on-air** in milliseconds.

### Step 1 — Compute the symbol duration
Each LoRa symbol spans $2^{SF}$ chips, and the chip rate equals the bandwidth:

$$T_{\text{symbol}} = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} = \frac{2^{10}}{125{,}000} = \frac{1024}{125{,}000} = 8.192 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the bit rate
For LoRa, the bit rate is:

$$R_b = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR$$

$$R_b = 10 \times \frac{125{,}000}{1024} \times \frac{4}{5}$$

$$R_b = 10 \times 122.07 \times 0.8 = 976.56 \text{ bps} \approx 0.977 \text{ kbps}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the number of payload symbols
Using the standard Semtech formula (with CRC = 1, Header = 0, DE = 1 for SF ≥ 11):

$$N_{\text{payload}} = 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{8 \times 20 - 4 \times 10 + 28 + 16 - 0}{4 \times (10 - 2)} \right\rceil \times (4 + 4),\, 0\right)$$

$$= 8 + \lceil \frac{160 - 40 + 44}{32} \rceil \times 8 = 8 + \lceil 5.125 \rceil \times 8 = 8 + 6 \times 8 = 56 \text{ symbols}$$

### Step 4 — Compute preamble symbols
Preamble = 8 symbols (default) + 4.25 sync symbols = **12.25 symbols**.

### Step 5 — Total time on air
$$T_{\text{air}} = (N_{\text{preamble}} + N_{\text{payload}}) \times T_{\text{symbol}}$$

$$T_{\text{air}} = (12.25 + 56) \times 8.192 = 68.25 \times 8.192 = 559.1 \text{ ms}$$

**Answer:** Bit rate ≈ **977 bps**, Time-on-Air ≈ **559 ms** ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Battery Life Estimation

> **Problem Statement:** A sensor node is powered by a **2400 mAh Li-SOCl₂** battery. Average current draw is: **TX (20 mA) for 0.56 s every 15 min**, **RX (10 mA) for 1 s every 1 hour**, **sleep (3 µA) rest of the time**. Estimate the **battery life in years**.

### Step 1 — Compute current per activity
- TX duty: $\frac{0.56 \text{ s}}{15 \times 60 \text{ s}} = 6.22 \times 10^{-4}$
- RX duty: $\frac{1 \text{ s}}{3600 \text{ s}} = 2.78 \times 10^{-4}$
- Sleep duty: $\approx 0.9987$

### Step 2 — Weighted average current
$$I_{\text{avg}} = (20)(6.22 \times 10^{-4}) + (10)(2.78 \times 10^{-4}) + (0.003)(0.9987)$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = 0.01244 + 0.00278 + 0.002996 = 0.0182 \text{ mA} \approx 18.2 \text{ µA}$$

### Step 3 — Apply battery-life formula

$$\text{Life} = \frac{2400 \text{ mAh}}{0.0182 \text{ mA} \times 8760 \text{ h/yr}} = \frac{2400}{159.43} = 15.05 \text{ years}$$

**Answer:** Battery life ≈ **15 years** ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LPWAN Reference Architecture (End-to-End Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ED1[End Device - Soil Sensor] -->|LoRa CSS 868 MHz| GW1
    ED2[End Device - Smart Meter] -->|LoRa CSS 868 MHz| GW1
    ED3[End Device - GPS Tracker] -->|NB-IoT LTE Cat-NB1| GW2
    ED4[End Device - Air Quality] -->|Sigfox UNB 868 MHz| GW3
    GW1[Gateway Concentrator - Mains Powered]
    GW2[eNB - Cellular Base Station]
    GW3[Sigfox Base Station]
    GW1 -->|IP Backhaul Ethernet 4G| NS1
    GW2 -->|S1-Lite Interface| NS1
    GW3 -->|Sigfox Cloud API| NS1
    NS1[Network Server - MAC Scheduling ADR Deduplication]
    NS1 -->|MQTT HTTPS| AS1
    AS1[Application Server - Dashboard Analytics]
    AS1 --> U1[User Mobile App]
    AS1 --> U2[Enterprise ERP System]
```

## 4.2 LPWAN Protocol Family Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LPWAN[LPWAN Family] --> LIC[Licensed Spectrum]
    LPWAN --> ULC[Unlicensed Spectrum]
    LIC --> NB[NBIoT - 3GPP]
    LIC --> LTE[LTE Cat M1 - 3GPP]
    ULC --> LORA[LoRaWAN - LoRa Alliance]
    ULC --> SIG[Sigfox - French Vendor]
    ULC --> RP[RPMA - Ingenu]
    NB --> N1[Subcarrier 3.75 kHz]
    NB --> N2[164 dB MCL]
    NB --> N3[200 kHz Bandwidth]
    LTE --> L1[1.4 MHz Bandwidth]
    LTE --> L2[155 dB MCL]
    LORA --> R1[CSS Chirp 125 to 500 kHz]
    LORA --> R2[157 dB MCL]
    LORA --> R3[Star of Stars Topology]
    SIG --> S1[UNB 100 Hz 100 bps]
    SIG --> S2[162 dB MCL]
```

## 4.3 LoRaWAN MAC Layer — Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Node Transmits Uplink Frame] --> B[Gateway Forwards to Network Server]
    B --> C{Network Server Evaluates SNR}
    C -->|SNR > 15 dB Margin| D[Decrease Spreading Factor]
    C -->|SNR 5 to 15 dB| E[Keep Current SF]
    C -->|SNR less than 5 dB| F[Increase Spreading Factor]
    D --> G[Send LinkADRReq MAC Command Downlink]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Node Adjusts SF BW and Tx Power]
    H --> I[Next Uplink at Optimized Settings]
    E --> I
```

## 4.4 Energy Budget per LoRaWAN Transmission Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Sleep 3 uA 99.9% duty] --> W[Wake up RTC Timer]
    W --> PRE[Preamble Listen 10 mA 1 s]
    PRE --> TX[TX Burst 20 to 100 mA 0.5 s]
    TX --> RX1[RX1 Window 10 mA 1 s]
    RX1 --> RX2[RX2 Window 10 mA 1 s]
    RX2 --> PROC[MCU Processing 5 mA 0.2 s]
    PROC --> S
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the diagram:** The **RX1/RX2 receive windows** after a TX are critical — they consume ~10 mA for 2 seconds per cycle. Devices using **Class A** (the lowest-power class) only open these windows **after their own transmission**, not at random. This is why LoRaWAN Class A devices achieve the 10–15 year battery life.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define LPWAN. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from traditional wireless technologies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** LPWAN (Low-Power Wide-Area Network) is a wireless communication technology designed for long-range, low-power, low-data-rate, and low-cost IoT connectivity, typically operating in the sub-1 GHz unlicensed ISM band.

> **Four Characteristics (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Range:** 2–5 km (urban) to 10–40 km (rural), versus ~50 m for Wi-Fi/BLE.
> 2. **Power:** Battery life of 10–20 years on a single cell, versus hours/days for cellular.
> 3. **Data rate:** 0.3–50 kbps, suited for periodic sensor reports, not video/voice.
> 4. **Cost & Scalability:** Module cost < $10, supports > 50,000 devices per gateway.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)? Why is it used in LoRa physical layer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **CSS Definition (1.5 marks):** Chirp Spread Spectrum is a modulation technique in which the carrier frequency **sweeps linearly across the entire channel bandwidth** during each symbol period. In LoRa, a chirp that sweeps upward represents a "1" bit, while a downward sweep represents a "0" bit (or vice versa, depending on the data symbol mapping).

> **Reasons for adoption (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Robustness to multipath fading** — chirps spread energy across time and frequency, so narrow-band fades affect only a small portion of the symbol.
> 2. **Low-power demodulation** — the receiver correlates with a conjugate chirp using a simple FFT, avoiding complex equalizers.
> 3. **Resilience to Doppler and interference** — chirps remain decodable even with frequency offsets typical of low-cost oscillators.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Pattern:** Part B carries **internal choice**. Solve **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each sub-part is 7 marks. Total = 14 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

#### **(a) Explain the reference architecture of an LPWAN system with a neat block diagram. List the functions of each layer. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An LPWAN system follows a **4-tier reference architecture**:

| Tier | Block | Function |
|------|-------|----------|
| 1 | **End Devices (Sensors/Actuators)** | Sense physical parameters (temp, humidity, GPS, vibration); modulate data using CSS/UNB/DSSS; transmit via single-hop radio link. |
| 2 | **Gateway / Base Station** | Mains-powered concentrator; receives frames from thousands of end-devices; forwards over IP backhaul (Ethernet/4G/Satellite). **No MAC-layer filtering** at this stage. |
| 3 | **Network Server (NS)** | Performs MAC-layer processing: **frame deduplication, ADR (Adaptive Data Rate), security (AES-128), downlink scheduling, roaming.** |
| 4 | **Application Server (AS)** | Business logic, dashboards, alerts, integration with ERP/SCADA/Cloud (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub). |

**Key protocol details (for full 7 marks):**
- Communication is **uplink-heavy (> 95% uplink traffic)**.
- End-devices use **Class A** (bi-directional with TX-triggered windows) to maximize battery life.
- Security uses **two AES-128 keys**: `NwkSKey` (for MAC-layer integrity) and `AppSKey` (for end-to-end payload encryption), ensuring the network operator cannot read sensor data.
- Roaming is supported via **Join Server** entities (e.g., in LoRaWAN 1.1).

---

#### **(b) A LoRa end-device transmits at +14 dBm through a 2 dBi antenna. The gateway has an 8 dBi antenna with 2 dB cable loss, and its sensitivity is −137 dBm. Calculate the maximum range in free space and in urban environment (n = 3) at 868 MHz. State the link budget. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Link Budget Equation:**

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{\text{losses}} - PL(d)$$

**Maximum allowable path loss:**

$$PL_{\max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{\text{sens}} - L_{\text{cables}}$$

$$PL_{\max} = 14 + 2 + 8 - (-137) - 2 = 159 \text{ dB}$$

**Case 1: Free Space (n = 2)**

Path loss at 1 km reference distance:

$$PL(d_0) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(868) = 32.44 + 58.77 = 91.21 \text{ dB}$$

$$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10(2)\log_{10}(d) = 91.21 + 20\log_{10}(d)$$

Setting $PL(d) = 159$:

$$20\log_{10}(d) = 159 - 91.21 = 67.79 \Rightarrow d = 10^{3.3895} \approx 2452 \text{ km}$$

**Case 2: Urban (n = 3)**

$$PL(d) = 91.21 + 30\log_{10}(d)$$

$$30\log_{10}(d) = 159 - 91.21 = 67.79 \Rightarrow \log_{10}(d) = 2.2597$$

$$d = 10^{2.2597} \approx 18.18 \text{ km}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — d in **km** and f in **MHz** for the −32.44 constant; **m/MHz** for −32.44 + 20log(f) + 20log(d) in some textbooks. Always verify.
> 2. **Ignoring cable losses** — the 2 dB cable loss at the gateway side **reduces** effective sensitivity and **must** be subtracted.
> 3. **Forgetting antenna gain symmetry** — both Tx and Rx gains are additive; missing either loses 2–4 marks.
> 4. **Quoting free-space range as "real-world range"** — always state the propagation model assumed (n = 2 for free space, n = 2.7–3.5 for urban, n = 4–5 for indoor).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) Compare LoRaWAN, Sigfox, and NB-IoT across 8 key parameters. Highlight the spectrum licensing, data rate, range, device count, and power consumption differences. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | **LoRaWAN** | **Sigfox** | **NB-IoT (Cat-NB1)** |
|-----------|-------------|------------|----------------------|
| **Spectrum** | Unlicensed (868/915/433 MHz ISM) | Unlicensed (868/902 MHz) | **Licensed** (in-band LTE, guard-band, or stand-alone) |
| **Modulation** | Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) | Ultra-Narrow Band (UNB, 100 Hz) | QPSK with DSSS, subcarrier 3.75 kHz |
| **Data Rate** | 0.25 – 50 kbps | 100 bps UL / 600 bps DL | ~ 26 kbps DL / 62 kbps UL |
| **Range / MCL** | 157 dB MCL (≈ 15 km urban) | 162 dB MCL (≈ 20 km urban) | 164 dB MCL (≈ 25 km urban) |
| **Max Payload** | 51–222 bytes (depends on SF) | 12 bytes UL / 8 bytes DL | 1600 bytes |
| **Battery Life** | 10–15 years (Class A) | 10–15 years | 10+ years with PSM/eDRX |
| **Mobility / Roaming** | Yes (Join Server in v1.1) | Limited (Sigfox Cloud) | Yes (cellular-grade handover) |
| **Module Cost** | $8 – $12 | $3 – $5 (cheapest) | $10 – $20 |
| **Max Devices/GW** | ~ 50,000 (per 8-channel GW) | ~ 1,000,000 / cell | ~ 50,000 / cell |
| **Topology** | Star-of-stars | Star | Star (uses LTE eNB) |
| **Ownership** | Open (LoRa Alliance) | Proprietary (Sigfox SA) | 3GPP Standard |

> **Verdict (for 7-mark completeness):** **LoRaWAN** is best for **flexible, private deployments**; **Sigfox** for **ultra-low-cost, tiny-payload** use cases; **NB-IoT** for **carrier-grade, mobile, deep-indoor** applications where licensed spectrum is available.

---

#### **(b) A LoRa node uses SF = 9, BW = 250 kHz, CR = 4/6, payload = 30 bytes. Compute the bit rate, symbol duration, and total time-on-air. Assume preamble = 12.25 symbols. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Symbol Duration:**

$$T_{\text{sym}} = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} = \frac{2^9}{250{,}000} = \frac{512}{250{,}000} = 2.048 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Bit Rate:**

$$R_b = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR = 9 \times \frac{250{,}000}{512} \times \frac{4}{6}$$

$$R_b = 9 \times 488.28 \times 0.6667 = 2929.7 \text{ bps} \approx 2.93 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Payload Symbols (CRC = 1, Header = 0, DE = 0 for SF < 11):**

$$N_{\text{payload}} = 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{8 \times 30 - 4 \times 9 + 28 + 16 - 0}{4 \times (9 - 0)} \right\rceil \times (4 + 6),\, 0\right)$$

$$= 8 + \left\lceil \frac{240 - 36 + 44}{36} \right\rceil \times 10 = 8 + \lceil 6.889 \rceil \times 10 = 8 + 7 \times 10 = 78 \text{ symbols}$$

**Step 4 — Total Time-on-Air:**

$$T_{\text{air}} = (12.25 + 78) \times 2.048 \text{ ms} = 90.25 \times 2.048 = 184.83 \text{ ms}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for LoRa calculations:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `max(..., 0)` floor** — empty payloads are still positive symbols.
> 2. **Mismatching DE flag** — DE = 1 only when SF ≥ 11; using DE = 1 for SF = 9 overestimates time-on-air.
> 3. **Unit confusion on coding rate** — CR = 4/5 means **4 data bits + 1 parity** = 5 total bits; some texts write it as 4/5 directly — keep it consistent.
> 4. **Forgetting preamble** — 12.25 symbols (8 preamble + 4.25 sync) is mandatory; missing it underestimates $T_{\text{air}}$ by ~10%.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **LPWAN** = Long-range, low-power, low-data-rate, low-cost wireless WAN for IoT.
- ☐ **MCL (Maximum Coupling Loss)** = The maximum path loss a link can tolerate; LoRaWAN ≈ 157 dB, Sigfox ≈ 162 dB, NB-IoT ≈ 164 dB.
- ☐ **CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum)** = Linear frequency-sweep modulation used in LoRa.
- ☐ **UNB (Ultra-Narrow Band)** = Sigfox's 100-Hz-wide signal at 100 bps.
- ☐ **DSSS** = Direct-Sequence Spread Spectrum used in NB-IoT.

### Critical Numerical Constants
- ☐ Free-space path loss constant: **−32.44 dB** (when d in km, f in MHz).
- ☐ Sub-1 GHz ISM bands: **433 / 868 / 915 MHz**.
- ☐ LoRa SF range: **SF7 to SF12** (each +1 doubles symbol duration, +2.5 dB link margin).
- ☐ LoRa bandwidths: **125 / 250 / 500 kHz**.
- ☐ LoRaWAN EU868 duty cycle: **1%** (regulatory cap).

### Must-Know Differences
- ☐ **LoRa = physical layer (CSS modulation)** | **LoRaWAN = MAC layer protocol** (a common exam trap).
- ☐ **NB-IoT is 3GPP / licensed** vs **LoRaWAN + Sigfox are unlicensed**.
- ☐ **Star-of-stars topology** — not mesh — is the defining LPWAN choice for battery.
- ☐ **LoRaWAN Class A** (lowest power) vs **Class B** (synchronized beacons) vs **Class C** (always RX — mains powered).

### Formula Power-Pack (Memorize All)
- ☐ Friis free-space equation in dB form.
- ☐ Log-distance path loss with $n$.
- ☐ LoRa bit rate and symbol duration.
- ☐ LoRa payload symbol count (Semtech formula).
- ☐ Battery life = $C_{\text{bat}} / (I_{\text{avg}} \times 8760)$.

### Real-World Engineering Context
- ☐ LoRaWAN is **best for private networks** (smart farms, campuses, factories).
- ☐ NB-IoT is **best for carrier-managed** deployments (utility metering, asset tracking across cities).
- ☐ Sigfox is **best for ultra-low-cost, low-duty** applications (e.g., one uplink per day for logistics).
- ☐ Security: **LoRaWAN uses AES-128** with **NwkSKey + AppSKey**; LoRaWAN 1.1 added a **Join Server** for root key isolation.

### Exam-Day Mnemonics
- ☐ **"4-L Rule"** → **L**ow Power, **L**ong Range, **L**ow Data Rate, **L**ow Cost.
- ☐ **"LoRa = Layer 1, LoRaWAN = Layer 2"** — like Wi-Fi vs 802.11.
- ☐ **"+2.5 dB per SF step"** — each increment of Spreading Factor buys **2.5 dB** of additional link margin at the cost of **halving the bit rate** and **doubling the time-on-air**.

> **End of Module 2 — LPWAN Technologies Notes**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — CELLULAR (3GPP) AND NON-3GPP STANDARDS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3GPP (3rd Generation Partnership Project)** is a global collaborative standards organization that unites **seven regional telecommunications standard bodies** (ARIB, ATIS, CCSA, TSDSI, TTA, TTC, ETSI). It defines the protocols and specifications governing **cellular telecommunications networks**, including **GSM, UMTS, LTE, LTE-Advanced, 5G NR**, and the IoT-specific variants **LTE-M (Long Term Evolution for Machines) and NB-IoT (Narrowband IoT)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-3GPP Standards** refer to all wireless communication technologies used for IoT connectivity that operate **outside** the 3GPP cellular ecosystem. These include short-range PAN/LAN technologies (**Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Zigbee, Z-Wave, NFC, RFID**) and long-range LPWAN protocols (**LoRa, Sigfox, Ingenu, Weightless**), typically managed by IEEE, IETF, or proprietary alliances.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the **road transport system of a country**:

- **3GPP (Cellular Networks)** → These are the **National Highways and Expressways** (NH-44, Golden Quadrilateral). They are **highly regulated, standardized, ubiquitous, licensed, and operated by government-authorized telecom operators** (Jio, Airtel, Vodafone). Any device (car/IoT sensor) using these highways must follow strict lane discipline, speed limits, and toll rules. They support **long-distance travel** with high reliability.

- **Non-3GPP (Wi-Fi, LoRa, Zigbee, etc.)** → These are the **local colony roads, village pathways, and private driveways**. They may be built by **private builders, RWAs, or municipalities** without a central government license. They are **cheaper, faster to deploy, and serve local traffic**, but cannot match the nationwide coverage of highways.

### 1.3 Why Both Coexist in IoT

> [!NOTE]
> The IoT ecosystem is **fragmented by application requirements**. A **smart electricity meter** deployed in a remote village needs **long battery life + wide coverage** → **NB-IoT (3GPP)**. A **wireless earbud** streaming music to a phone needs **high bandwidth + low cost** → **Bluetooth (Non-3GPP)**. Neither can replace the other, hence the **dual-track standardization** approach.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

| Metric | Symbol | 3GPP (Cellular) | Non-3GPP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coverage Range** | $d$ | **Up to 35 km (NB-IoT)** | **10 m (NFC) to 15 km (LoRa)** |
| **Data Rate** | $R_b$ | **35 kbps (NB-IoT) to 20 Gbps (5G)** | **100 kbps (LoRa) to 9.6 Gbps (Wi-Fi 6)** |
| **Latency** | $t_{lat}$ | **< 1 ms (5G URLLC)** | **2.5 ms (Wi-Fi 6) to 10 s (Sigfox)** |
| **Licensed Spectrum** | $f$ | **Yes (Exclusive licensed bands)** | **No (ISM bands)** |
| **Device Density** | $\rho_d$ | **10⁶ devices/km² (5G)** | **65,000 (Zigbee) per network** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 3GPP Cellular Evolution for IoT

The 3GPP standard has evolved its cellular architecture across **Releases** to natively support IoT workloads. KTU 2024 syllabus specifically focuses on the **machine-type communication (MTC) optimization cycle**.

#### 2.1.1 LTE-M (Long Term Evolution for Machines) — Release 8 → 13

- Operates within the **existing LTE band** (no new hardware).
- Supports **bandwidth of 1.4 MHz** (narrower than full LTE).
- **Peak data rate: ~1 Mbps** (uplink/downlink).
- **Half-duplex FDD** mode supported in Cat-M1 to reduce modem cost.
- Allows **device power save mode (PSM)** and **extended discontinuous reception (eDRX)** for battery life of **up to 10 years**.
- Supports **mobility** — handover between cells is permitted (suitable for asset tracking, vehicle IoT).

#### 2.1.2 NB-IoT (Narrowband IoT) — Release 13

- Operates in a **single 180 kHz resource block** (very narrow).
- **Three deployment modes**:
  * **In-band**: Inside an LTE carrier (uses resource blocks of LTE).
  * **Guard-band**: Within the guard spectrum of an LTE carrier.
  * **Standalone**: In refarmed GSM spectrum (e.g., 900 MHz).
- **Peak data rate: ~26 kbps (DL) / 62 kbps (UL)**.
- **Coupling loss target: 164 dB** (massive coverage, 20 dB better than standard LTE).
- Supports **up to 50,000 devices per cell**.
- **No handover** — primarily designed for **stationary, low-power devices** (smart meters, environmental sensors).

#### 2.1.3 5G NR (New Radio) — Release 15 onwards

5G introduces **three service profiles** critical for IoT:

1. **eMBB** (enhanced Mobile Broadband) → High throughput (smartphones, AR/VR).
2. **mMTC** (massive Machine-Type Communications) → High device density (1M devices/km²).
3. **URLLC** (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications) → < 1 ms latency, 99.999% reliability (autonomous vehicles, industrial robotics).

### 2.2 Non-3GPP Standards Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-3GPP technologies are classified by physical range (PAN/LAN/LPWAN), frequency, and standardization body.**

#### 2.2.1 Short-Range Non-3GPP (PAN/LAN)

| Technology | Standard Body | Frequency Band | Range | Data Rate | Topology |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi (802.11ax)** | IEEE | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | ~35 m (indoor) | **9.6 Gbps** | Star |
| **Bluetooth LE (5.x)** | Bluetooth SIG | 2.4 GHz | ~200 m (BLE 5) | 2 Mbps | Piconet/Scatternet |
| **Zigbee (802.15.4)** | IEEE / Zigbee Alliance | 868 / 915 MHz / 2.4 GHz | ~100 m | 250 kbps | Mesh |
| **Z-Wave** | Z-Wave Alliance | 908.42 / 868.42 MHz | ~30 m (indoor) | 100 kbps | Mesh |
| **NFC / RFID** | ISO/IEC | 13.56 MHz | < 10 cm | 424 kbps | Point-to-point |

#### 2.2.2 Long-Range Non-3GPP (LPWAN)

| Technology | Modulation | Spectrum | Range | Battery Life | Payload |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LoRa (LoRaWAN)** | Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) | **868 / 915 MHz (ISM)** | **2–15 km** | 10+ years | 51–242 bytes |
| **Sigfox** | UNB BPSK (Ultra-Narrow Band) | 868 / 915 MHz | **10–50 km** | 10+ years | 12 bytes (UL) |
| **Ingenu (RPMA)** | RPMA DSSS | 2.4 GHz | 15 km | 10 years | 19 bytes |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are HIGH-YIELD for KTU University Examination problems. Memorize all units and boundary conditions.**

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$ | $d$ in **km**, $f$ in **MHz**, result in **dB** |
| **Received Power (Friis Eq.)** | $$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$ | $P_t, P_r$ in W, $\lambda$ in m, $d$ in m |
| **SNR at Receiver** | $$\mathrm{SNR} = \dfrac{P_r}{N_0 B}$$ | $N_0$ in W/Hz, $B$ in Hz, dimensionless ratio |
| **Shannon-Hartley Capacity** | $$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \mathrm{SNR}\right)$$ | $C$ in bps, $B$ in Hz |
| **Link Budget Equation** | $$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{total}$$ | All in **dB / dBm / dBi** |
| **Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL)** | $$MCL = P_{tx} - S_{sens} - M_{fade}$$ | $P_{tx}$ in dBm, $S_{sens}$ in dBm, $M_{fade}$ in dB |
| **Battery Life Estimation** | $$T_{bat} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}}$$ | $C_{bat}$ in mAh, $I_{avg}$ in mA, $T$ in hours |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture (Kerala Case Study)**: A **coconut plantation in Thrissur** may deploy **LoRa soil moisture sensors** (Non-3GPP) because the plantation is in a rural zone without guaranteed cellular coverage. The **cost per sensor is ₹800** versus ₹2,500 for a 3GPP modem.
- **E-KYC Smart Cards (Aadhaar-based)**: Uses **NFC (Non-3GPP)** for < 10 cm secure handshake.
- **Smart Electricity Meters in Kerala (KSEB)**: KSEB has piloted **NB-IoT (3GPP)** smart meters in Kochi and Thiruvananthapuram for remote meter reading and outage detection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) — **Most Repeated KTU Derivative**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks for the **derivation of FSPL** and its application in IoT link budget. This is a guaranteed question worth **3–7 marks**.

#### Step 1: Start with the Isotropic Radiator

An **isotropic antenna** radiates electromagnetic energy uniformly in all directions. The power density at distance $d$ is:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

Here, $P_t$ is the **transmitted power** (W), and the surface area of a sphere of radius $d$ is $4\pi d^2$.

#### Step 2: Account for Real Antenna Gain

A real receiving antenna has an **effective aperture** $A_e$ related to its gain $G_r$ and wavelength $\lambda$ by:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

The received power is the product of power density and effective aperture:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

#### Step 3: Substitute Wavelength in Terms of Frequency

We use the wave relation $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r c^2}{(4\pi d f)^2}$$

#### Step 4: Define Path Loss as $L = P_t / P_r$ (Assumed Isotropic, $G_t = G_r = 1$)

$$L = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \frac{(4\pi d f)^2}{c^2}$$

#### Step 5: Convert to Decibels (dB)

Take $10 \log_{10}$ on both sides:

$$L_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

#### Step 6: Evaluate the Constant Term for $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz

$$\frac{4\pi}{c} = \frac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 4.19 \times 10^{-8}$$

In dB: $20\log_{10}(4.19 \times 10^{-8}) \approx -147.55$

When $d$ is converted to km ($d_{km} = d_{m}/1000$) and $f$ to MHz ($f_{MHz} = f_{Hz}/10^6$), the constant reduces to **32.44** (with $-3$ dB correction from km/m and $-6$ dB from MHz/Hz, giving $-147.55 - 3 - 6 = -156.55$... classical derivation gives the **standard 32.44** form as follows).

The standard KTU board formula is:

$$\boxed{FSPL_{(dB)} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: NB-IoT Link Budget (Kerala Smart Meter Scenario)

> **Problem Statement**: An NB-IoT smart meter operates at $f = 900$ MHz, transmits with $P_t = 23$ dBm, has transmit and receive antenna gains of $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, and the receiver sensitivity is $S_{sens} = -129$ dBm. The required maximum coupling loss is **MCL = 164 dB**. Determine the maximum allowable distance.

**Step 1: Required Free-Space Path Loss** (conservative, no cable loss)

$$L_{FSPL} = MCL = 164 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into FSPL formula**

$$164 = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(900)$$

**Step 3: Compute the frequency term**

$$20\log_{10}(900) = 20 \times 2.9542 = 59.08 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4: Solve for the distance term**

$$20\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 164 - 32.44 - 59.08 = 72.48 \text{ dB}$$

$$\log_{10}(d_{km}) = \frac{72.48}{20} = 3.624$$

**Step 5: Exponentiate**

$$d_{km} = 10^{3.624} \approx 4206 \text{ km}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **unrealistically large** distance is theoretical (free-space). In real deployments, urban clutter, foliage, and building penetration losses reduce this to **3–15 km** practical range. **KTU board answer: 4,206 km (free-space) but state the realistic figure is 3–15 km.** *(Always quote both — full marks guaranteed.)*

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: 3GPP vs Non-3GPP Selection Criteria

| Application | Best 3GPP Fit | Best Non-3GPP Fit | Selection Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Smart Electricity Meter** | **NB-IoT (3GPP)** | LoRa (Non-3GPP) | NB-IoT: licensed interference-free; LoRa: cheaper but interference-prone |
| **Wearable Fitness Band** | LTE-M (3GPP) | **BLE 5.0 (Non-3GPP)** | BLE: ultra-low cost, no SIM fee, low power |
| **Industrial Robot Control** | **5G URLLC (3GPP)** | Wi-Fi 6 (Non-3GPP) | URLLC: 1 ms latency, 99.999% reliability |
| **Smart Home Light Bulb** | LTE-M (3GPP) | **Zigbee / BLE (Non-3GPP)** | Zigbee: mesh self-healing, no monthly SIM cost |
| **Vehicle Fleet Tracking** | **LTE-M (3GPP)** | LoRa (Non-3GPP) | LTE-M: supports handover between cell towers |
| **Forest Fire Sensor** | NB-IoT (3GPP) | **Sigfox (Non-3GPP)** | Both viable; Sigfox lower cost but smaller payload |

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between 3GPP and Non-3GPP IoT standards. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | 3GPP | Non-3GPP |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Standardized cellular IoT under 3GPP partnership | Wireless IoT outside 3GPP ecosystem |
| **Spectrum** | **Licensed** (exclusive) | **Unlicensed (ISM bands)** |
| **Examples** | **NB-IoT, LTE-M, 5G NR** | **LoRa, Sigfox, Wi-Fi, Zigbee** |
| **Coverage** | Operator-deployed nationwide | Privately deployed local networks |
| **Reliability** | High (managed QoS) | Variable (interference-prone) |

*(Note: 3GPP needs licensed spectrum and SIM; Non-3GPP uses free ISM bands.)*

**Allocation**: 1 mark definition + 1 mark spectrum difference + 1 mark examples.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List the three deployment modes of NB-IoT. Mention the bandwidth of NB-IoT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

NB-IoT operates in three modes:
1. **In-band** — deployed within an existing LTE carrier (1 mark)
2. **Guard-band** — deployed in the unused guard spectrum of LTE (1 mark)
3. **Standalone** — deployed in refarmed GSM spectrum (1 mark)

**Bandwidth = 180 kHz** (a single LTE resource block).

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 2 — Internal Choice Pattern (Q. from any ONE choice)**

---

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of NB-IoT with its deployment modes and protocol stack. Discuss how NB-IoT achieves a maximum coupling loss of 164 dB. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare LTE-M and NB-IoT across the following parameters: bandwidth, peak data rate, mobility support, device density, battery life, and target applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📖 Model Solution for Question A

**(a) NB-IoT Architecture, Deployment Modes, and MCL Achievement (7 marks)**

**Architecture (2 marks):**

NB-IoT reuses the LTE protocol stack but optimizes each layer for low power and narrowband operation. The protocol stack consists of:

- **Physical Layer (PHY)**: Operates on 180 kHz bandwidth using QPSK modulation for uplink (single-tone or multi-tone) and QPSK/16-QAM for downlink.
- **MAC Layer**: Simplified scheduling; supports up to 128 HARQ processes.
- **RLC/PDCP Layer**: Supports *Data Radio Bearers* optimized for small infrequent packets.
- **RRC Layer**: Implements *Power Save Mode (PSM)* and *eDRX* (extended Discontinuous Reception) cycles up to **~3 hours**.
- **NAS Layer**: Manages *control plane CIoT EPS optimization* for sending small IP/Non-IP packets over control-plane signaling without data-plane setup.

**Three Deployment Modes (3 marks):**

| Mode | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **In-band** | NB-IoT occupies 1 PRB inside an LTE carrier's 10 MHz band | Coexistence with LTE traffic |
| **Guard-band** | NB-IoT uses the 100 kHz unused guard between LTE and adjacent carrier | No interference with LTE |
| **Standalone** | NB-IoT operates in refarmed 2G GSM 900 MHz spectrum | Dedicated IoT-only band |

**How 164 dB MCL is Achieved (2 marks):**

- **Repetition coding**: Uplink transmissions are repeated up to **128 times** to combat deep fading.
- **Single-tone transmission**: Concentrates power into a narrow 3.75 kHz or 15 kHz subcarrier.
- **Relaxed receiver sensitivity**: NB-IoT devices decode signals at -129 dBm (vs. -100 dBm for LTE).
- **Reduced bandwidth**: Narrow 180 kHz band raises processing gain and link budget.
- **Net result**: 20 dB improvement over standard LTE MCL of 144 dB.

---

**(b) LTE-M vs NB-IoT Comparison (7 marks)**

| Parameter | LTE-M (Cat-M1) | NB-IoT (Cat-NB1) |
|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth** | 1.4 MHz | **180 kHz** |
| **Peak Data Rate (DL)** | ~300 kbps | ~26 kbps |
| **Peak Data Rate (UL)** | ~375 kbps | ~62 kbps (multi-tone) |
| **Mobility** | **Full handover support** | No handover (stationary only) |
| **Device Density** | ~50,000/cell | ~50,000/cell |
| **Battery Life** | 5–10 years | **Up to 10+ years** |
| **Voice Support** | **Yes (VoLTE)** | No (data only) |
| **Target Apps** | Asset tracking, wearables, connected vehicles | Smart meters, parking sensors, environmental monitoring |
| **Cost of Modem** | Moderate | **Lowest (~$3–5)** |

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the LoRaWAN architecture. Discuss LoRa modulation, the chirp spread spectrum technique, and the role of Spreading Factor (SF7 to SF12) in range-vs-data-rate trade-off. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IoT gateway is deployed at frequency 868 MHz, transmitted power 14 dBm, antenna gains 2 dBi each, and receiver sensitivity -130 dBm. Compute the free-space path loss and the maximum theoretical range in km. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📖 Model Solution for Question B

**(a) LoRaWAN Architecture and Chirp Spread Spectrum (7 marks)**

**LoRaWAN Architecture (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[End Devices] --> B[LoRa Gateway]
    B --> C[Network Server]
    C --> D[Application Server]
    classDef node fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    class A,B,C,D node
```

- **End Devices**: Sensors/actuators transmitting LoRa-modulated packets via ISM bands (868 MHz EU, 915 MHz US, 433 MHz Asia).
- **Gateways (Concentrators)**: Receive all LoRa packets and forward them to the Network Server via IP backhaul (Ethernet/4G/Satellite). They are **transparent bridges** — no MAC-layer processing.
- **Network Server (LNS)**: Performs **deduplication, security (NwkSKey), Adaptive Data Rate (ADR), and routing** to the right application.
- **Application Server**: Interprets payload data and forwards to end-user dashboards.

**LoRa Modulation — Chirp Spread Spectrum (2 marks):**

LoRa uses **CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum)** where the carrier frequency linearly sweeps from $f_{min}$ to $f_{max}$ across the channel bandwidth $B$. The instantaneous frequency is:

$$f(t) = f_c + \frac{B}{T_s} \left(t - T_s/2\right), \quad 0 \le t \le T_s$$

A *down-chirp* sweeps high-to-low; an *up-chirp* sweeps low-to-high. The receiver correlates the incoming chirp with a known replica — even at very low SNR (down to **-20 dB**).

**Spreading Factor Trade-off (2 marks):**

$$R_s = \frac{B}{2^{SF}}, \quad SF \in \{7,8,9,10,11,12\}$$

- Higher SF → Lower symbol rate → **Longer range, higher sensitivity, longer airtime**
- Lower SF → Higher data rate → **Shorter range**

| SF | Bit Rate (for 125 kHz BW) | Sensitivity | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| SF7 | 5.5 kbps | -123 dBm | Shortest |
| SF10 | 980 bps | -132 dBm | Mid |
| SF12 | 250 bps | -137 dBm | **Longest** |

---

**(b) Free-Space Path Loss and Maximum Range (7 marks)**

**Given:** $f = 868$ MHz, $P_t = 14$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, $S_{sens} = -130$ dBm.

**Step 1: Compute Maximum Allowable Path Loss (MCL)**

$$MCL = P_t + G_t + G_r - S_{sens} = 14 + 2 + 2 - (-130) = 148 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2: Apply FSPL formula with $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz**

$$148 = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(868)$$

**Step 3: Evaluate $20\log_{10}(868)$**

$$20\log_{10}(868) = 20 \times 2.9385 = 58.77 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4: Solve for distance term**

$$20\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 148 - 32.44 - 58.77 = 56.79 \text{ dB}$$

$$\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 2.8395$$

**Step 5: Compute the range**

$$d_{km} = 10^{2.8395} \approx 691.1 \text{ km}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The free-space theoretical range is **~691 km** — practically impossible. Real LoRa deployments in urban Kerala report **2–5 km** and in line-of-sight rural Kerala **up to 15 km**, due to terrain, vegetation, and building penetration losses (often 25–40 dB extra). **Always state both: theoretical (691 km) and practical (2–15 km).**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Most students lose 1–2 marks by giving only the theoretical range** (e.g., 691 km) without qualifying that this is free-space and the real-world range is 2–15 km. **Always state both and mention additional propagation losses.**
> 2. **Unit mismatches in FSPL** are the #1 cause of marks lost. The constant 32.44 is valid **only** for $d$ in **km** and $f$ in **MHz**. If $d$ is in meters or $f$ in GHz, the constant changes to **-27.55**.
> 3. **Students often confuse NB-IoT bandwidth (180 kHz) with LTE bandwidth (1.4 MHz for LTE-M, 20 MHz for full LTE).** Memorize all three.
> 4. **3GPP vs Non-3GPP table in 3-mark questions** must list *both* licensed vs unlicensed spectrum AND give 2 examples — examiners often check exactly this.
> 5. **LoRa vs LoRaWAN confusion**: LoRa is the **physical layer modulation (CSS)**; LoRaWAN is the **MAC/network protocol** by the LoRa Alliance. Same way Wi-Fi is the technology, IEEE 802.11 is the standard.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 60-Second Memory Refresh**

- ⭐ **3GPP** = Cellular, licensed spectrum, NB-IoT (180 kHz), LTE-M (1.4 MHz), 5G NR.
- ⭐ **Non-3GPP** = Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Zigbee, LoRa, Sigfox; uses unlicensed **ISM bands** (433/868/915 MHz, 2.4/5/6 GHz).
- ⭐ **NB-IoT deployment modes**: **In-band / Guard-band / Standalone** — three modes, 180 kHz each.
- ⭐ **NB-IoT MCL = 164 dB** (20 dB better than LTE) via repetition coding + narrowband.
- ⭐ **LTE-M supports handover**; **NB-IoT does not** (stationary devices only).
- ⭐ **5G NR three profiles**: eMBB, mMTC, URLLC (1 ms latency, 99.999% reliability).
- ⭐ **LoRa modulation = CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum)**; SF7 (fastest) to SF12 (longest range).
- ⭐ **LoRaWAN architecture**: End Device → Gateway → Network Server → Application Server.
- ⭐ **FSPL formula**: $32.44 + 20\log(d_{km}) + 20\log(f_{MHz})$ — memorize as it is.
- ⭐ **Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL)** = $P_{tx} - S_{sens} - M_{fade}$ (all dB/dBm).
- ⭐ **Sigfox uses UNB BPSK** with 12-byte payload — ultra-narrowband, not spread spectrum.
- ⭐ **Bluetooth LE 5.0 range** = ~200 m (4× improvement over BLE 4.2).
- ⭐ **Zigbee topology** = Mesh (IEEE 802.15.4, 2.4 GHz, 250 kbps).
- ⭐ **5G URLLC latency** = < 1 ms (mission-critical IoT).
- ⭐ **Real-world NB-IoT range** = 3–15 km; **LoRa** = 2–15 km; theoretical FSPL values are always overestimated.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Various Protocols like Sigfox

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, **Low-Power Wide-Area Network (LPWAN)** protocols are a family of wireless communication technologies designed to enable long-range, low-bit-rate, and energy-efficient data transmission for massive-scale IoT deployments. **Sigfox** is a proprietary Ultra-Narrow Band (UNB) LPWAN technology that operates in the sub-GHz ISM (Industrial, Scientific, and Medical) bands and is optimised for sending small, infrequent messages (typically **12 bytes** uplink and **8 bytes** downlink) over distances of **3–10 km** in urban and **30–50 km** in rural environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to **compare Sigfox, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, and LTE-M** with respect to modulation, data rate, range, power consumption, payload size, security, and deployment cost. Direct definitional questions on Sigfox are regularly asked for **3 marks**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Protocol Stack Classification**
> LPWAN protocols sit at the **Data Link (Layer 2)** and partially at the **Network (Layer 3)** of the OSI model. They do **not** replace IP-based networking; rather, they complement short-range protocols (BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi) for backhaul-style, low-throughput telemetry.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a logistics company that has **10,000 shipping containers** scattered across a country. Each container only needs to report **two things** every few hours: *"Where am I?"* (GPS coordinates) and *"Am I sealed?"* (a 1-bit tamper status).

- **Wi-Fi / Bluetooth** → Like hiring a courier to deliver a personal letter. Fast, but each container needs its own Wi-Fi hotspot and battery — impossible at scale.
- **Cellular 4G/5G** → Like using a phone call to say *"I'm fine."* Overkill — huge bandwidth, expensive SIM, drains battery in hours.
- **Sigfox / LoRaWAN / NB-IoT** → Like using a **walkie-talkie on a dedicated, long-range frequency**, where each device "chirps" a tiny coded blip every few minutes. The base station tower can hear **millions** of these devices simultaneously, batteries last **10+ years**, and the airtime cost is fractions of a cent.

**Sigfox, specifically**, is like a *telegraph* — extremely short messages (max 12 bytes), sent ultra-slowly with very narrow bandwidth, but heard across continents. It trades bandwidth for **extreme range and battery life**.

---

## 1.3 The IoT Connectivity Landscape — Where LPWAN Sits

| Tier | Range | Data Rate | Power | Example Protocols |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-Range PAN/LAN** | < 100 m | High | Low | BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi, Thread |
| **Mesh / Mid-Range** | 100 m – 1 km | Medium | Medium | Z-Wave, Mesh Bluetooth |
| **Cellular (eMBB)** | km – tens of km | Very High | High | 4G LTE, 5G |
| **LPWAN** | **2 – 50 km** | **Low (0.3 – 200 kbps)** | **Very Low** | **Sigfox, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, LTE-M** |

---

## 1.4 Why Sigfox is a "Protocol Comparison" Topic in KTU

KTU Module 2 emphasizes that **no single IoT protocol fits all use cases**. The exam routinely asks students to produce a **comparison table** and justify the choice of protocol for a given scenario (e.g., smart agriculture vs. asset tracking vs. smart metering). Sigfox is the **canonical proprietary LPWAN reference**, always contrasted with the open LoRaWAN and the 3GPP-standardised NB-IoT/LTE-M.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember the Big Four LPWAN Protocols: "SLoN-L"**
> - **S** — Sigfox (UNB, proprietary)
> - **Lo** — LoRaWAN (CSS chirp spread spectrum, open)
> - **N** — NB-IoT (Narrowband IoT, 3GPP Release 13)
> - **L** — LTE-M (Long Term Evolution for Machines, 3GPP)

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| ISM Sub-GHz Bands | **868 MHz (EU)**, **915 MHz (US)**, **433 MHz (Asia)** |
| Sigfox Payload (UL/DL) | **12 bytes / 8 bytes** |
| Max messages per day (Sigfox) | **140 uplink, 4 downlink** |
| LoRaWAN payload | up to **243 bytes** |
| NB-IoT bandwidth | **180 kHz** (single LTE resource block) |
| LTE-M bandwidth | **1.4 MHz** |
| Typical LPWAN battery life | **5 – 15 years** on **2.4 Ah** cell |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of the Major LPWAN Protocols

### 2.1.1 Sigfox

**Modulation:** Ultra-Narrow Band (UNB) — Differential Binary Phase-Shift Keying (DBPSK) uplink; Gaussian Frequency-Shift Keying (GFSK) downlink.

**Spectrum:** Operates in **unlicensed ISM sub-GHz bands** — 868 MHz (Europe), 915 MHz (North America), 433 MHz (Asia). Bandwidth per channel is only **100–600 Hz** (extremely narrow).

**Topology:** **Star topology** — end devices talk directly to Sigfox base stations; no meshing, no hand-over.

**Key Mechanism:** Each message is transmitted **three times** on **three randomly chosen frequencies** to combat interference. The base station selects the cleanest copy.

**Duty Cycle Regulation:** EU regulations cap transmissions at **1% duty cycle** (36 seconds per hour), which is *why* only **140 uplink messages/day** are allowed.

**Why it works:** Narrow bandwidth → high receiver sensitivity (~**−137 dBm**) → long range with low power.

---

### 2.1.2 LoRaWAN

**Modulation:** **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** — patented by Semtech. Spreading factor (SF) varies from **SF7 to SF12**; higher SF = longer range but lower data rate.

**Spectrum:** Same unlicensed ISM bands as Sigfox.

**Topology:** **Star-of-stars** — end nodes → gateways → network server.

**Key Mechanism:** Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) allows the server to optimise SF and transmit power per device.

**Classes:**
- **Class A** (default, lowest power, downlink only after uplink)
- **Class B** (synchronised downlink slots)
- **Class C** (always listening, highest power)

**LoRa vs LoRaWAN:** **LoRa** is the physical layer (modulation); **LoRaWAN** is the MAC/network layer (open standard by LoRa Alliance).

---

### 2.1.3 NB-IoT (Narrowband IoT)

**Modulation:** QPSK (Quadrature Phase-Shift Keying) downlink; BPSK or QPSK uplink.

**Spectrum:** **Licensed** cellular spectrum (uses one LTE resource block = 180 kHz).

**3GPP Standard:** Released in **3GPP Release 13 (2016)**. Deployed *in-band*, *guard-band*, or *standalone*.

**Topology:** Cellular — devices connect to operator's evolved NodeB (eNB) via LTE infrastructure.

**Key Mechanism:** **Repetition coding** — bits are repeated up to 128× to ensure reception at very low SINR (down to **−15 dB**).

**Key Feature:** Supports **IP-based packets** and optional **non-IP data delivery (NIDD)** for ultra-low overhead.

---

### 2.1.4 LTE-M (Long-Term Evolution for Machines, aka Cat-M1)

**Modulation:** 16-QAM, QPSK — same as standard LTE.

**Spectrum:** **Licensed** cellular, bandwidth **1.4 MHz**.

**3GPP Standard:** 3GPP Release 13.

**Key Feature vs NB-IoT:** Supports **voice (VoLTE)**, **mobility (handover)**, and **higher data rates (~1 Mbps)**.

**Best for:** Wearables, vehicle telematics, asset tracking with mobility.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Decision

| Design Decision | Reason |
|---|---|
| Sigfox uses UNB (100 Hz) | Smaller noise floor → higher sensitivity → longer range |
| LoRaWAN uses CSS | Robust against multipath fading, Doppler, interference |
| NB-IoT uses repetition coding | Cellular towers can reuse existing infrastructure; repetition overcomes low SNR |
| LTE-M has 1.4 MHz bandwidth | Enough to support mobility handovers and VoLTE |
| All use sub-GHz bands | Lower path loss; better building penetration than 2.4 GHz |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE:** In all tables below, the vertical bar `|` is replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table syntax.

### 2.3.1 Shannon–Hartley Theorem (Foundation for LPWAN Capacity)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C$ = channel capacity (bps), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (linear).

**LPWAN Insight:** Sigfox deliberately minimises $B$ to **100 Hz**, which *reduces capacity* but *raises sensitivity* by reducing the noise floor $N$. LoRaWAN trades time (longer chirps) for the same effect.

---

### 2.3.2 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL (dB)} &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $d$ = distance (metres), $f$ = frequency (Hz), $c$ = $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

---

### 2.3.3 Link Budget

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{RX} &= P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{path} + G_{RX}
\end{aligned}
$$

For successful reception, $P_{RX} \geq \text{Sensitivity}$.

---

### 2.3.4 Battery Life Approximation

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{battery} &= \frac{C_{battery} \cdot V}{I_{avg} \cdot V}
\end{aligned}
$$

A simplified estimate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{battery} \ (\text{years}) &\approx \frac{C_{battery} \ (\text{Ah})}{I_{sleep} \ (\mu A) \cdot 24 \cdot 365 / 10^{6}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 2.3.5 Spreading Factor vs Bit Rate (LoRaWAN)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_b &= \frac{SF \cdot \frac{BW}{2^{SF}}}{CR}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R_b$ = bit rate (bps), $SF$ = spreading factor (7–12), $BW$ = bandwidth (Hz, typically 125 kHz), $CR$ = coding rate (1–4).

---

## 2.4 Master Comparison Matrix (The Most Important Table)

| Parameter | **Sigfox** | **LoRaWAN** | **NB-IoT** | **LTE-M (Cat-M1)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Owner / Standard** | Sigfox (proprietary) | LoRa Alliance (open) | 3GPP Rel-13 | 3GPP Rel-13 |
| **Modulation** | UNB (DBPSK UL, GFSK DL) | Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) | QPSK / BPSK | QPSK / 16-QAM |
| **Spectrum** | Unlicensed ISM sub-GHz | Unlicensed ISM sub-GHz | Licensed LTE | Licensed LTE |
| **Bandwidth** | **100 Hz** | 125 kHz / 250 kHz | **180 kHz** | **1.4 MHz** |
| **Data Rate** | **100–600 bps** | 0.3–50 kbps | **< 200 kbps** | **< 1 Mbps** |
| **Range (urban)** | 3–10 km | 2–8 km | 1–5 km | 1–5 km |
| **Range (rural)** | 30–50 km | 10–20 km | 10–25 km | 10–20 km |
| **Max Payload** | **12 B UL / 8 B DL** | **243 B** | **1600 B** | **~ tens of KB** |
| **Messages/day** | **140 UL / 4 DL** | Unlimited (fair use) | Unlimited | Unlimited |
| **Topology** | Star | Star-of-stars | Cellular star | Cellular with handover |
| **Power Consumption** | **Very Low** | Low | Medium | Medium-High |
| **Battery Life** | **10–20 years** | 8–15 years | 5–10 years | 3–7 years |
| **Mobility Support** | None (stationary) | Limited | Limited | **Full handover** |
| **Security** | AES-128 (light) | AES-128 (app + network) | 3GPP LTE security (AES-256) | 3GPP LTE security |
| **Roaming** | Sigfox Cloud | LoRaWAN backend federation | Operator roaming | Operator roaming |
| **Cost (module)** | ~ $2–5 | ~ $3–10 | ~ $8–15 | ~ $10–20 |
| **Subscription** | Per device/year (managed) | Free (private network) | SIM-based (operator) | SIM-based (operator) |
| **Voice Support** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (VoLTE) |
| **Best Use Cases** | Smart metering, asset tracking | Agriculture, smart cities | Smart cities, metering | Wearables, telematics |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture (LoRaWAN preferred):** Open standard, large payload fits soil moisture + temp + humidity.
- **National Smart Meter Roll-out (NB-IoT preferred):** Reuses operator LTE towers; licensed spectrum = no interference.
- **Moving Vehicle Telematics (LTE-M mandatory):** Needs handover across cell towers.
- **Static, Ultra-Low-Cost Sensor (Sigfox):** Cheapest module, longest battery, simplest deployment.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Decision Rule:**
> *Licensed spectrum* → NB-IoT / LTE-M.
> *Unlicensed + open standard* → LoRaWAN.
> *Unlicensed + cheapest + smallest payload* → Sigfox.
> *Mobility + voice* → LTE-M.
