# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why Sigfox Achieves Extreme Range — Sensitivity from Bandwidth

**Goal:** Show that reducing bandwidth $B$ reduces noise floor and improves receiver sensitivity.

The thermal noise power (Johnson–Nyquist noise) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= k \cdot T \cdot B
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k$ = **Boltzmann's constant** $= 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T$ = temperature in Kelvin (≈ 290 K at room temperature), $B$ = bandwidth in Hz.

In dBm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{dBm}} &= -174 + 10 \log_{10}(B)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** For Sigfox with $B = 100$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{dBm}} &= -174 + 10 \log_{10}(100) \\
              &= -174 + 20 \\
              &= -154 \ \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** For LoRaWAN with $B = 125{,}000$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{dBm}} &= -174 + 10 \log_{10}(125{,}000) \\
              &= -174 + 50.97 \\
              &= -123.03 \ \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For NB-IoT with $B = 180{,}000$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{dBm}} &= -174 + 10 \log_{10}(180{,}000) \\
              &= -174 + 52.55 \\
              &= -121.45 \ \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Sigfox's noise floor is **~30 dB lower** than LoRaWAN's, which directly translates to **~30 dB better sensitivity**, equating to **~30× longer range** under the same transmit power. **This is the single most important conceptual reason Sigfox is long-range.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Free-Space Path Loss Comparison at 868 MHz

**Goal:** Compare the path loss of Sigfox vs LoRaWAN to predict range.

Using FSPL at $f = 868$ MHz $= 8.68 \times 10^{8}$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL}(d) &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the constant term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right) &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^{8}}\right) \\
                                        &= 20 \log_{10}(4.19 \times 10^{-8}) \\
                                        &= -147.56 \ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute frequency term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 \log_{10}(8.68 \times 10^{8}) &= 178.77 \ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Total FSPL at distance $d$ (km):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL}(d) &= 31.21 + 20 \log_{10}(d) \quad \text{(dB, with } d \text{ in km)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Table of FSPL values.

| Distance $d$ | FSPL (dB) |
|---|---|
| 1 km | 31.2 |
| 5 km | 45.2 |
| 10 km | 51.2 |
| 20 km | 57.2 |
| 50 km | 65.2 |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Achievable Range from Link Budget

**Link Budget Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{RX} &= P_{TX} + G_{TX} - \text{FSPL} + G_{RX}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Given (typical Sigfox device):**

- $P_{TX}$ = **+14 dBm** (25 mW)
- $G_{TX}$ = **2 dBi**
- $G_{RX}$ = **6 dBi**
- Receiver sensitivity = **−137 dBm**

**Step 1:** Maximum allowable path loss:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL}_{\max} &= P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - \text{Sensitivity} \\
                 &= 14 + 2 + 6 - (-137) \\
                 &= 159 \ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for distance $d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
159 &= 31.21 + 20 \log_{10}(d) \\
20 \log_{10}(d) &= 127.79 \\
\log_{10}(d) &= 6.39 \\
d &= 10^{6.39} \approx 2.45 \times 10^{6} \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to km:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &\approx 245 \ \text{km} \quad \text{(free-space, LOS, ideal conditions)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** In practice, with **obstacles, fading, and margin**, the achievable urban range is roughly 1/5 of free-space:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{urban}} &\approx 50 \ \text{km} \quad \text{(theoretical max)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Real deployments observe **3–10 km** in urban and **30–50 km** in rural — the difference is the **link margin** used to overcome multipath, foliage, and building penetration losses.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Battery Life for a Sigfox Device

**Assumptions:**
- Battery capacity $C = 2400$ mAh (2.4 Ah)
- Average current consumption $I_{\text{avg}} = 10 \ \mu A$ (mostly sleep + 6 transmissions/day)
- Device voltage $V = 3.6$ V (nominal)

**Step 1:** Convert battery capacity to coulombs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= C \cdot 3600 \\
  &= 2.4 \times 3600 \\
  &= 8640 \ \text{C (Coulombs)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Average current drawn per second.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg}} &= 10 \ \mu A = 10 \times 10^{-6} \ A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Total operation time in seconds.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{Q}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{8640}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.64 \times 10^{8} \ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to years.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{years}} &= \frac{8.64 \times 10^{8}}{365 \times 24 \times 3600} \\
                &= \frac{8.64 \times 10^{8}}{3.1536 \times 10^{7}} \\
                &\approx 27.4 \ \text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply derating factor of **~0.5** (battery self-discharge + temperature effects).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{realistic}} &\approx 13.7 \ \text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

This validates the **10–15 year** battery-life claim for Sigfox devices in product datasheets.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Protocol

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks *"Justify the choice of protocol for a given scenario."* Use this matrix-style reasoning.

| Scenario | Best Protocol | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Static smart electricity meter in dense city | **NB-IoT** | Licensed spectrum avoids interference; deep indoor penetration with repetition coding |
| Cattle tracking on a moving farm (no cell coverage) | **LoRaWAN (private gateway)** | Open standard; private gateway; long range; low cost |
| Soil moisture sensors across 500 acres, no power | **Sigfox or LoRaWAN** | Tiny payloads (4–8 bytes); multi-year battery |
| Connected smartwatch with voice calling | **LTE-M** | Only LPWAN with VoLTE + handover |
| GPS tracker on a delivery truck crossing states | **LTE-M** | Needs cellular roaming + handover |
| City-wide air quality monitor with rich data | **NB-IoT** | Larger payload (~200 bytes); licensed reliability |
| Underground parking lot sensor, deepest in-building | **NB-IoT** | Best penetration due to repetition coding |
| Single-vineyard smart irrigation (private owner) | **LoRaWAN** | Private network, no operator fees, cheap |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sigfox End-to-End Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    devA[Sigfox End Device Sensor] -->|UNB 100 Hz DBPSK| bsA[Sigfox Base Station 1]
    devB[Sigfox End Device Sensor] -->|UNB 100 Hz DBPSK| bsA
    devC[Sigfox End Device Sensor] -->|UNB 100 Hz DBPSK| bsB[Sigfox Base Station 2]
    bsA --> cloudA[Sigfox Cloud Backend]
    bsB --> cloudA
    cloudA --> apiA[Customer Application Server]
    apiA --> userA[End User Dashboard Mobile or Web]
```

**Reading Guide:**
- End devices use **battery** and **broadcast** on 3 random frequencies.
- Base stations are **receive-only (uplink)** — they only transmit during downlink acknowledgements.
- The Sigfox Cloud deduplicates the 3 redundant copies and forwards the message **once** to the customer's server via REST API/HTTP callback.

---

## 4.2 LoRaWAN Star-of-Stars Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NodeCluster1 [Field Sensors]
        n1[Soil Moisture Node SF7]
        n2[Temperature Node SF9]
        n3[Pressure Node SF12]
    end
    gw1[LoRaWAN Gateway 1]
    gw2[LoRaWAN Gateway 2]
    ntw[Network Server LNS]
    appSrv[Application Server]
    n1 -->|868 or 915 MHz| gw1
    n2 -->|868 or 915 MHz| gw1
    n3 -->|868 or 915 MHz| gw2
    gw1 -->|IP backhaul| ntw
    gw2 -->|IP backhaul| ntw
    ntw --> appSrv
```

**Reading Guide:**
- A **single message** can be received by **multiple gateways** — this is *why* LoRaWAN needs a Network Server to deduplicate.
- **SF (Spreading Factor)** is assigned per device; ADR balances range vs airtime.

---

## 4.3 NB-IoT Cellular Deployment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dev[NB-IoT Device Category NB1 or NB2] -->|180 kHz LTE Resource Block| enb[Evolved NodeB eNB on existing LTE tower]
    enb -->|S1-Lite interface| mme[Mobility Management Entity MME]
    mme --> sgw[Serving Gateway SGW]
    sgw --> pdn[Packet Data Network Gateway PGW]
    pdn --> cloud[Customer Cloud or IoT Platform]
```

**Reading Guide:**
- NB-IoT uses the **existing LTE infrastructure**; no new towers.
- The "S1-Lite" interface is an optimisation for small, infrequent packets.
- Supports **IP, non-IP, and SMS** bearers.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Layered View of All Four Protocols

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP [Application Layer]
        a1[HTTP or MQTT]
    end
    subgraph NET [Network Layer]
        n1A[Sigfox Cloud]
        n1B[LoRaWAN Server]
        n1C[NB-IoT SCEF]
        n1D[LTE-M PGW]
    end
    subgraph MAC [MAC Layer]
        m1A[Sigfox UNB MAC]
        m1B[LoRaWAN LoRaMAC]
        m1C[NB-IoT MAC based on LTE]
        m1D[LTE-M MAC based on LTE]
    end
    subgraph PHY [Physical Layer]
        p1A[DBPSK 100 Hz]
        p1B[Chirp Spread Spectrum 125 kHz]
        p1C[QPSK 180 kHz]
        p1D[QPSK and 16QAM 1.4 MHz]
    end
    APP --> NET
    NET --> MAC
    MAC --> PHY
```

---

## 4.5 Protocol Selection Flowchart for KTU Application Questions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Choose IoT Protocol] --> q1{Need licensed spectrum?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Need mobility and voice?}
    q1 -->|No| q3{Private network allowed?}

```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List three key characteristics of the Sigfox protocol and explain why its data rate is so low compared to cellular technologies.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Modulation:** Sigfox uses **Ultra-Narrow Band (UNB)** with only **100 Hz** of bandwidth per channel and **DBPSK** modulation. (1 mark)
2. **Regulatory Cap:** EU regulations enforce a **1% duty cycle**, limiting transmissions to **36 seconds/hour**, which corresponds to a maximum of **140 uplink messages/day of 12 bytes each**. (1 mark)
3. **Purpose-Built Design:** Sigfox is optimised for **tiny, infrequent, uplink-heavy telemetry** (e.g., a sensor reporting temperature every 15 minutes). High data rate is *not* required and would *increase* battery consumption. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Sigfox | LoRaWAN |
|---|---|---|
| **Modulation** | UNB (DBPSK), 100 Hz bandwidth | Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS), 125 kHz bandwidth |
| **Max Payload** | 12 bytes UL / 8 bytes DL | Up to 243 bytes |
| **Standardisation** | Proprietary (single vendor) | Open (LoRa Alliance, multi-vendor) |

(1 mark per row × 3 = 3 marks)
