*(b)* A municipal corporation plans to deploy **10,000 water meters** across a city. Each meter must send **one 8-byte packet every 4 hours**. The deployment must last **at least 10 years on a single battery**. Justify the most suitable LPWAN protocol with at least **three** technical reasons. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a): Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | Sigfox | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT | LTE-M |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Modulation | UNB-DBPSK | Chirp Spread Spectrum | QPSK / BPSK | QPSK / 16-QAM |
| Bandwidth | 100 Hz | 125 kHz | 180 kHz | 1.4 MHz |
| Data Rate | 100–600 bps | 0.3–50 kbps | < 200 kbps | < 1 Mbps |
| Spectrum | Unlicensed ISM | Unlicensed ISM | Licensed LTE | Licensed LTE |
| Max Payload | 12 B UL / 8 B DL | 243 B | 1600 B | ~ tens of KB |
| Range (urban) | 3–10 km | 2–8 km | 1–5 km | 1–5 km |
| Battery Life | 10–20 years | 8–15 years | 5–10 years | 3–7 years |
| Mobility | No | Limited | Limited | Full handover |
| Voice | No | No | No | Yes (VoLTE) |
| Standard | Proprietary | LoRa Alliance | 3GPP Rel-13 | 3GPP Rel-13 |

(Marking: 0.5 mark per correctly filled cell for 7 chosen parameters = up to 3.5 marks; plus 1 mark for table heading/structure, 1 mark for units, 1.5 marks for accuracy. Total = 7 marks.)

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b): Justification — Sigfox (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Payload Analysis:** Each packet is **8 bytes**, which is well within Sigfox's **12-byte uplink limit**. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Traffic Analysis:**
- Packets per day = $24 / 4 = 6$ packets/day.
- Per month = $6 \times 30 = 180$ packets/month.
- Per year = $\sim 2190$ packets/year.
- Over 10 years = $\sim 21{,}900$ packets/device.
- Per day per device = 6 packets, which is **way below the 140 packets/day Sigfox limit**. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Battery Life Justification:**
Using $I_{\text{avg}} \approx 10 \ \mu A$ and $C = 2.4 \ \text{Ah}$, derived earlier:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{years}} &\approx \frac{2.4 \times 3600}{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.1536 \times 10^{7}} \\
                 &\approx 27.4 \ \text{years (ideal)} \\
T_{\text{realistic}} &\approx 13.7 \ \text{years (with derating)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This comfortably exceeds the 10-year requirement. (1.5 marks)

**Step 4 — Cost Justification:**
Sigfox modules cost **\$2–\$5** per device — the lowest among the four. With 10,000 units, the module savings vs NB-IoT (~\$10) is ~ **\$50,000–\$80,000**. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Coverage Justification:**
For meters deployed **indoors in basements**, the **lower noise floor** of Sigfox (–154 dBm at 100 Hz BW) gives superior in-building penetration compared to NB-IoT. (1 mark)

**Step 6 — Operational Simplicity:**
Sigfox offers a **managed cloud service** — no need for the municipality to operate gateways (unlike LoRaWAN) or pay cellular subscriptions (unlike NB-IoT/LTE-M). (0.5 marks)

**Conclusion:** **Sigfox** is the most suitable choice based on **payload fit, ultra-low power, in-building range, lowest module cost, and zero infrastructure burden**. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**
*(a)* Explain the **physical layer modulation** of Sigfox and LoRaWAN. Derive how **bandwidth affects noise floor and receiver sensitivity** using the thermal noise equation, and show that Sigfox has a **~30 dB advantage** over LoRaWAN. (7 marks)

*(b)* An agricultural IoT deployment covers **2 km²** of farmland with sensors transmitting **30-byte packets every 10 minutes**. The owner wants **no recurring subscription**. Recommend the best protocol and design the network architecture with a block diagram. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a): Sigfox vs LoRaWAN Modulation & Sensitivity (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Sigfox Modulation (1.5 marks):**
Sigfox uses **Ultra-Narrow Band (UNB)** with **Differential Binary Phase-Shift Keying (DBPSK)** for uplink and **GFSK** for downlink. Each channel is only **100 Hz** wide. The same 12-byte payload is transmitted **three times on three different random frequencies** (frequency hopping) to combat interference and fading.

**Step 2 — LoRaWAN Modulation (1.5 marks):**
LoRaWAN uses **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** patented by Semtech. A chirp is a signal whose frequency increases or decreases linearly over time. LoRaWAN supports **Spreading Factors SF7 to SF12** and bandwidths of **125 kHz, 250 kHz, or 500 kHz**. Higher SF → longer airtime but more robust link.

**Step 3 — Thermal Noise Equation (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{dBm}} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(B)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute for Sigfox ($B = 100$ Hz): (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{dBm}} &= -174 + 10 \log_{10}(100) = -174 + 20 = -154 \ \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute for LoRaWAN ($B = 125{,}000$ Hz): (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{dBm}} &= -174 + 10 \log_{10}(125{,}000) = -174 + 50.97 = -123.03 \ \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Difference and Interpretation (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta N &= -123.03 - (-154) = 30.97 \ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **~30 dB lower noise floor** for Sigfox translates directly to a **~30 dB better receiver sensitivity**, which in free-space path loss translates to a **30× range multiplier** for the same transmit power.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b): LoRaWAN Deployment (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Protocol Justification: LoRaWAN (1 mark)**
- 30-byte payload < 243-byte LoRaWAN limit ✅
- No recurring subscription (private gateway) ✅
- Long range covers 2 km² easily ✅

**Step 2 — Network Architecture Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Soil Sensor SF7]─┐
[Temp Sensor SF9]─┼──→[LoRaWAN Gateway 8ch]──→[Private LNS]──→[Cloud Dashboard]
[Humidity SF10]──┘    (placed at center)      (local server)
```

**Step 3 — Capacity Planning (1.5 marks):**
- Sensors in 2 km²: ~**50–100 devices**
- Each device transmits **30 bytes every 10 min = 144 packets/day**
- A single **8-channel LoRaWAN gateway** can handle **>1000 packets/hour** → sufficient for the load.

**Step 4 — Coverage Justification (1.5 marks):**
At 868 MHz with SF9–SF10, a single gateway at the centre of the field provides **~3–5 km** line-of-sight range, comfortably covering the 2 km² (radius ~ 800 m).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions (Top 5 Pitfalls)**
> 1. **Writing "$|x|$" in tables** — breaks markdown and the examiner cannot find your answer. Use `\vert x \vert` or `mod x`.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — dBm ≠ dB ≠ dBi. Always annotate: "−137 dBm" not just "−137".
> 3. **Confusing LoRa vs LoRaWAN** — LoRa is the **physical layer** (Semtech proprietary); LoRaWAN is the **MAC layer** (open). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 4. **Saying Sigfox is "open" or "3GPP"** — Sigfox is **proprietary**. NB-IoT and LTE-M are 3GPP. LoRaWAN is open but **not 3GPP**.
> 5. **No justification in scenario questions** — A bare table without "because..." loses 2–3 marks. Always pair a protocol choice with **three technical reasons**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sigfox** is a **proprietary, UNB-modulated, ultra-low-power, ultra-narrow-bandwidth (100 Hz)** protocol for **tiny payloads (12 B)** and **10+ year battery life**.
- **LoRaWAN** is the **open-standard counterpart** using **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** with payloads up to **243 B** — best when you want a **private network with no subscription**.
- **NB-IoT** is the **3GPP-licensed** LPWAN option reusing **LTE towers**, with **180 kHz** bandwidth and **deep indoor penetration** via **repetition coding (up to 128×)**.
- **LTE-M (Cat-M1)** is the **only LPWAN with mobility handover and VoLTE** — pick it for **moving assets or wearables**.
- **Range rule of thumb:** Sigfox > LoRaWAN ≈ NB-IoT > LTE-M (because bandwidth rules sensitivity).
- **Battery rule:** Higher data rate or higher bandwidth → **more power drained** → **shorter battery life**.
- **Cost rule:** Sigfox modules **cheapest**, but you **pay subscription**; LoRaWAN is **free** but you **buy your own gateway**.
- **Decision cheat-code for KTU exams:** "**Static + tiny payload + no operator** → Sigfox. **Static + larger payload + private** → LoRaWAN. **Urban licensed reliability** → NB-IoT. **Mobile + voice** → LTE-M."
- **Frequency remember:** All LPWAN uses **sub-GHz ISM bands** (433 / 868 / 915 MHz) **except NB-IoT and LTE-M**, which use **licensed LTE spectrum**.
- **3GPP release:** NB-IoT and LTE-M are both **3GPP Release 13 (2016)**.
- **Noise floor formula to memorise:** $\boxed{N_{\text{dBm}} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B)}$ — this is the **single most asked formula** in LPWAN comparison questions.
- **Always mention duty-cycle regulation** (EU 1% cap) when explaining Sigfox's 140 msg/day limit.
- **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** **"S.L.O.N. Loves Long Boring Telemetry"** — Sigfox, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, LTE-M for long, low-bandwidth telemetry.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LoRa
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LoRa

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of LoRa

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**LoRa (Long Range)** is a proprietary **physical layer (PHY) modulation technique** based on **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** technology, originally developed by Cycleo and later acquired and commercialized by **Semtech Corporation**. It is the foundational radio layer that enables **Low Power Wide Area Network (LPWAN)** communication for Internet of Things (IoT) deployments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *LoRa is a spread-spectrum modulation technique derived from Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) technology. It encodes information on chirped sinusoidal signals whose instantaneous frequency varies linearly with time, enabling long-range, low-power, and highly robust communication at the cost of data throughput.*

LoRa operates in the **sub-GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM)** radio bands, which are license-free globally:
- **433 MHz** (Asia, ITU Region 1)
- **868 MHz** (Europe, ITU Region 1)
- **915 MHz** (North America, ITU Region 2)
- **2.4 GHz** (Global S2-LR variant, newer releases)

It is critical to distinguish between two often-confused terms:
- **LoRa**: The proprietary **physical layer modulation** (the radio signal itself).
- **LoRaWAN**: The **open MAC-layer protocol and network architecture** built *on top of* LoRa, maintained by the LoRa Alliance.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Whispering Across a Valley" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are standing on one side of a vast, noisy valley and you need to send a short message to a friend on the opposite hill — without using a megaphone, and powered only by a small battery that must last for years.
>
> - **Normal radio (like Wi-Fi or Bluetooth)**: You would shout the message *very fast and clearly*. The message is brief, but the shouting uses lots of energy and cannot travel very far.
> - **LoRa's approach**: You instead stretch each syllable of your message over a long period, sliding your voice up and down in pitch (a **chirp**) so it spreads across the noise floor. Even though the message takes *longer*, the signal is so robust and faint that it can travel **kilometers** while using microscopic amounts of energy — so little that a coin cell battery can power the device for **up to 10 years**.

This perfectly captures LoRa's design philosophy: **trade speed for extreme range and energy efficiency**.

## 1.3 Core Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Headline Specifications (Industrial Standard Values):**
> - **Range:** **2–5 km in urban areas**, **15+ km in rural/line-of-sight** deployments.
> - **Data Rate:** **0.3 kbps to 50 kbps** (configurable).
> - **Battery Life:** **Up to 10 years** on a single coin cell (2,400 mAh).
> - **Node Capacity:** **Millions of messages per base station** (thanks to orthogonal Spreading Factors).
> - **Receiver Sensitivity:** as low as **−137 dBm** (better than most cellular LTE signals).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)

Unlike conventional FSK or OOK modulation where frequency is held constant during a symbol, **LoRa continuously varies the frequency of the carrier signal in a linear fashion**. This linearly swept sinusoidal signal is called a **chirp**.

There are two variants of chirps in LoRa:
1. **Up-chirp**: Instantaneous frequency rises from $f_{min}$ to $f_{max}$ over the symbol period.
2. **Down-chirp**: Instantaneous frequency falls from $f_{max}$ to $f_{min}$ (used for synchronization and preamble).

The instantaneous frequency of an up-chirp is mathematically defined as:

$$
f(t) = f_{min} + \left(\frac{BW}{T_s}\right) \cdot t, \quad 0 \leq t < T_s
$$

Where:
- $BW$ = Channel Bandwidth (Hz)
- $T_s$ = Symbol Duration (seconds)
- $f_{min}$ = Lower band edge

### 2.1.1 How Information is Encoded in Chirps

A **Spreading Factor (SF)** determines how many bits are encoded into a single chirp symbol. The chirp is divided into $2^{SF}$ discrete frequency bins within the bandwidth $BW$. Each starting frequency bin uniquely represents a symbol value.

| Spreading Factor (SF) | Frequency Bins ($2^{SF}$) | Bits per Symbol |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| SF7 | 128 | 7 |
| SF8 | 256 | 8 |
| SF9 | 512 | 9 |
| SF10 | 1024 | 10 |
| SF11 | 2048 | 11 |
| SF12 | 4096 | 12 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Orthogonality Principle:** Chirps generated with different SFs appear as noise to one another. This means **multiple LoRa signals with different SFs can occupy the same channel simultaneously** without interfering — a property called **orthogonality**. This is what gives LoRa its massive network capacity.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of LoRa Configuration

The behavior of every LoRa transmitter is governed by three tunable parameters. Engineers constantly trade off between them:

### Pillar 1: Spreading Factor (SF)
- **Higher SF** → Slower data rate, longer range, better sensitivity, longer time on air.
- **Lower SF** → Faster data rate, shorter range, lower sensitivity.

### Pillar 2: Bandwidth (BW)
- Available options: **7.8 kHz, 10.4 kHz, 15.6 kHz, 20.8 kHz, 31.2 kHz, 41.7 kHz, 62.5 kHz, 125 kHz, 250 kHz, 500 kHz**
- Standard LPWAN use: **125 kHz, 250 kHz, 500 kHz**.
- **Higher BW** → Faster data rate, lower sensitivity (more noise admitted).
- **Lower BW** → Slower data rate, higher sensitivity.

### Pillar 3: Coding Rate (CR)
- Forward Error Correction (FEC) rates: **4/5, 4/6, 4/7, 4/8**.
- Denoted as $\frac{4}{4+n}$ where $n \in \{1,2,3,4\}$.
- **Higher CR** → More redundancy → Greater robustness against interference, but slower effective throughput.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery of these five equations guarantees 90% of LoRa numerical questions in KTU examinations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Symbol Rate** ($R_s$) | $R_s = \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}}$ | $BW$ in Hz; $R_s$ in symbols/sec (baud) |
| 2 | **Symbol Period** ($T_s$) | $T_s = \dfrac{2^{SF}}{BW}$ | $T_s$ in seconds |
| 3 | **Raw Bit Rate** ($R_b$) | $R_b = SF \times \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR$ | $R_b$ in bits/sec (bps) |
| 4 | **Number of Symbols** ($n_{payload}$) | $n_{payload} = 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil \dfrac{8PL - 4SF + 44}{4(SF-2)} \right\rceil, 0\right)$ | $PL$ = Payload in bytes |
| 5 | **Time on Air** ($T_{packet}$) | $T_{packet} = T_{preamble} + n_{payload} \cdot T_s$ | $T_{preamble}$ = Preamble duration (typically $T_s \cdot (4.25 + n_{preamble})$) |
| 6 | **Receiver Sensitivity** (approx.) | $S = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF + SNR_{limit}$ | $NF$ = Noise Figure (~6 dB); $SNR_{limit}$ depends on SF |
| 7 | **Maximum Link Budget** | $LB = P_{TX} - S_{RX}$ | $P_{TX}$ in dBm; $S_{RX}$ in dBm |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does LoRa matter in modern IoT?**
>
> - **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture, livestock tracking, weather stations across hundreds of square kilometers without cellular coverage.
> - **Smart Cities:** Smart streetlight control, parking sensors, waste-bin level monitoring.
> - **Asset Tracking:** Low-cost GPS-free trackers for shipping containers.
> - **Utility Metering:** Water, gas, and electricity Automatic Meter Reading (AMR).
> - **Disaster Early-Warning Systems:** Tsunami sensors, forest fire detection — where cellular infrastructure has failed or was never built.

The **trade-off triangle** every IoT engineer must internalize:

$$
\boxed{\text{Higher Range} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Higher SF} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Lower Data Rate} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Longer Time on Air} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Higher Energy per Bit}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bit Rate Equation for LoRa

The LoRa bit rate is built from three fundamental layers. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Symbol Rate from Bandwidth and Spreading Factor

A LoRa symbol occupies the entire channel bandwidth $BW$ and is divided into $2^{SF}$ discrete, equally-spaced frequency bins. Therefore, the symbol rate is the bandwidth divided by the number of possible chips per symbol:

$$
R_s = \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \quad \text{[symbols/sec]}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** Each chirp sweeps through $2^{SF}$ frequency slots; the *chip rate* equals the bandwidth because each chirp is one sweep of the entire band.

### Step 2 — Bits Encoded per Symbol

Because the symbol can start at any one of the $2^{SF}$ orthogonal frequencies, and each starting position is distinguishable at the receiver, the number of bits carried per symbol is:

$$
n_{bits/symbol} = \log_2(2^{SF}) = SF
$$

### Step 3 — Raw Bit Rate Before FEC

Multiplying the symbol rate by the bits per symbol yields the gross bit rate *before* Forward Error Correction redundancy:

$$
R_{gross} = R_s \times SF = \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times SF = \frac{SF \cdot BW}{2^{SF}}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Coding Rate (FEC Penalty)

The Coding Rate $CR$ represents the fraction of *useful* data bits out of the total bits transmitted. The effective (net) bit rate is therefore:

$$
\boxed{R_b = \frac{SF \cdot BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot CR \quad \text{[bps]}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** When $SF$ doubles (e.g., SF7 → SF8), the bit rate halves. This is the mathematical proof of the range-vs-throughput trade-off.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Symbol Period and the 2.4 GHz Case Study

Given $SF = 9$ and $BW = 125 \text{ kHz}$, find the symbol period.

### Step 1 — Apply the Symbol Period Formula

$$
T_s = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
T_s = \frac{2^{9}}{125 \times 10^{3}}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate Numerator and Denominator

$$
T_s = \frac{512}{125{,}000}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Computation

$$
T_s = 0.004096 \text{ s} = 4.096 \text{ ms}
$$

**Interpretation:** Every LoRa symbol at SF9 occupies exactly 4.096 ms. The receiver must listen for this entire duration to reliably extract energy from the chirp.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Comparative Throughput Analysis

Compare the bit rate of two configurations:
- **Configuration A:** $SF = 7$, $BW = 125 \text{ kHz}$, $CR = \dfrac{4}{5}$
- **Configuration B:** $SF = 12$, $BW = 125 \text{ kHz}$, $CR = \dfrac{4}{8}$

### Case A — Calculation

$$
R_{b,A} = \frac{7 \times 125{,}000}{2^{7}} \times \frac{4}{5}
$$

Intermediate evaluation:

$$
R_{b,A} = \frac{875{,}000}{128} \times 0.8
$$

$$
R_{b,A} = 6835.94 \times 0.8 = 5468.75 \text{ bps} \approx 5.47 \text{ kbps}
$$

### Case B — Calculation

$$
R_{b,B} = \frac{12 \times 125{,}000}{2^{12}} \times \frac{4}{8}
$$

Intermediate evaluation:

$$
R_{b,B} = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{4096} \times 0.5
$$

$$
R_{b,B} = 366.21 \times 0.5 = 183.11 \text{ bps} \approx 0.18 \text{ kbps}
$$

### Comparative Result

$$
\frac{R_{b,A}}{R_{b,B}} = \frac{5468.75}{183.11} \approx 29.87
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Configuration A is **~30 times faster** than Configuration B. However, Configuration B offers roughly **~9 dB better receiver sensitivity** (3 dB per doubling of SF), translating into dramatically increased range. This single calculation is worth its weight in gold in KTU exam halls.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: The SF vs Sensitivity vs Range Trade-off

The following table summarizes the *operational behavior* of LoRa at **$BW = 125$ kHz** (industry default):

| SF | Bit Rate (CR=4/5) | Symbol Period | SNR Limit | Sensitivity (typ.) | Relative Air-time |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 7 | 5,469 bps | 1.024 ms | −7.5 dB | −123 dBm | 1.0× |
| 8 | 3,125 bps | 2.048 ms | −10.0 dB | −126 dBm | 2.0× |
| 9 | 1,758 bps | 4.096 ms | −12.5 dB | −129 dBm | 4.1× |
| 10 | 977 bps | 8.192 ms | −15.0 dB | −132 dBm | 8.2× |
| 11 | 537 bps | 16.384 ms | −17.5 dB | −134.5 dBm | 16.4× |
| 12 | 293 bps | 32.768 ms | −20.0 dB | −137 dBm | 32.8× |

**Observation:** Sensitivity improves by approximately **2.5–3 dB per doubling of SF**. Combined with a TX power of +14 dBm, the maximum link budget approaches **151 dB** — the highest of any commercial low-power radio technology.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Receiver Sensitivity Floor Calculation

Given $BW = 125 \text{ kHz}$, $NF = 6 \text{ dB}$, $SNR_{limit} = -20 \text{ dB}$ (SF12):

### Step 1 — Thermal Noise Power

$$
N = kTB = -174 \text{ dBm/Hz} + 10\log_{10}(125{,}000)
$$

$$
N = -174 + 50.97 = -123.03 \text{ dBm}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Friis-like Sensitivity Formula

$$
S = N + NF + SNR_{limit}
$$

$$
S = -123.03 + 6 + (-20)
$$

$$
S = -137.03 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Interpretation:** A LoRa receiver at SF12 can decode a signal as weak as **−137 dBm**, which is millions of times weaker than the noise floor — a feat unachievable with conventional narrowband radios.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LoRaWAN Network Topology (Star-of-Stars Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["End Device Sensor A"]
    nodeB["End Device Sensor B"]
    nodeC["End Device Sensor C"]
    nodeD["End Device Sensor D"]
    nodeE["End Device Sensor E"]
    nodeF["End Device Sensor F"]
    nodeG["LoRaWAN Gateway Concentrator"]
    nodeH["Network Server NS"]
    nodeI["Application Server AS"]
    nodeJ["End User Dashboard / Cloud Platform"]

    nodeA -->|LoRa RF 868 915 MHz| nodeG
    nodeB -->|LoRa RF| nodeG
    nodeC -->|LoRa RF| nodeG
    nodeD -->|LoRa RF| nodeG
    nodeE -->|LoRa RF uplink| nodeG
    nodeF -->|LoRa RF uplink| nodeG
    nodeG -->|IP Backhaul TCP IP via 4G Ethernet Fiber| nodeH
    nodeH -->|TLS TCP| nodeI
    nodeI -->|HTTPS MQTT REST API| nodeJ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Insight:** LoRaWAN uses a **Star-of-Stars** topology. End-devices do not peer with each other — every chirp is broadcast to all gateways in range. The Network Server performs **de-duplication** by selecting the best packet from among all gateways that received the same transmission. This radically simplifies the MAC protocol and saves end-device battery.

---

## 4.2 LoRa Modulation Pipeline (Transmitter Side)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1["Input Data Bits"]
    in2["Whitening Scrambler"]
    in3["Forward Error Correction FEC Encoder"]
    in4["Interleaver Block Permutation"]
    in5["Gray Mapper Symbol Lookup"]
    in6["Chirp Generator Direct Digital Synthesizer"]
    in7["Up-mixer to RF Carrier"]
    in8["Power Amplifier"]
    in9["Antenna Sub-GHz"]

    in1 --> in2 --> in3 --> in4 --> in5 --> in6 --> in7 --> in8 --> in9
```

**Block-by-block rationale:**
1. **Whitening:** Removes DC bias from long strings of 0s or 1s.
2. **FEC Encoder:** Hamming-based convolutional coding for noise robustness.
3. **Interleaver:** Spreads burst errors across many symbols to defeat impulsive noise.
4. **Gray Mapper:** Maps data to the $2^{SF}$ chirp starting frequencies.
5. **Chirp Generator:** The heart of LoRa — synthesizes the linear FM chirp via a DDS (Direct Digital Synthesizer).
6. **Up-mixer / PA:** Translates the baseband chirp to the licensed-free RF carrier.

---

## 4.3 LoRa Receiver Demodulation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r1["Antenna"]
    r2["Low-Noise Amplifier LNA"]
    r3["Down-conversion Mixer"]
    r4["Anti-Alias Filter"]
    r5["ADC Sampler"]
    r6["De-chirp Correlator FFT Bank"]
    r7["Peak Detector Symbol Decision"]
    r8["De-interleaver"]
    r9["FEC Decoder Viterbi"]
    r10["De-whitening"]
    r11["Recovered Bit Stream"]

    r1 --> r2 --> r3 --> r4 --> r5 --> r6 --> r7 --> r8 --> r9 --> r10 --> r11
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The De-chirp Trick:** The receiver multiplies the incoming chirp with a *down-chirp* (a conjugate local oscillator). This converts the frequency shift into a constant frequency tone. A simple **FFT** then finds the energy peak, which directly yields the original symbol. This is mathematically equivalent to matched filtering and is why LoRa achieves such extraordinary sensitivity.

---

## 4.4 LoRa Frame / Packet Structure (LoRaWAN Class A)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["Preamble 8 to 12 Up-chirps"]
    p2["Sync Word 2 Up-chirps"]
    p3["SFD Start Frame Delimiter 2.25 Down-chirps"]
    p4["PHY Header 1 byte Implicit or Explicit"]
    p5["PHY Payload 1 to 255 bytes"]
    p6["CRC 2 bytes if CR 4 over 5 or higher"]
    p7["Downlink Window 1 RX1 Optional"]
    p8["Downlink Window 2 RX2 Optional"]

    p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 --> p5 --> p6 --> p7 --> p8
```

---

## 4.5 LoRaWAN Protocol Stack vs OSI Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    l1["Application Layer LoRaWAN App Commands"]
    l2["MAC Layer LoRaWAN Class A B C MAC Commands"]
    l3["Physical Layer LoRa CSS Modulation"]
    l4["RF Hardware Sub-GHz ISM Band"]

    l1 --> l2 --> l3 --> l4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** LoRa only defines the **Physical Layer (Layer 1)**. LoRaWAN defines the **MAC and higher layers (Layer 2 and above)**. The two terms are *not interchangeable* — this is a frequently tested conceptual trap in KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Examination Convention:** Part A tests direct, definition-level recall. Answers should be **3–4 lines** with precise terminology.

---

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Explain the LoRa physical layer modulation technique. Why is it preferred for long-range IoT communication?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

LoRa uses **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** modulation, a physical layer technique where data is encoded onto chirp signals — sinusoidal carriers whose instantaneous frequency varies linearly with time over the channel bandwidth.

The key reasons LoRa is preferred for long-range IoT communication are:
1. **High receiver sensitivity** (down to **−137 dBm**) due to spread-spectrum processing gain.
2. **Robustness against interference and multipath fading** because of wide-bandwidth chirps.
3. **Low power consumption** enabling **up to 10 years** of battery life on a coin cell.
4. **Orthogonal Spreading Factors (SF7–SF12)** allow massive device density per gateway.

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between LoRa and LoRaWAN.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | LoRa | LoRaWAN |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Layer** | Physical layer (PHY) only | MAC + Network + Application layers |
| **Ownership** | Proprietary (Semtech) | Open standard (LoRa Alliance) |
| **Function** | Defines the radio modulation (CSS chirps) | Defines the communication protocol, device classes, security, network architecture |
| **Scope** | How bits become RF chirps | How end-devices communicate with gateways and servers |

**One-line summary:** *LoRa is the radio; LoRaWAN is the language spoken over that radio.*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Convention:** Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer either OR (a) OR (b) based on the choice.

---

### QUESTION 2A

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a LoRaWAN network. Discuss the roles of end-devices, gateways, and the network server.

**(b)** A LoRa end-device is configured with **SF = 10**, **BW = 125 kHz**, and **Coding Rate = 4/7**. Compute:
   (i) The symbol rate.
   (ii) The symbol period.
   (iii) The effective bit rate.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The LoRaWAN network follows a **Star-of-Stars** topology consisting of four major entities:

**1. End-Devices (Sensors/Actuators):**
- Battery-powered or energy-harvesting sensors (e.g., temperature, soil moisture, GPS trackers).
- Transmit LoRa-modulated chirps to all gateways within radio range.
- Support three classes: **Class A** (lowest power, downlink only after uplink), **Class B** (synchronized downlink slots), and **Class C** (continuously listening — highest power).

**2. Gateways (Concentrators):**
- RF receivers that demodulate LoRa chirps and forward them via IP backhaul (4G, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, or Satellite).
- A single gateway can handle **millions of messages per day**.
- They are *transparent relays* — they do not perform device authentication.

**3. Network Server (NS):**
- The brain of the network: performs **device authentication**, **de-duplication** (selecting best packet among multiple gateways), **adaptive data rate (ADR)**, and **MAC command scheduling**.
- Manages downlink queues and security keys (NwkSKey, AppSKey).

**4. Application Server (AS):**
- Receives decrypted application payloads from the NS.
- Provides the data to end-users via dashboards, analytics platforms, or third-party integrations (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT).

**Architectural diagram** (already shown in Section 4.1 above).

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

Given: $SF = 10$, $BW = 125{,}000 \text{ Hz}$, $CR = \dfrac{4}{7}$

**(i) Symbol Rate:**

$$
R_s = \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} = \frac{125{,}000}{2^{10}} = \frac{125{,}000}{1024}
$$

$$
R_s = 122.07 \text{ symbols/sec}
$$

**(ii) Symbol Period:**

$$
T_s = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} = \frac{1024}{125{,}000}
$$

$$
T_s = 0.008192 \text{ s} = 8.192 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Effective Bit Rate:**

$$
R_b = \frac{SF \cdot BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot CR = \frac{10 \times 125{,}000}{1024} \times \frac{4}{7}
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
R_b = \frac{1{,}250{,}000}{1024} \times \frac{4}{7} = 1220.7 \times 0.5714
$$

$$
\boxed{R_b \approx 697.5 \text{ bps}}
$$

---

### QUESTION 2B

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) modulation technique used by LoRa. How does spreading factor affect range, data rate, and time on air?

**(b)** A smart agriculture deployment uses LoRa nodes at **SF = 12**, **BW = 250 kHz**, **CR = 4/8**, with a transmit power of **+14 dBm** and receiver noise figure of **6 dB**. Compute:
   (i) The raw bit rate.
   (ii) The thermal noise floor.
   (iii) The receiver sensitivity.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS):**

CSS is a spread-spectrum technique that transmits data using **chirps** — sinusoidal signals whose instantaneous frequency sweeps linearly across the entire channel bandwidth during a single symbol period.

There are two chirp types:
- **Up-chirp:** Frequency rises from $f_{min}$ to $f_{max}$.
- **Down-chirp:** Frequency falls from $f_{max}$ to $f_{min}$ (used in preamble).

**Information encoding:** The data symbol determines the *starting frequency* of the chirp. There are $2^{SF}$ possible starting frequencies, each uniquely representing one of the symbol values.

**Effect of Spreading Factor (SF) on system parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect of Increasing SF |
|:---|:---|
| **Range** | Increases (higher processing gain → better sensitivity) |
| **Data Rate** | Decreases (exponentially — bit rate $\propto 2^{-SF}$) |
| **Time on Air** | Increases (symbol period doubles per SF step) |
| **Energy per Bit** | Increases (longer airtime) |
| **Receiver Sensitivity** | Improves by ~2.5–3 dB per SF doubling |
| **Network Capacity** | Decreases (each SF reduces available orthogonal channels) |

**Why CSS works so well:**
- Spreads narrowband interference across the entire bandwidth.
- Doppler-tolerant (chirps are robust to frequency shifts).
- Resistant to multipath fading in urban environments.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

Given: $SF = 12$, $BW = 250{,}000 \text{ Hz}$, $CR = \dfrac{4}{8} = 0.5$, $NF = 6 \text{ dB}$, $P_{TX} = 14 \text{ dBm}$

**(i) Raw Bit Rate:**

$$
R_b = \frac{SF \cdot BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot CR = \frac{12 \times 250{,}000}{4096} \times 0.5
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
R_b = \frac{3{,}000{,}000}{4096} \times 0.5 = 732.42 \times 0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{R_b \approx 366.21 \text{ bps}}
$$

**(ii) Thermal Noise Floor:**

$$
N = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW)
$$

$$
N = -174 + 10\log_{10}(250{,}000)
$$

$$
N = -174 + 53.98 = -120.02 \text{ dBm}
$$

**(iii) Receiver Sensitivity:**

For SF12, the minimum required SNR is $SNR_{limit} = -20 \text{ dB}$.

$$
S_{RX} = N + NF + SNR_{limit}
$$

$$
S_{RX} = -120.02 + 6 + (-20)
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{RX} \approx -134.02 \text{ dBm}}
$$

**Bonus (Link Budget):**

$$
LB = P_{TX} - S_{RX} = 14 - (-134.02) = 148.02 \text{ dB}
$$

This 148 dB link budget is among the highest in any commercial low-power wireless technology.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up LoRa vs LoRaWAN** (lose 1–2 marks): Always clarify which layer you are discussing. LoRa is *modulation*; LoRaWAN is *protocol*.
> 2. **Formula sign errors** (lose 1–3 marks): The bit rate equation requires the coding rate as a **fraction less than 1** (e.g., $4/5 = 0.8$), not as the raw integer 4/5.
> 3. **Unit inconsistencies**: $BW$ must always be in **Hz** in the formula; converting from kHz to Hz before substitution earns full marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to explain orthogonal SFs**: When discussing network capacity, explicitly mention that SFs are orthogonal — this is a high-yield keyword.
> 5. **Missing the trade-off triangle**: Examiners love questions that test the inverse relationship between range, data rate, and time on air.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LoRa — 30-Second Revision Cheat Sheet for KTU Exams:**

- **LoRa = Physical Layer; LoRaWAN = MAC + Higher Layers.**
- **Modulation = Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** — linearly swept sinusoidal carriers.
- **Operating Bands:** 433 MHz, 868 MHz, 915 MHz (sub-GHz ISM); also 2.4 GHz.
- **Spreading Factors:** SF7 to SF12 (6 options, orthogonal to each other).
- **Standard Bandwidths:** 125 kHz, 250 kHz, 500 kHz.
- **Coding Rates:** 4/5, 4/6, 4/7, 4/8 (FEC via convolutional code + interleaver).
- **Key Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
  - Symbol Rate: $R_s = \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}}$
  - Symbol Period: $T_s = \dfrac{2^{SF}}{BW}$
  - Bit Rate: $R_b = \dfrac{SF \cdot BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot CR$
  - Sensitivity: $S = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF + SNR_{limit}$
- **Range:** ~5 km urban, ~15 km rural; up to **10 years battery life**.
- **Sensitivity Floor:** approximately **−137 dBm** at SF12 and BW=125 kHz.
- **Network Architecture:** Star-of-Stars — End Devices → Gateways → Network Server → Application Server.
- **Device Classes:** A (lowest power), B (synchronized downlinks), C (always listening).
- **Maximum Link Budget:** ~150 dB (industry-leading for LPWAN).
- **Key Trade-off:** Higher SF → Higher Range & Sensitivity → Lower Data Rate & Higher Time-on-Air.
- **Orthogonality of SFs:** Different SFs do not interfere — enables massive concurrent device support.
- **LoRaWAN Security:** Two 128-bit AES keys — **NwkSKey** (network integrity) and **AppSKey** (application payload encryption).
- **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR):** Network Server command that optimizes SF, BW, and TX power per node to maximize battery life and network capacity.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** When a numerical question gives $SF$, $BW$, and $CR$, immediately write down all three formulas ($R_s$, $T_s$, $R_b$) before substitution. Examiners reward a structured approach and penalize "magic" number jumps.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LoRaWAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** LoRaWAN # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **LoRaWAN (Long Range Wide Area Network)** is a low-power, wide-area networking protocol defined by the **LoRa Alliance** and built on top of the proprietary **LoRa (Long Range) physical layer modulation scheme** patented by Semtech Corporation. It is specifically designed to securely connect battery-operated **end devices** to regional, national, or global Internet of Things (IoT) networks over long radio communication ranges. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition to Memorize:** LoRaWAN is a **MAC (Media Access Control) layer protocol** that governs how LoRa-equipped end devices communicate with **gateways**, which in turn forward packets to a centralized **network server** over an IP backbone. It operates in the unlicensed sub-GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio bands. ### Key Standardized Terminology | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **LoRa** | The physical (PHY) layer modulation technique (Chirp Spread Spectrum) | | **LoRaWAN** | The open MAC-layer networking protocol sitting on top of LoRa | | **End Device (ED)** | The battery-powered sensor/actuator node (also called "mote") | | **Gateway (GW)** | The radio transceiver that bridges LoRa frames to IP networks (acts as a transparent bridge) | | **Network Server (NS)** | Central intelligence — handles MAC commands, deduplication, ADR, security | | **Application Server (AS)** | Handles payload decryption and forwards data to the end-user application | | **Join Server (JS)** | Manages root keys and secure device onboarding (Join procedure) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy: "The Postal Service of IoT"** Imagine you are a villager living 15 km away from the nearest city post office, and you only have a small, low-power walkie-talkie (battery operated, cannot transmit far) that you want to use to send a tiny postcard to a friend in another city. - **End Device (Your walkie-talkie):** You can only chirp loudly in a special "bird-call" frequency that travels far but carries very little information. - **Gateway (Local Post Office with a loudspeaker):** Multiple villagers within 10 km can all chirp toward the local post office. The post office simply *amplifies* the chirp and forwards it onward — it doesn't decode addresses. - **Network Server (The Central Sorting Hub):** This is the smart brain. It knows which chirp came from whom, deduplicates copies (since multiple post offices may have heard the same chirp), and decides the best chirp speed for each villager to save their batteries. - **Application Server (The Friend's Mailbox):** The actual destination that reads the postcard content. This is precisely how **LoRaWAN** works: a **"star-of-stars" topology** where battery-limited end devices chatter to multiple gateways, and all intelligence lives in the cloud-based **network server**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **ISM Band in India (IN865-867):** Carrier frequencies from **865 MHz to 867 MHz** (a 2 MHz-wide unlicensed band) - **LoRa Chirp Bandwidth (BW):** Standard values: **125 kHz, 250 kHz, 500 kHz** - **Spreading Factor (SF):** Integer values from **SF7 (fastest, shortest range) to SF12 (slowest, longest range)** - **Max MAC Payload Size:** Up to **243 bytes** (at SF7/BW125) - **Bit Rate Range:** Approximately **0.3 kbps to 50 kbps** - **Typical Range:** **2–5 km (urban)** and up to **15 km (rural/line-of-sight)** - **Duty Cycle Limit (EU/India-style regulations):** ≤ **1%** (often enforced as 36 seconds of airtime per hour per channel) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight — LoRa ≠ LoRaWAN:** A very common board-exam pitfall. **LoRa** is the physical (radio modulation) layer invented by Semtech. **LoRaWAN** is the open networking protocol on top of it. They are **NOT the same** — LoRa is the "voice," LoRaWAN is the "language and rules of conversation." # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 LoRa Modulation — Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) The physical layer of LoRa uses a patented modulation called **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)**. Unlike traditional FSK or OOK modulation, a LoRa symbol is a linear frequency sweep (a "chirp") that moves from the bottom of the channel bandwidth to the top, where the start frequency encodes the data symbol. ### 2.1.1 Why Chirp Spread Spectrum? 1. **Robustness to multipath fading** and Doppler shifts. 2. **Low power spectral density** → coexists with other ISM-band devices. 3. **High receiver sensitivity** (down to **−148 dBm**), enabling multi-km range. 4. **Orthogonal Spreading Factors** → multiple SFs can be transmitted on the same channel simultaneously without interference (this is a critical KTU concept). ## 2.2 LoRaWAN Network Architecture — Star of Stars The LoRaWAN topology is a **"star-of-stars"** (also called a *double-star*): **End Devices ⇢ Gateways ⇢ Network Server ⇢ Application Server** - **End Devices → Gateways:** Wireless LoRa (single-hop, no meshing). - **Gateways → Network Server:** Standard IP backhaul (Ethernet, 3G/4G, Wi-Fi, satellite). - **Network Server → Application Server:** Encrypted payload exchange. > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Concept:** The **gateway is dumb** — it merely forwards raw LoRa frames over IP using a protocol like **Packet Forwarder (UDP)** or **Basic Station**. All intelligence (MAC filtering, ADR, security key management, deduplication) lives in the **Network Server**. ## 2.3 LoRaWAN Device Classes The MAC specification defines three bi-directional device classes, each with different trade-offs between downlink latency and power consumption: | Class | Downlink Windows | Power Profile | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Class A** (Baseline) | Only after every uplink (2 short RX windows) | **Lowest power** | Battery sensors (temperature, meter reading) | | **Class B** (Beacon) | Scheduled downlink at fixed intervals + Class A | Moderate | Actuators, valve control | | **Class C** (Continuous) | Continuously listening except during TX | **Highest power** (mains-powered) | Streetlight control, industrial actuators | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Class A is mandatory.** All LoRaWAN devices **MUST** implement Class A. Classes B and C are optional add-ons built on top of Class A behavior. ## 2.4 Spreading Factor, Bandwidth, and Bit Rate (THE KEY FORMULA) The **bit rate** of a LoRa transmission is governed by a single, very important formula. KTU frequently asks derivations or numericals based on this. Let: - $SF$ = Spreading Factor (7 to 12) - $BW$ = Bandwidth in Hz (e.g., $125 \times 10^3$) - $CR$ = Coding Rate (1 to 4, representing $4/5, 4/6, 4/7, 4/8$) ### Bit Rate (Data Rate) Formula $$R_b = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR \quad \text{[bits per second, bps]}$$ ### Symbol Duration (Time on Air contribution per symbol) $$T_s = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} \quad \text{[seconds per symbol]}$$ ### Receiver Sensitivity (approximate empirical formula) The lower the SF and the higher the BW, the higher the bit rate but the **lower the sensitivity** (shorter range). The relationship is: $$\text{Sensitivity (dBm)} \approx -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF + SNR_{limit}$$ Where: - $-174$ dBm/Hz = thermal noise floor (physical constant) - $NF$ = receiver noise figure (typically 6 dB) - $SNR_{limit}$ = minimum SNR required to demodulate (depends on SF) | SF | SNR Limit (dB) | Sensitivity (BW=125 kHz, NF=6) | |---|---|---| | 7 | −7.5 dB | ≈ −123 dBm | | 8 | −10 dB | ≈ −126 dBm | | 9 | −12.5 dB | ≈ −129 dBm | | 10 | −15 dB | ≈ −132 dBm | | 11 | −17.5 dB | ≈ −134.5 dBm | | 12 | −20 dB | ≈ −137 dBm | ## 2.5 LoRaWAN Frame Format (PHY + MAC) A LoRaWAN packet (sent over the air) consists of: **Preamble | PHDR | PHDR_CRC | PHYPayload | CRC (optional, for SF > 6)** Where **PHYPayload** is the MAC layer payload: **MHDR (1 byte) | FHDR (7–22 bytes) | FPort (1 byte) | FRMPayload (0–N bytes) | MIC (4 bytes)** - **MHDR:** MAC header (MType + RFU + Major) - **FHDR:** DevAddr (4B) + FCtrl (1B) + FCnt (2B) + FOpts (0–15B) - **FPort:** Application port number (1–223 = application data, 224 = MAC commands, 0 = FRMPayload contains MAC commands) - **MIC:** Message Integrity Code (4-byte CMAC, AES-128 based) - **DevAddr:** 32-bit short device address (assigned by NS after Join) ## 2.6 LoRaWAN Security Architecture LoRaWAN uses two distinct AES-128 cryptographic layers: | Layer | Key | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Network Layer (Integrity)** | **NwkSKey** (Network Session Key) | Ensures source authenticity and message integrity (MIC calculation) | | **Application Layer (Confidentiality)** | **AppSKey** (Application Session Key) | Encrypts/decrypts the FRMPayload (end-to-end payload encryption) | | **Root Key (Provisioning)** | **AppKey** | Used only during Over-The-Air Activation (OTAA) to derive session keys | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Board Fact:** The **Network Server** holds NwkSKey. The **Application Server** holds AppSKey. This split means the network operator **cannot read application data** — a fundamental privacy property of LoRaWAN. ## 2.7 Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) ADR is a network-server-side mechanism that dynamically optimizes each end device's **SF, BW, and TX power** to: 1. Maximize battery life 2. Maximize network capacity 3. Maintain reliable communication If a device's packets arrive with high RSSI and SNR (i.e., the link has margin), the NS commands the device to **increase the data rate** (lower SF, higher BW, lower TX power). If packets are lost, the NS commands **decreased data rate** (higher SF, lower BW, higher TX power). > [!NOTE] > **Why ADR matters for KTU:** It is one of the most frequently asked concepts. ADR is a **server-controlled, not device-controlled, optimization.** ## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Formula / Quantity | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | $R_b$ | $SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR$ | bps | Bit rate (data rate) | | $T_s$ | $\frac{2^{SF}}{BW}$ | seconds | Symbol period | | $T_{packet}$ | $\left[ N_{preamble} + 4.25 + 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{8PL - 4SF + 28 + 16CRC - 20H}{4(SF-2DE)}\right\rceil (CR+4), 0\right) \right] \cdot T_s$ | seconds | Semtech time-on-air formula | | Sensitivity | $-174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF + SNR_{limit}$ | dBm | Thermal noise floor $+$ receiver NF $+$ modulation SNR floor | | Max Payload | Function of SF (SF7: 243B, SF12: 51B) | bytes | MAC-level maximum | | Duty Cycle | $\frac{T_{packet}}{T_{observation}} \leq 1\%$ | dimensionless | $\leq 36$ s per hour per channel (ETSI/India) | | Energy per Bit | $E_b = \frac{P_{TX} \cdot T_{packet}}{N_{bits}}$ | Joules/bit | Used in link-budget comparisons | (Where in the table above: $PL$ = payload bytes, $H=0$ (header off), $DE=1$ if low-data-rate optimization enabled, $CRC$ = 1 for SF>6.) > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** These formulas are used by IoT system architects to calculate **battery life** (e.g., "Will a 2400 mAh Li-SOCl₂ cell last 10 years transmitting every 15 minutes?"), **network capacity** (How many devices per km²?), and **link budget** feasibility. ## 2.9 Regional Parameters (KTU Frequently Asked) | Region | Band | Channels | Common in KTU Context | |---|---|---|---| | EU868 | 863–870 MHz | 8 mandatory + 1 RX2 | European deployments | | US915 | 902–928 MHz | 64 uplink + 8 downlink | North America | | **IN865** | **865–867 MHz** | **3 mandatory (865.0625, 865.4025, 865.985 MHz)** | **India — for Kerala projects** | | AS923 | 915–928 MHz | 2–16 | Asia-Pacific | | AU915 | 915–928 MHz | 64 uplink + 8 downlink | Australia | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the LoRa Bit Rate Formula **Problem Setup:** Show that for a LoRa transmission using Spreading Factor $SF$ over Bandwidth $BW$, each symbol encodes $SF$ bits. **Step 1 — Number of distinct chirp positions.** A LoRa symbol is a chirp that begins at one of $2^{SF}$ equally spaced starting frequencies within the bandwidth $BW$. The receiver detects the start frequency to recover the data. Since there are $2^{SF}$ possible start positions, each symbol carries: $$N_{bits/symbol} = \log_2(2^{SF}) = SF \text{ bits}$$ **Step 2 — Time duration of a single symbol.** The chirp sweeps the entire bandwidth $BW$ in Hz exactly once per symbol. Therefore the symbol period $T_s$ is the reciprocal of the bandwidth: $$T_s = \frac{1}{BW/2^{SF}} = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} \text{ seconds}$$ **Step 3 — Gross bit rate (uncoded).** Bit rate is bits per symbol divided by seconds per symbol: $$R_{b,\text{gross}} = \frac{SF \text{ bits}}{T_s} = \frac{SF \cdot BW}{2^{SF}} \text{ bps}$$ **Step 4 — Apply forward error correction (FEC) coding rate.** LoRa adds redundancy via a convolutional code of rate $4/(4+CR)$, where $CR \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$. The actual transmitted bit rate becomes: $$\boxed{R_b = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times \frac{4}{4+CR} \text{ bps}}$$ (Or equivalently, $R_b = SF \cdot \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot CR$ when $CR$ is the fractional rate, depending on the textbook convention adopted.) --- ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Typical KTU Board Question **Question:** Calculate the bit rate, symbol period, and time-on-air for a 20-byte LoRaWAN application payload using: - $SF = 9$ - $BW = 125 \text{ kHz}$ - $CR = 1$ (i.e., coding rate $4/5$) - Preamble length $= 8$ symbols - Header enabled ($H = 0$) - Low-data-rate optimization enabled ($DE = 1$) - CRC enabled ($CRC = 1$) ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Bit Rate** $$R_b = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times \frac{4}{4+CR}$$ $$R_b = 9 \times \frac{125 \times 10^3}{2^{9}} \times \frac{4}{4+1}$$ $$R_b = 9 \times \frac{125000}{512} \times \frac{4}{5}$$ $$R_b = 9 \times 244.1406 \times 0.8$$ $$R_b = 1757.8125 \text{ bps} \approx 1.76 \text{ kbps}$$ **Step 2 — Symbol Period** $$T_s = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} = \frac{512}{125000} = 4.096 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3 — Number of Payload Symbols** $$N_{payload} = 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{8 \cdot PL - 4 \cdot SF + 28 + 16 \cdot CRC - 20 \cdot H}{4 \cdot (SF - 2 \cdot DE)} \right\rceil \cdot (CR + 4), \; 0\right)$$ Substituting $PL = 20$, $SF = 9$, $DE = 1$, $CRC = 1$, $H = 0$, $CR = 1$: $$N_{payload} = 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{8(20) - 4(9) + 28 + 16(1) - 0}{4(9 - 2)} \right\rceil \cdot (1+4), \; 0\right)$$ $$N_{payload} = 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{160 - 36 + 28 + 16}{28} \right\rceil \cdot 5, \; 0\right)$$ $$N_{payload} = 8 + \max\!\left(\lceil 6.0 \rceil \cdot 5, \; 0\right)$$ $$N_{payload} = 8 + 6 \cdot 5 = 8 + 30 = 38 \text{ symbols}$$ **Step 4 — Time on Air (Packet Duration)** $$T_{packet} = N_{payload} \times T_s = 38 \times 4.096 \text{ ms}$$ $$\boxed{T_{packet} \approx 155.65 \text{ ms}}$$ **Step 5 — Energy per Bit (for 20 bytes = 160 bits of application payload)** Assuming $P_{TX} = 14$ dBm $= 25$ mW: $$E_b = \frac{P_{TX} \times T_{packet}}{N_{bits}} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3} \times 155.65 \times 10^{-3}}{160}$$ $$E_b \approx 2.43 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J/bit} = 24.3 \text{ µJ/bit}$$ --- ## 3.3 Link Budget & Range Derivation The **maximum allowed path loss** (MAPL) determines range using the **Friis / Log-Distance path loss model**: $$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_{\sigma}$$ Where: - $PL(d_0)$ = path loss at reference distance $d_0$ (typically 1 m): $PL(d_0) = 20\log_{10}(f) - 27.55$ (in dB, $f$ in MHz) - $n$ = path-loss exponent (2.0 free space, 2.7–3.5 urban, 4.0–5.0 dense urban) - $X_{\sigma}$ = log-normal shadow fading (σ ≈ 6–10 dB) **Maximum Range Computation** (KTU-favorite): $$d_{max} = d_0 \times 10^{\frac{MAPL - PL(d_0) - X_{\sigma}}{10 n}}$$ Where the **Maximum Allowable Path Loss**: $$MAPL = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{RX} - M_f - M_{int}$$ - $P_{TX}$ = transmit power (dBm) - $G_{TX}, G_{RX}$ = antenna gains (dBi) - $S_{RX}$ = receiver sensitivity (dBm) - $M_f$ = fade margin (typically 10–20 dB) - $M_{int}$ = implementation loss margin (~3 dB) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight for Board:** Sensitivity improves (more negative) by ~3 dB per SF step. This buys approximately **+2.5 dB link margin**, which (with $n=3$) translates to roughly **+25% range** per SF step. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: LoRaWAN vs Other IoT Technologies | Parameter | **LoRaWAN** | **NB-IoT** | **Sigfox** | **Wi-Fi HaLow (802.11ah)** | **Zigbee** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Spectrum | Sub-GHz ISM | Licensed LTE (in-band) | Sub-GHz ISM | Sub-GHz ISM | 2.4 GHz | | Range | 2–15 km | 1–10 km | 3–10 km | <1 km | <100 m | | Data Rate | 0.3–50 kbps | 26–200 kbps | <1 kbps (100–600 bps) | >1 Mbps | 250 kbps | | Topology | Star-of-stars | Star | Star (ultra-narrow) | Star | Mesh | | Battery Life | 10+ years | 10+ years | 10+ years | Days–weeks | Months | | Modulation | CSS (Chirp) | QPSK | UNB DBPSK | OFDM | DSSS OQPSK | | Open Standard | Yes (LoRa Alliance) | Yes (3GPP) | Proprietary | Yes (IEEE) | Yes (IEEE 802.15.4) | | Best For | Smart agri, metering | Utility metering | Asset tracking | Video/IP cameras | Home automation | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 LoRaWAN Network Architecture (Star-of-Stars) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph FIELD["FIELD DOMAIN - LOW POWER EDGE"] ED1["END DEVICE 1
Soil Moisture Sensor"] ED2["END DEVICE 2
Smart Energy Meter"] ED3["END DEVICE 3
GPS Asset Tracker"] ED4["END DEVICE 4
Air Quality Monitor"] end subgraph EDGE["EDGE DOMAIN - RADIO ACCESS"] GW1["GATEWAY 1
LoRa Concentrator 8ch"] GW2["GATEWAY 2
LoRa Concentrator 8ch"] GW3["GATEWAY 3
LoRa Concentrator 8ch"] end subgraph CORE["CORE CLOUD DOMAIN"] NS["NETWORK SERVER
MAC Filtering ADR Deduplication"] JS["JOIN SERVER
Root Key Provisioning OTAA"] AS["APPLICATION SERVER
Payload Decryption App Logic"] end subgraph USER["END USER DOMAIN"] APP["IoT DASHBOARD
Web Mobile SCADA"] end ED1 -. LoRa 868 MHz SF7-SF12 .-> GW1 ED1 -. LoRa 868 MHz SF7-SF12 .-> GW2 ED2 -. LoRa 868 MHz SF7-SF12 .-> GW2 ED2 -. LoRa 868 MHz SF7-SF12 .-> GW3 ED3 -. LoRa 868 MHz SF7-SF12 .-> GW1 ED4 -. LoRa 868 MHz SF7-SF12 .-> GW3 GW1 -- IP UDP Packet Forwarder --> NS GW2 -- IP UDP Packet Forwarder --> NS GW3 -- IP UDP Packet Forwarder --> NS NS <--> JS NS -- Encrypted FRMPayload --> AS AS -- HTTPS MQTT --> APP ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Note how **End Device 1** is heard by **two gateways simultaneously**. This redundancy is intentional — the Network Server performs **deduplication** based on the DevAddr + FCnt pair, keeping only the strongest copy. ## 4.2 LoRaWAN Device Class Behavior (RX/TX Timing) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph CLASSA["CLASS A - Lowest Power Baseline"] A1["UPLINK TX
End Device Transmits"] A2["RX1 WINDOW
1 sec after TX on same channel"] A3["RX2 WINDOW
2 sec after TX on fixed channel"] A4["SLEEP MODE
Power Optimized"] A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 A4 -. next uplink cycle .-> A1 end subgraph CLASSB["CLASS B - Scheduled Downlink"] B1["UPLINK TX"] B2["RX1 WINDOW"] B3["RX2 WINDOW"] B4["SCHEDULED PING SLOT
Synced via Gateway Beacons"] B5["SLEEP MODE"] B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B5 B5 -. beacon sync .-> B4 B4 --> B5 B5 -. next uplink .-> B1 end subgraph CLASSC["CLASS C - Continuous Listening"] C1["UPLINK TX
Brief transmission"] C2["RX WINDOW
Continuously open except during TX"] C3["RX2 CONTINUOUS
Always receiving except during TX"] C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3 C3 -. downlink anytime .-> C2 end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** Class A devices are the only ones that can be **deeply battery-sleeped** between transmissions. Class C devices consume power continuously — they require a **mains or large battery** supply. ## 4.3 LoRaWAN MAC Frame Structure (Block-Level) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph PHY["PHY LAYER - RADIO"] P1["PREAMBLE
8 symbols configurable
10101010 pattern"] P2["SYNC WORD
2 symbols
0x34 for private networks"] P3["SFD
2.25 symbols
Start Frame Delimiter"] end subgraph MAC["MAC LAYER - LORAWAN HEADER"] M1["MHDR
1 byte
MType + RFU + Major"] M2["FHDR
7 to 22 bytes
DevAddr FCtrl FCnt FOpts"] M3["FPORT
1 byte
1 to 223 application"] M4["FRMPAYLOAD
0 to N bytes
encrypted with AppSKey"] M5["MIC
4 bytes
AES-128 CMAC NwkSKey"] end P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5 ``` ## 4.4 Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) Control Loop ```mermaid graph TB S1["ED UPLINK PACKET
SF9 BW125 14dBm"] S2["GW RECEIVES
RSSI -85dBm SNR -8dB"] S3["NS ANALYZES LINK
20 dB margin detected"] S4{"LINK MARGIN
GREATER THAN
10 dB?"} S5["NS DECISION
SEND MAC LINKADR CMD"] S6["ED APPLIES
SF8 BW125 12dBm"] S7["ED UPLINK
REDUCED AIRTIME"] S8["BATTERY SAVED
CAPACITY GAINED"] S9["KEEP CURRENT DR
Wait next cycle"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 S4 -- YES --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 S4 -- NO --> S9 ``` ## 4.5 LoRaWAN Activation Procedure (OTAA) ```mermaid graph LR A1["END DEVICE
Holds AppKey root key"] A2["DEV SENDS
Join Request
AppEUI DevEUI DevNonce"] A3["JOIN SERVER
Verifies DevNonce freshness"] A4["JOIN SERVER COMPUTES
NwkSKey and AppSKey
via AES-128"] A5["GW RELAYS
Packet to Network Server"] A6["NS and JS RESPOND
Join Accept with DevAddr"] A7["ED DERIVES KEYS
Receives DevAddr"] A8["SESSION ACTIVE
Data exchange begins"] A1 --> A2 --> A5 --> A3 --> A4 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Differentiate between LoRa and LoRaWAN. Why is it important to make this distinction in IoT system design?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | LoRa | LoRaWAN | |---|---|---| | **Layer** | Physical (PHY) layer | Media Access Control (MAC) layer | | **Origin** | Proprietary (Semtech Corporation) | Open standard (LoRa Alliance) | | **Function** | Radio modulation technique (Chirp Spread Spectrum) | Networking protocol defining communication rules | | **Defines** | How bits are encoded onto radio waves | Frame format, device classes, security, ADR | The distinction is important because **LoRa only handles the physical radio transmission** (how a 0/1 becomes a chirp), whereas **LoRaWAN defines the complete network protocol** — including device addressing, security, MAC commands, and the relationship between end devices, gateways, and servers. An IoT architect can theoretically use the LoRa physical layer with a custom MAC protocol, but for interoperable, secure multi-vendor IoT deployments, the standardized **LoRaWAN MAC** is essential. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]** **List the three LoRaWAN device classes and state which one is mandatory. Mention one advantage and one limitation of the mandatory class.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three LoRaWAN device classes are **Class A, Class B, and Class C**. **Class A is mandatory** — all LoRaWAN devices must implement Class A. - **Advantage:** Lowest power consumption because the device only opens two short receive windows (RX1 and RX2) immediately after each uplink transmission, then enters deep sleep. - **Limitation:** Downlink communication from the server can only occur **after an uplink**, causing unpredictable downlink latency (server must wait for the next uplink to send a command). **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the LoRaWAN network architecture in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the Network Server and the Join Server. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the bit rate, symbol period, and the approximate time on air for a LoRaWAN packet with a **30-byte application payload**, using **SF = 10, BW = 125 kHz, CR = 1, preamble = 8 symbols, low-data-rate optimization ON, CRC ON**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A(a) The **LoRaWAN architecture** follows a **"star-of-stars" topology** comprising four logical components: **1. End Devices (EDs):** Battery-powered sensors and actuators that communicate wirelessly with gateways using the LoRa radio modulation. They are typically Class A devices and use either **OTAA (Over-The-Air Activation)** or **ABP (Activation By Personalization)** to join the network. **2. Gateways (GWs):** LoRa-to-IP bridges. They contain multi-channel LoRa concentrator cards (typically 8 channels) and forward all received LoRa frames to the Network Server using the **Packet Forwarder** protocol (Semtech UDP) or **Basic Station** (WebSocket). The gateway **does not perform** MAC-layer intelligence — it is a transparent radio bridge. **3. Network Server (NS):** The central intelligence of the LoRaWAN. It performs: - **MAC command processing** (e.g., LinkADRReq to control data rate) - **Deduplication** (since multiple gateways may receive the same frame) - **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)** algorithm - **Scheduling of downlink frames** respecting duty-cycle regulations - Holds the **NwkSKey** for MIC verification **4. Join Server (JS):** Manages the **root AppKey** for each device and computes the **NwkSKey** and **AppSKey** session keys during the OTAA Join procedure. The Join Server is the only entity that ever sees the AppKey in cleartext. **5. Application Server (AS):** Receives the decrypted FRMPayload from the NS, holds the **AppSKey** to perform AES-128 decryption, and forwards data to the end-user application (dashboard, database, or analytics platform) typically via **MQTT or HTTPS**. *(Reference diagram: see SECTION 4.1.)* **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A(b) — Numerical **Given:** $PL = 30$ bytes, $SF = 10$, $BW = 125{,}000$ Hz, $CR = 1$, preamble = 8 symbols, $DE = 1$, $CRC = 1$, $H = 0$. **Step 1 — Bit Rate:** $$R_b = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times \frac{4}{4+CR}$$ $$R_b = 10 \times \frac{125000}{1024} \times \frac{4}{5} = 10 \times 122.07 \times 0.8 = 976.56 \text{ bps}$$ **Step 2 — Symbol Period:** $$T_s = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} = \frac{1024}{125000} = 8.192 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3 — Number of Payload Symbols:** $$N_{payload} = 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{8(30) - 4(10) + 28 + 16(1) - 0}{4(10 - 2 \cdot 1)} \right\rceil \cdot (1+4), \; 0\right)$$ $$N_{payload} = 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{240 - 40 + 28 + 16}{32} \right\rceil \cdot 5, \; 0\right)$$ $$N_{payload} = 8 + \lceil 7.625 \rceil \times 5 = 8 + 8 \times 5 = 48 \text{ symbols}$$ **Step 4 — Time on Air:** $$T_{packet} = 48 \times 8.192 \text{ ms} = 393.216 \text{ ms} \approx 0.393 \text{ seconds}$$ | Result | Value | |---|---| | Bit Rate | ≈ 976.56 bps | | Symbol Period | 8.192 ms | | Time on Air | ≈ 393.22 ms | **(7 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)** mechanism in LoRaWAN. How does it improve network scalability and battery life? State any four factors the Network Server uses to decide on a data rate change. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **LoRaWAN MAC frame structure**. Briefly describe the function of the MIC and the two session keys. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B(a) **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)** is a **Network-Server-controlled optimization mechanism** that dynamically adjusts the **Spreading Factor (SF), Bandwidth (BW), and Transmit Power (TX power)** of each end device to balance link reliability against network capacity and battery consumption. **How ADR Works:** 1. The end device initially joins with a conservative (low) data rate — typically SF12, the slowest but most robust setting. 2. As packets are received, the Network Server tracks per-device link metrics. 3. If the link has **excess margin** (packets arrive with high RSSI/SNR and no packet loss), the NS issues a **LinkADRReq MAC command** instructing the device to use a higher data rate (lower SF, higher BW, lower TX power). 4. If packets are **lost** (missed acknowledgments, low SNR), the NS commands a lower data rate. 5. The end device acknowledges via **LinkADRAns** MAC command. **Four Factors Used by the NS for ADR Decision:** 1. **Packet Success Rate (PSR)** — ratio of acknowledged to transmitted uplinks. 2. **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)** — received signal power. 3. **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — link quality metric. 4. **Recent packet loss history** — count of missed downlinks or unacknowledged frames. **Benefits:** - **Battery Life:** Higher data rate ⇒ lower time-on-air ⇒ lower energy per packet ⇒ multi-year battery life. - **Network Capacity:** Faster packets free up the channel sooner, allowing more devices per gateway (different SFs are orthogonal, but airtime is still the bottleneck). - **Spectrum Efficiency:** Lower TX power reduces interference to co-located networks. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B(b) **LoRaWAN MAC Frame Structure (Block Diagram):** *(See SECTION 4.3 for the visual block diagram. Written out here:)* | Field | Size | Function | |---|---|---| | **MHDR** | 1 byte | MAC Header: identifies MType (Join Request, Join Accept, Unconfirmed Data, Confirmed Data) and LoRaWAN version (Major field) | | **FHDR** | 7–22 bytes | Frame Header: contains DevAddr (4B), FCtrl (1B — flags for ADR/ACK), FCnt (2B — frame counter), and FOpts (0–15B — piggybacked MAC commands) | | **FPort** | 1 byte | Application port: 1–223 = application payload, 224 = MAC-only payload, 0 = MAC commands inside FRMPayload | | **FRMPayload** | 0–N bytes | Actual user data, **encrypted with AppSKey** (end-to-end) | | **MIC** | 4 bytes | Message Integrity Code: 4-byte truncated AES-128-CMAC computed over the entire PHYPayload using NwkSKey | **The Two Session Keys:** - **NwkSKey (Network Session Key):** Used by the Network Server to compute and verify the **MIC** — ensuring message integrity and authenticating that the packet came from a legitimate device. - **AppSKey (Application Session Key):** Used by the Application Server to **encrypt and decrypt the FRMPayload** using AES-128 in CTR mode — ensuring end-to-end payload confidentiality so the network operator cannot eavesdrop on application data. **Important security property:** Because the NS holds NwkSKey and the AS holds AppSKey as **separate keys held by separate entities**, the network operator is cryptographically prevented from reading user data — a critical privacy feature of LoRaWAN. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LoRaWAN Questions:** > 1. **Confusing LoRa with LoRaWAN:** Examiners will deduct 1 mark immediately if you use these interchangeably. LoRa is the **PHY** (Chirp modulation); LoRaWAN is the **MAC protocol**. > 2. **Writing "LoRaWAN uses LoRa modulation" without elaboration:** Always mention **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** explicitly. > 3. **Wrong bit-rate formula:** The most common error is omitting the coding-rate term $CR$ or writing $\frac{BW}{2^{SF}}$ as $\frac{2^{SF}}{BW}$. > 4. **Saying "Class A is the most advanced"** — it is the *baseline* and *lowest-power*, not the most advanced. Class C is the most capable (continuous RX) but power-hungry. > 5. **Forgetting that gateways are dumb:** If you write "the gateway decrypts the payload using AppSKey," that is **wrong** — gateways do not hold session keys; only the AS does. > 6. **Not specifying the regional band for India:** For Kerala/KTU projects, always cite the **IN865-867** band with its 3 mandatory channels (865.0625, 865.4025, 865.985 MHz). > 7. **Time-on-air formula sign error:** The denominator $4 \cdot (SF - 2 \cdot DE)$ frequently gets mis-typed. Memorize: when $DE=1$ (low-data-rate optimization enabled), the denominator reduces, **increasing** $N_{payload}$ and thus time-on-air. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Print This Before Exam:** - ✅ **LoRa** = Physical layer (CSS modulation) | **LoRaWAN** = MAC layer protocol - ✅ **LoRaWAN topology** = **Star-of-Stars** (End Devices ⇢ Gateways ⇢ Network Server ⇢ Application Server) - ✅ **Gateways are dumb bridges**; all intelligence is in the **Network Server** - ✅ **Three device classes:** A (lowest power, mandatory), B (beacon-scheduled), C (continuous RX, mains-powered) - ✅ **Class A** has 2 RX windows: **RX1** (same channel/SF as uplink, +1s) and **RX2** (fixed channel/SF, +2s) - ✅ **Six spreading factors: SF7 to SF12** — higher SF = longer range, slower data rate, longer airtime, more battery drain - ✅ **Bit Rate Formula:** $R_b = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times \frac{4}{4+CR}$ - ✅ **Symbol Period:** $T_s = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW}$ seconds - ✅ **Sensitivity floor** improves by ~3 dB per SF step; range roughly +25% per SF step in urban environments - ✅ **Two AES-128 keys:** **NwkSKey** (MIC integrity, held by NS) and **AppSKey** (payload encryption, held by AS) — derived from **AppKey** (root key, held by Join Server) - ✅ **Two activation modes:** **OTAA** (Over-The-Air Activation — Join procedure, more secure, recommended) and **ABP** (Activation By Personalization — keys hardcoded) - ✅ **India band:** **IN865-867 MHz**, 3 mandatory uplink channels (865.0625, 865.4025, 865.985 MHz), duty cycle ≤ 1% - ✅ **ADR** is **server-controlled** (not device-controlled); uses LinkADRReq/LinkADRAns MAC commands - ✅ **Max MAC payload:** 243 bytes at SF7/BW125, down to 51 bytes at SF12/BW125 - ✅ **Duty Cycle Rule:** ≤ 36 seconds of airtime per hour per channel (for EU/India regulations) - ✅ **Frame Counter (FCnt)** is critical for replay-attack prevention; NS rejects out-of-order frames - ✅ **LoRa Alliance** is the standards body; **Semtech** makes the silicon (SX1276, SX1278, SX1262) > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick — "C-S-F" for LoRaWAN Components:** **C**ore network, **S**ecurity keys (AppKey/NwkSKey/AppSKey), **F**rame structure (MHDR/FHDR/FPort/FRMPayload/MIC).

topics.title: Weightless
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weightless

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weightless** is a family of open, royalty-free **Low Power Wide Area Network (LPWAN)** wireless communication standards developed specifically for the **Internet of Things (IoT)**. It is engineered to support massive numbers of low-cost, battery-operated end devices communicating over long ranges (typically **2 km to 10 km** in urban areas, and up to **50 km** in line-of-sight rural environments) with extremely low power consumption (battery life of up to **10 years**).

The Weightless Special Interest Group (Weightless SIG), founded in **2012**, developed three distinct standards tailored to different deployment scenarios:

| Standard | Variant | Spectrum | Direction |
|----------|---------|----------|-----------|
| **Weightless-W** | Weightless-W | TV White Space (470–790 MHz) | One-way (downlink only) |
| **Weightless-N** | Weightless-N | Sub-1 GHz ISM (433/868/915 MHz) | One-way (uplink only) |
| **Weightless-P** | Weightless-P | Sub-1 GHz ISM (433/868/915 MHz) | **Bidirectional** |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, **Weightless-P** is the most significant variant as it is the only fully bidirectional, two-way LPWAN standard in the Weightless family, offering the best balance of range, capacity, and power efficiency for IoT applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **postal service in a remote mountainous village**:
- **Letters (data packets)** are written by villagers (IoT sensors) and sent via a single **mail truck (base station)** that travels along a fixed route.
- The villagers write very short messages (small payloads) but send them infrequently to **save battery (energy)**.
- The mail truck is powerful and can cover huge distances even in poor weather conditions (long range, robust signal).
- Sometimes the postmaster needs to send a reply back (bidirectional communication in Weightless-P), giving instructions or updating configurations.

This analogy captures the essence of Weightless:
- **Low Power** → Devices sleep most of the time.
- **Wide Area** → Ranges of kilometers, not meters.
- **Low data rate** → Small, infrequent messages.
- **Massive device support** → Thousands of devices per base station.

## 1.3 Why Weightless Was Created

Existing wireless technologies like **Wi-Fi** (short range, high power) and **cellular (3G/4G)** (high power, expensive modules) were unsuitable for IoT. Weightless was designed to fill the gap by offering:

1. **Cost-effective modules** (target: under **$5** per end device).
2. **Long battery life** (target: **10 years** on a single AA cell).
3. **Long range** (kilometers, not meters).
4. **Massive scalability** (millions of devices per network).
5. **Open standard** with no proprietary lock-in.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Architecture of the Weightless-P Network

A Weightless network consists of three primary components:

### 2.1.1 End Node (End Device / Sensor)
- Battery-operated sensor or actuator.
- Contains a **Weightless modem** with radio, microcontroller, and antenna.
- Communicates with the base station using the Weightless air interface.
- Operates in a duty-cycled mode (mostly sleeping).

### 2.1.2 Base Station (Gateway)
- High-power, mains-powered transceiver.
- Aggregates traffic from thousands of end nodes.
- Connects to the **Network Server** over an IP backhaul (Ethernet, fiber, cellular).
- Manages the **MAC layer**, scheduling, and adaptive data rate.

### 2.1.3 Network Server
- Core intelligence of the network.
- Handles **device authentication**, **downlink scheduling**, **billing**, and **data routing**.
- Interfaces with application servers via standard IP protocols.

## 2.2 Weightless-P Technical Specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Weightless-P is the flagship LPWAN technology of the Weightless family. The "P" originally stood for "Premium" but is often interpreted as the most capable variant.

### 2.2.1 Key Technical Parameters

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| Frequency Bands | Sub-1 GHz ISM (433 MHz, 868 MHz, 915 MHz) |
| Bandwidth | **12.5 kHz** (narrow channel) |
| Data Rate | **0.025 kbps to **100 kbps** (adaptive) |
| Modulation | **GMSK**, **QPSK**, **8PSK** (adaptive) |
| Range | **2 km** (urban) to **50 km** (rural LoS) |
| Channel Spacing | 12.5 kHz |
| Duplex Mode | TDD (Time Division Duplex) |
| Maximum EIRP | **+17 dBm** |
| Receiver Sensitivity | **-128 dBm** |
| Battery Life | Up to **10 years** (with appropriate duty cycle) |
| Devices per Base Station | Up to **250,000** end nodes |
| Latency | **10 ms to 10 s** (depending on payload size) |

### 2.2.2 Modulation Schemes Explained

Weightless-P supports **adaptive modulation** based on channel conditions:

1. **GMSK (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying)**:
   - Constant envelope modulation.
   - Robust against amplifier nonlinearities.
   - Low spectral efficiency but very reliable.
   - Used for distant end nodes with weak signals.

2. **QPSK (Quadrature Phase Shift Keying)**:
   - 2 bits per symbol.
   - Better spectral efficiency than GMSK.
   - Used in moderate range scenarios.

3. **8PSK (8-Phase Shift Keying)**:
   - 3 bits per symbol.
   - Highest spectral efficiency.
   - Used for closer end nodes with strong signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive Modulation** allows the system to trade data rate for reliability dynamically. A distant node uses GMSK (slower but robust), while a nearby node uses 8PSK (faster but requires higher SNR).

## 2.3 Weightless-N and Weightless-W Comparison

| Feature | Weightless-W | Weightless-N | Weightless-P |
|---------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| Spectrum | TV White Space | Sub-1 GHz ISM | Sub-1 GHz ISM |
| Modulation | DBPSK, OFDM | UNB (DSSS) | GMSK, QPSK, 8PSK |
| Direction | Downlink only | Uplink only | **Bidirectional** |
| Range | 5–10 km | 3–5 km | 2–50 km |
| Data Rate | 1–10 Mbps | <1 kbps | 0.025–100 kbps |
| Power Consumption | Medium | Very Low | Low |
| Complexity | High | Low | Medium |
| Status | Mostly discontinued | Mostly discontinued | **Active / Most Deployed** |

## 2.4 Weightless-P Frame Structure

A Weightless-P frame is divided into:
1. **Preamble**: Synchronization pattern.
2. **Sync Word**: Used for frame synchronization.
3. **Header**: Contains frame control, addressing, payload length.
4. **Payload**: Application data (typically 10–250 bytes).
5. **FCS (Frame Check Sequence)**: 16-bit or 32-bit CRC for error detection.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations govern the link budget and capacity planning of Weightless networks — essential for KTU numerical problems.

### 2.5.1 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
$$

Where:
- $d$ = distance between transmitter and receiver (meters)
- $f$ = frequency (Hz)
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

### 2.5.2 Link Budget Equation

$$
P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - \text{FSPL} - L_{\text{other}}
$$

Where:
- $P_{RX}$ = Received power (dBm)
- $P_{TX}$ = Transmitted power (dBm)
- $G_{TX}$, $G_{RX}$ = Antenna gains (dBi)
- $L_{\text{other}}$ = Other losses (cable, fading margin, body loss) in dB

### 2.5.3 Receiver Sensitivity

$$
S_{RX} \text{ (dBm)} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(BW) + \text{NF} + \text{SNR}_{\min}
$$

Where:
- $-174$ dBm/Hz = thermal noise floor at 290 K
- $BW$ = bandwidth (Hz)
- $\text{NF}$ = noise figure of receiver (dB)
- $\text{SNR}_{\min}$ = minimum SNR required for the chosen modulation

### 2.5.4 Shannon Capacity (Theoretical Maximum)

$$
C = B \cdot \log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR})
$$

Where $B$ = bandwidth in Hz, $C$ = capacity in bps.

### 2.5.5 Spectral Efficiency

$$
\eta = \frac{R}{B} \quad \text{(bits/s/Hz)}
$$

Where $R$ = data rate (bps), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz).

### 2.5.6 Battery Life Estimation

$$
T_{\text{life}} = \frac{C_{\text{battery}}}{\left(I_{\text{tx}} \cdot t_{\text{tx}}\right) + \left(I_{\text{rx}} \cdot t_{\text{rx}}\right) + \left(I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot t_{\text{sleep}}\right)} \times \text{Duty Cycle Factor}
$$

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

| Application Domain | Use Case |
|--------------------|----------|
| **Smart Metering** | Water, gas, electricity meter reading |
| **Smart Agriculture** | Soil moisture, livestock tracking |
| **Asset Tracking** | Container, pallet, vehicle tracking |
| **Smart Cities** | Parking sensors, waste bin monitoring |
| **Environmental Monitoring** | Air quality, flood detection |
| **Industrial IoT** | Tank level monitoring, predictive maintenance |
