| Connected between | GPIO pin and $V_{CC}$ (3.3 V) | GPIO pin and GND |
| Pin state when switch OPEN | **HIGH (3.3 V)** | **LOW (0 V)** |
| Pin state when switch CLOSED | **LOW (0 V)** | **HIGH (3.3 V)** |
| Switch position | Between GPIO and GND | Between GPIO and $V_{CC}$ |

**Why necessary:** When the switch is **open**, the GPIO pin is electrically isolated (high-impedance / "floating"). It can pick up stray electromagnetic noise and read random HIGH/LOW values, causing erroneous behaviour. A pull-up/pull-down resistor **anchors** the pin to a known, stable logic level (0 V or 3.3 V) when the switch is open.

The Raspberry Pi provides internal pull-up and pull-down resistors of about **50 kΩ** that can be enabled in software, removing the need for an external resistor in most cases. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> *As per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 internal-choice questions typically allocate 7 + 7 marks across two sub-parts.*

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 Choice Q1]**

**(a) Design an interfacing circuit to drive a red LED using a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin. Compute the required series resistor value and the power it dissipates. Assume $V_F = 2.0\,\text{V}$, desired $I_F = 10\,\text{mA}$, and $V_{CC} = 3.3\,\text{V}$. Justify the choice of standard resistor. (7 marks)**

**(b) With a neat circuit diagram, explain how a push-button switch is interfaced to a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin using a pull-up resistor. How does the Pi's internal pull-up simplify the hardware? (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Step 1 — Circuit description.** A red LED is connected to GPIO17 (physical pin 11) through a series resistor $R_{LED}$. The LED's cathode is tied to GND (physical pin 6). The Pi drives GPIO17 HIGH (3.3 V) to turn the LED on.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL.** The voltage drop across $R_{LED}$ is the supply minus the LED forward voltage:

$$
V_{R} \;=\; V_{CC} - V_F \;=\; 3.3 - 2.0 \;=\; 1.3\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ohm's law.** To restrict current to 10 mA:

$$
R_{LED} \;=\; \frac{V_{R}}{I_F} \;=\; \frac{1.3}{0.010} \;=\; 130\,\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Standard value selection.** The nearest higher E12 value is **220 Ω**. Using 220 Ω gives a safe current of:

$$
I_{F,actual} \;=\; \frac{1.3}{220} \;\approx\; 5.9\,\text{mA}
$$

This is well within the 16 mA GPIO limit. **220 Ω is the standard choice.**

**Step 5 — Power dissipation in the resistor.**

$$
P_R \;=\; I_{F,actual}^2 \cdot R_{LED} \;=\; (5.9 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot 220 \;\approx\; 7.7\,\text{mW}
$$

A standard **1/4 W (250 mW)** resistor is more than adequate (it will operate cool). **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Step 1 — Circuit description.** A normally-open push-button switch is connected between **GPIO27** (pin 13) and **GND** (pin 6). A pull-up resistor $R_{PU} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ is connected between **GPIO27** and the **3.3 V rail** (pin 1).

**Step 2 — Operating principle (using $V_{pin}$ derivation).**

| Switch State | Path | $V_{pin}$ | Logic |
|---|---|---|---|
| OPEN | $3.3\,\text{V} \to R_{PU} \to$ GPIO27 | ≈ 3.3 V | HIGH (1) |
| CLOSED | $3.3\,\text{V} \to R_{PU} \to$ switch $\to$ GND | ≈ 0 V | LOW (0) |

When the switch is **open**, the pull-up resistor pulls the pin up to 3.3 V (no current flows into the high-impedance GPIO). When the switch is **closed**, the switch short-circuits the pin to GND, overriding the pull-up.

**Step 3 — Current through the pull-up when switch is closed:**

$$
I_{PU} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}}{R_{PU}} \;=\; \frac{3.3}{10\,000} \;=\; 330\,\mu\text{A}
$$

This is a very small, safe current.

**Step 4 — Internal pull-up simplification.** The Raspberry Pi's Broadcom SoC has built-in pull-up resistors of approximately **50 kΩ**. These can be enabled in software via:

```python
GPIO.setup(SWITCH_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)
```

This eliminates the need for an **external 10 kΩ resistor**, reducing the BOM, wiring complexity, and PCB area. The internal pull-up is weaker (higher resistance), making it slightly more susceptible to noise in electrically noisy environments, but for hobbyist and educational use, it is more than sufficient. **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice Q2]**

**(a) With the help of a circuit diagram, explain how a Raspberry Pi can be used to read a switch input. Compare the use of internal pull-up vs external pull-down configurations. (7 marks)**

**(b) Write a short Python program (using `RPi.GPIO`) that reads a push-button connected to GPIO27 and blinks an LED connected to GPIO17 at 1 Hz only while the button is held pressed. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Step 1 — Circuit diagram (described verbally; physical layout on breadboard):**
- GPIO27 (pin 13) connects to one terminal of the push-button.
- The other terminal of the push-button connects to GND (pin 6).
- A pull-up resistor of 10 kΩ is connected between GPIO27 and the 3.3 V rail (pin 1).
- An LED with 220 Ω series resistor is on GPIO17 (as in Q.A).

**Step 2 — Internal pull-up (recommended for clean designs).** Enable in code:

```python
GPIO.setup(27, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)
```

**Advantage:** No external hardware needed; no extra wiring. **Disadvantage:** Internal resistance is ~50 kΩ, weaker than a typical 10 kΩ external pull-up, so it's more noise-sensitive in electrically long wires.

**Step 3 — External pull-down.** Wire a 10 kΩ resistor between GPIO27 and GND. The switch connects GPIO27 to 3.3 V.

**Advantage:** Stronger pull (10 kΩ vs 50 kΩ) → better noise immunity; works even if the switch wire is long. **Disadvantage:** Extra component, extra wiring.

| Feature | Internal Pull-up | External Pull-down |
|---|---|---|
| External parts | None | 10 kΩ resistor |
| Pull resistance | ≈ 50 kΩ (weak) | 10 kΩ (stronger) |
| Noise immunity | Lower | Higher |
| Suitable for long wires | No | Yes |
| Software control | Yes (`PUD_UP`) | Always on |

**[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Algorithm in plain English:**
1. Import `RPi.GPIO` and `time`.
2. Use BCM numbering.
3. Configure GPIO17 as OUTPUT (LED).
4. Configure GPIO27 as INPUT with internal pull-down enabled (so it reads LOW when switch is open and HIGH when switch is closed via 3.3 V connection — adjust per wiring).
5. In an infinite loop:
   - If the switch is pressed, blink the LED with 0.5 s ON and 0.5 s OFF (1 Hz = 1 cycle per second).
   - If the switch is released, turn the LED OFF.

**Program:**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

LED = 17
SW  = 27

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setup(LED, GPIO.OUT)
GPIO.setup(SW, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_DOWN)

try:
    while True:
        if GPIO.input(SW) == GPIO.HIGH:        # Button pressed
            GPIO.output(LED, GPIO.HIGH)       # LED ON
            time.sleep(0.5)                   # 500 ms
            GPIO.output(LED, GPIO.LOW)        # LED OFF
            time.sleep(0.5)                   # 500 ms
        else:
            GPIO.output(LED, GPIO.LOW)        # LED OFF when button released
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

**Mapping to the question's requirements:**
- **"1 Hz"** → 1 cycle per second → 0.5 s ON + 0.5 s OFF = 1.0 s period → $f = 1/T = 1\,\text{Hz}$ ✅
- **"only while button held"** → `if GPIO.input(SW) == HIGH:` ensures the blink only happens in that branch; else block forces LED OFF ✅
- **Debounce** → implicit via the 0.5 s delay (well above the 5–20 ms bounce window) ✅

**[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mA to A** in the resistor formula: writing $R = 1.3 / 10 = 0.13$ instead of $R = 1.3 / 0.010 = 130\,\Omega$ — instant **zero** for that sub-part.
> 2. **Choosing 3.3 V directly across the LED** (no resistor) — student assumes the LED is "rated" for 3.3 V. Most LEDs are NOT.
> 3. **Confusing pull-up vs pull-down polarity** — writing "switch open = LOW" for a pull-up circuit. Memorise the truth table.
> 4. **Forgetting `GPIO.cleanup()`** in the program — minor deduction, but shows lack of best-practice awareness.
> 5. **Using BOARD numbering without declaring it** — always call `GPIO.setmode(...)` explicitly.
> 6. **Ignoring debounce** — a single sentence like "a 200 ms delay is added to debounce" earns full credit on the debounce aspect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🧠

- [x] **Raspberry Pi GPIO logic level = 3.3 V (NOT 5 V).** Connecting 5 V to a GPIO pin will damage the Pi.
- [x] **Maximum current per GPIO pin ≈ 16 mA**; total across all pins ≈ 50 mA. Design LEDs to draw ≤ 10 mA.
- [x] **Current-limiting resistor formula:** $R_{LED} = (V_{CC} - V_F) / I_F$ — must always be computed.
- [x] **Standard E12 values near 130 Ω:** choose the **next higher** (220 Ω is the universal safe choice for 3.3 V logic).
- [x] **$V_F$ depends on LED colour:** red ≈ 2.0 V, blue/white ≈ 3.0–3.4 V. Use the correct value in calculations.
- [x] **Power dissipated by the resistor** is in mW for typical designs — a 1/4 W resistor is always sufficient.
- [x] **Switch interfacing needs a pull-up OR pull-down resistor** to prevent the floating-pin problem.
- [x] **Pull-up (default HIGH) configuration is preferred** in practice and matches the Pi's internal pull-ups.
- [x] **Pi's internal pull-up/pull-down resistance ≈ 50 kΩ** — can be software-enabled via `pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP/DOWN`.
- [x] **Mechanical switch debouncing** is mandatory — use 100–200 ms delay or a software debounce counter.
- [x] **BCM numbering** vs **BOARD numbering** — `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` is the KTU-recommended standard.
- [x] Always end the program with `GPIO.cleanup()` to release pins and prevent damage on next run.
- [x] The **floating pin** is the #1 source of "phantom" button presses in IoT projects — pull resistors fix it.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**PcDino** is a low-cost, compact, Arduino-compatible **Internet of Things (IoT) development board** that integrates the **ESP8266 Wi-Fi SoC (System on Chip)** into a thumb-drive-sized form factor. It is designed primarily for rapid prototyping of Wi-Fi-enabled embedded applications, sensor networks, and cloud-connected devices. The board exposes a set of general-purpose input/output (GPIO) pins, supports serial communication protocols, and can be programmed using the **Arduino Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** via a micro-USB interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** PcDino is classified under *“Other IoT Devices”* in Module 4, alongside devices like Raspberry Pi, NodeMCU, ESP32, and other single-board computers. Students must understand its architecture, specifications, pinout, and how it compares with other development boards for IoT.

## Key Terminology Decoded

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **ESP8266** | A low-power, 32-bit Wi-Fi microcontroller SoC made by Espressif Systems. |
| **SoC (System on Chip)** | An integrated circuit that consolidates the CPU, RAM, ROM, Wi-Fi, and peripheral interfaces onto a single chip. |
| **GPIO** | General-Purpose Input/Output pins used to interface with sensors and actuators. |
| **Arduino-Compatible** | Means the board can be programmed using the Arduino IDE and Arduino-style C/C++ sketches. |
| **SoM (System on Module)** | A board-level integration that packs a complete module (here, ESP8266) for easy embedding. |

## Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — “The Swiss Army Knife of IoT Prototyping”**
> Imagine you want to build a smart home device that reads room temperature and uploads it to the cloud. Normally, you would need three separate things:
> 1. A microcontroller (the “brain”),
> 2. A Wi-Fi module (the “mouth” to talk to the internet),
> 3. A USB programmer (the “teacher” to load code).
>
> **PcDino combines all three into a single device the size of a USB flash drive.** Just plug it into your laptop, write a few lines of code in the Arduino IDE, and you have a Wi-Fi-connected IoT node ready to deploy — no extra shields, no breadboard clutter, no separate programmer.

## Why PcDino Matters in IoT

- **Cost-Effective:** Among the cheapest Wi-Fi-enabled development boards available.
- **Compact Footprint:** Its small form factor makes it suitable for wearable and embedded IoT deployments.
- **Ease of Programming:** Compatible with the widely-used Arduino ecosystem.
- **Built-in Networking:** Eliminates the need for an external Wi-Fi shield (unlike the Arduino Uno + ESP-01 combo).
- **Community Support:** Large open-source community provides libraries, firmware, and tutorials.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line takeaway:** *PcDino = ESP8266 + Arduino ecosystem + USB programmer, all in one tiny board.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Architecture of PcDino

The PcDino board is fundamentally an **ESP8266-based development platform**. Its internal architecture can be broken down as follows:

### A. Core Processing Unit
- **MCU:** Tensilica L106 32-bit RISC processor (typically running at **80 MHz** in normal mode, with overclocking support up to **160 MHz**).
- **Architecture:** RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) with a streamlined instruction pipeline.
- **Operating Voltage:** **3.3 V** (Logic level is 3.3 V — *critical to remember for sensor interfacing*).

### B. Memory Subsystem
- **SRAM:** ~50 KB to 160 KB available for user programs (depending on the ESP8266 variant).
- **Flash Memory:** External SPI flash, typically **1 MB to 4 MB**, used for storing firmware and user sketches.
- **ROM:** Boot ROM is part of the internal mask ROM of ESP8266.

### C. Wireless Subsystem
- **Wi-Fi Standards Supported:** **IEEE 802.11 b/g/n**
- **Frequency Band:** **2.4 GHz**
- **Modes Supported:** Station (STA), Access Point (AP), and STA+AP combined mode.
- **Security Protocols:** WPA, WPA2, WEP.

### D. Peripheral Interfaces
- **GPIO Pins:** Typically 9 to 11 usable digital pins (exact count depends on board revision).
- **ADC:** 1× 10-bit Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC on a dedicated pin (A0).
- **Communication Protocols:** UART, SPI, I²C (software-bit-banged), and PWM (software-emulated on most pins).
- **USB Interface:** Micro-USB port for power and programming, powered by an onboard **CH340 or CP2102 USB-to-Serial converter**.

## 2.2 Operating Modes of ESP8266 (Applicable to PcDino)

| Mode | Description | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Active Mode (AM)** | CPU and Wi-Fi are ON; full functionality. | Normal operation. |
| **Modem-Sleep Mode** | CPU ON, Wi-Fi radio OFF between DTIM intervals. | Light power saving. |
| **Light-Sleep Mode** | CPU paused, Wi-Fi OFF; resumes on GPIO interrupt or timer. | Battery-powered apps. |
| **Deep-Sleep Mode** | Only the Real-Time Clock (RTC) remains active. | Ultra-low-power IoT nodes. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** Deep-sleep current of ESP8266 is approximately **10 µA**, making it ideal for battery-operated IoT sensor nodes. Wake-up is triggered by an external GPIO reset or a timer.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Specification Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since PcDino is a hardware platform rather than a mathematical concept, the "formulas" here are its **electrical and timing specifications**, which are commonly asked in KTU exams as short-answer or comparison questions.

| Parameter | Symbol / Value | Unit | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Operating Voltage | $V_{cc} = 3.3$ | V | Do NOT feed 5 V to GPIO pins. |
| Input Voltage (via USB) | $V_{usb} = 5$ | V | Regulated down onboard to 3.3 V. |
| Typical Active Current | $I_{active} \approx 70$ | mA | Average during Wi-Fi transmission. |
| Deep-Sleep Current | $I_{sleep} \approx 10$ | $\mu A$ | Critical for low-power IoT design. |
| ADC Resolution | $N = 10$ | bits | Quantization levels: $2^{10} = 1024$ |
| ADC Reference Voltage | $V_{ref} = 3.3$ | V | Default internal reference. |
| Wi-Fi Frequency | $f_{wifi} = 2.4$ | GHz | ISM band. |
| Maximum Clock Speed | $f_{clk} = 160$ | MHz | Overclocked; default is 80 MHz. |
| Flash Memory (typical) | $M_{flash} = 4$ | MB | Stores program + file system. |

### ADC Quantization Formula (Applied to PcDino's Analog Pin)

The on-board ADC converts an analog voltage $V_{in}$ into a digital value $D$:

$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^{N} - 1) \right\rfloor
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Digital output value (integer from 0 to 1023)
- $V_{in}$ = Input analog voltage
- $V_{ref}$ = Reference voltage (default 3.3 V)
- $N$ = ADC resolution in bits (= 10)

### Reverse Calculation (Voltage from ADC Reading)

$$
V_{in} = \frac{D}{2^{N} - 1} \times V_{ref}
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PcDino is used in:
- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture sensors reporting data over Wi-Fi.
- **Home Automation:** Wi-Fi-controlled relays, smart bulbs.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Wireless monitoring of machine parameters.
- **Wearables:** Compact form factor fits into health-monitoring bands.
- **Academic Projects:** Most popular low-cost Wi-Fi node for B.Tech final-year IoT projects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — ADC Conversion on PcDino

> **Problem:** A temperature sensor (e.g., LM35) outputs an analog voltage of **0.85 V** at room temperature. The output is connected to the **A0 pin** of PcDino. Calculate the digital value that the ESP8266's 10-bit ADC will read.

### Given Data
- $V_{in} = 0.85 \text{ V}$
- $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$
- $N = 10 \text{ bits}$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the total number of quantization levels.

$$
L = 2^{N} - 1 = 2^{10} - 1 = 1024 - 1 = 1023
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the ADC conversion formula.

$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times L \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 3:** Plug in numerical values.

$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{0.85}{3.3} \times 1023 \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the fraction.

$$
\frac{0.85}{3.3} \approx 0.2576
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by 1023.

$$
0.2576 \times 1023 \approx 263.52
$$

**Step 6:** Take the floor value.

$$
D = 263
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{D = 263}
$$

### Verification (Reverse Calculation)

$$
V_{in} = \frac{263}{1023} \times 3.3 \approx 0.848 \text{ V}
$$

This is within the quantization error of $\pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2 \times 1023} = \pm 1.61 \text{ mV}$ — acceptable.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Power Budget Calculation

> **Problem:** A PcDino node spends 90% of its time in deep-sleep and 10% in active mode transmitting data. Calculate the average current consumption. Given: $I_{active} = 70 \text{ mA}$, $I_{sleep} = 10 \text{ µA}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Convert units to be consistent (use mA).

$$
I_{sleep} = 10 \, \mu A = 0.01 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the duty-cycle weighted average formula.

$$
I_{avg} = (D_{active} \times I_{active}) + (D_{sleep} \times I_{sleep})
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute duty cycles $D_{active} = 0.10$ and $D_{sleep} = 0.90$.

$$
I_{avg} = (0.10 \times 70) + (0.90 \times 0.01)
$$

**Step 4:** Compute each term.

$$
I_{avg} = 7.0 + 0.009
$$

**Step 5:** Final result.

$$
\boxed{I_{avg} \approx 7.009 \text{ mA}}
$$

### Engineering Insight

> [!TIP]
> This is why **deep-sleep is the single most important power-saving technique** in ESP8266-based IoT designs. By spending 90% of the time asleep, the average current drops from 70 mA to about 7 mA — a **10× improvement**. With a 1000 mAh battery, theoretical battery life becomes:
>
> $$
> t_{battery} = \frac{C}{I_{avg}} = \frac{1000 \text{ mAh}}{7.009 \text{ mA}} \approx 142.7 \text{ hours} \approx 5.95 \text{ days}
> $$

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — PcDino vs Other IoT Boards

| Feature | **PcDino** | **Arduino Uno** | **NodeMCU (ESP8266)** | **Raspberry Pi Pico** | **Raspberry Pi 4** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| MCU | ESP8266 | ATmega328P | ESP8266 | RP2040 | Broadcom BCM2711 |
| Clock Speed | 80 MHz | 16 MHz | 80 MHz | 133 MHz | 1.5 GHz |
| Wi-Fi | Built-in | No (needs shield) | Built-in | No | Built-in (Pi 4 has Wi-Fi) |
| Operating Voltage | 3.3 V | 5 V | 3.3 V | 3.3 V | 5 V |
| GPIO Count | ~9 | 14 | ~11 | 26 | 40 |
| ADC | 1× 10-bit | 6× 10-bit | 1× 10-bit | 3× 12-bit | None (external needed) |
| Flash | 1–4 MB | 32 KB | 1–4 MB | 2 MB | microSD based |
| RAM | ~50 KB | 2 KB | ~50 KB | 264 KB | 4 GB |
| Best Suited For | Compact Wi-Fi IoT | Sensor-rich projects | Wi-Fi IoT | Real-time control | Edge AI / Gateway |
| Cost (approx.) | Low | Low | Low | Low | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Be prepared to draw or explain the comparison between PcDino and any one of: Arduino Uno, NodeMCU, or Raspberry Pi. Focus on **Wi-Fi capability, voltage levels, and use cases**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — PcDino Internal Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the internal functional architecture of the PcDino board, mapping the data, power, and signal flow between its major subsystems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    USB["Micro USB Port"] --> UART["CH340 / CP2102 USB to Serial Converter"]
    UART --> ESP["ESP8266 SoC Main Processor"]
    REG["3.3V LDO Voltage Regulator"] --> ESP
    USB --> REG
    ESP --> WIFI["2.4 GHz WiFi Radio 802.11 b/g/n"]
    ESP --> CPU["Tensilica L106 32 bit RISC CPU 80 MHz"]
    ESP --> SRAM["SRAM ~50 KB User Program"]
    ESP --> FLASH["External SPI Flash 1 to 4 MB"]
    ESP --> GPIO["GPIO Pins D0 to D8"]
    ESP --> ADC["10 bit SAR ADC Pin A0"]
    GPIO --> SENS["External Sensors and Actuators"]
    WIFI --> AP["Wireless Network Access Point or Router"]
    AP --> CLOUD["Cloud Server MQTT or HTTP"]
```

## 4.2 Power and Programming Flow Diagram

This diagram illustrates how power flows into the PcDino and how firmware is uploaded.

```mermaid
graph LR
    PSU["USB 5V from PC or Charger"] --> LDO["AMS1117 3.3V LDO Regulator"]
    BAT["External 3.7V LiPo Battery via VIN"] --> LDO
    LDO --> ESPV["ESP8266 Core at 3.3V"]
    LDO --> GPIOV["GPIO Logic at 3.3V"]
    PC["Host PC with Arduino IDE"] --> UART["CH340 USB to UART Bridge"]
    UART --> ESPV
    ESPV --> FLASH["Flash Memory Stores Firmware"]
```

## 4.3 Typical IoT Application Flow Using PcDino

```mermaid
graph TD
    SENSOR["Analog or Digital Sensor LM35 DHT11 PIR"] --> READ["PcDino Reads Data via GPIO or ADC"]
    READ --> PROC["Local Processing on ESP8266"]
    PROC --> WIFI["WiFi Transmission via 802.11"]
    WIFI --> ROUTER["Home or Office Router"]
    ROUTER --> CLOUD["Cloud Platform ThingSpeak Firebase AWS IoT"]
    CLOUD --> DASH["User Dashboard on Mobile or Web"]
    DASH --> USER["End User Views or Acts on Data"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Block diagrams of IoT systems are **very frequently asked** in Part B questions (7–14 marks). The student should be able to draw a *3-layer IoT architecture* (Perception → Network → Application) and clearly place PcDino in the **Perception + Network layer**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: What is PcDino? Mention two of its key features.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**
PcDino is a compact, low-cost, **Arduino-compatible IoT development board** built around the **ESP8266 Wi-Fi SoC**. It integrates a 32-bit RISC processor, built-in 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi, GPIO pins, and a 10-bit ADC, all into a thumb-drive-sized form factor with a micro-USB interface for power and programming.

**Two key features:**
1. **Built-in Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 b/g/n)** — eliminates the need for an external Wi-Fi shield.
2. **Arduino IDE compatibility** — allows easy programming in C/C++ using a large library ecosystem.

### Question 2
**Q: List the various operating (power-saving) modes of the ESP8266 used in PcDino.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**
The ESP8266 (used in PcDino) supports four power modes:

1. **Active Mode** — CPU and Wi-Fi are fully ON; typical current ≈ 70 mA.
2. **Modem-Sleep Mode** — CPU is ON, Wi-Fi radio is OFF between DTIM beacon intervals.
3. **Light-Sleep Mode** — CPU is paused; resumes on GPIO interrupt or timer.
4. **Deep-Sleep Mode** — Only the RTC remains active; current ≈ **10 µA**; wake-up via GPIO reset or timer.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture of the PcDino board in detail with a neat block diagram. Mention its processor, memory, wireless, and peripheral specifications. **(7 Marks)**
**Q: (b)** Compare PcDino with Arduino Uno and Raspberry Pi in terms of processing power, Wi-Fi capability, operating voltage, and suitable applications. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
PcDino is a Wi-Fi-enabled IoT development board based on the **ESP8266 SoC**, designed for rapid prototyping of networked embedded applications.

**2. Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.)*
The diagram should show: USB → CH340 → ESP8266 → {CPU, SRAM, Flash, Wi-Fi Radio, GPIO, ADC}.

**3. Processor (1 mark):**
- **Tensilica L106 32-bit RISC** processor, default clock **80 MHz** (up to 160 MHz).

**4. Memory (1 mark):**
- **SRAM:** ~50 KB for user program.
- **Flash:** 1–4 MB external SPI flash for firmware.

**5. Wireless (1 mark):**
- **2.4 GHz Wi-Fi**, IEEE 802.11 b/g/n, supports STA, AP, and STA+AP modes.
- Security: WPA/WPA2.

**6. Peripherals (1 mark):**
- ~9 GPIO pins, 1× 10-bit ADC, UART, SPI, I²C (software), PWM (software).
- Powered via micro-USB using **CH340/CP2102** USB-to-UART bridge.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | PcDino | Arduino Uno | Raspberry Pi 4 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Processor | ESP8266, 32-bit, 80 MHz | ATmega328P, 8-bit, 16 MHz | Broadcom ARM Cortex-A72, 1.5 GHz |
| Wi-Fi | Built-in | Not available (needs shield) | Built-in |
| Operating Voltage | 3.3 V | 5 V | 5 V |
| Applications | Compact Wi-Fi IoT sensor nodes | Sensor-rich wired projects | Edge AI, gateways, image processing |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
PcDino is best suited for **compact, low-power, Wi-Fi-enabled IoT sensor nodes**, whereas Arduino Uno suits beginner-level wired embedded projects, and Raspberry Pi 4 is a fully-fledged mini-computer suitable for edge computing and AI applications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**Q: (a)** Describe the ADC subsystem of PcDino. With a suitable example, show how an analog sensor voltage is converted into a digital value. **(7 Marks)**
**Q: (b)** Explain the deep-sleep mode of the ESP8266 used in PcDino. A PcDino node operates 95% in deep-sleep and 5% in active mode. Given $I_{active} = 70 \text{ mA}$ and $I_{sleep} = 10 \, \mu A$, calculate the average current consumption and the theoretical battery life with a 2000 mAh battery. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**ADC Subsystem (3 marks):**
- PcDino uses the **internal 10-bit SAR ADC** of the ESP8266, available on pin **A0**.
- Input range: 0 to 3.3 V (using internal $V_{ref}$).
- Resolution: $V_{step} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{1023} \approx 3.22 \text{ mV}$.

**Conversion Formula (2 marks):**

$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^{N} - 1) \right\rfloor
$$

**Example (2 marks):**
For $V_{in} = 1.65$ V:
$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{1.65}{3.3} \times 1023 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 511.5 \right\rfloor = 511
$$

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Deep-Sleep Mode Description (3 marks):**
- In deep-sleep mode, **only the RTC** of the ESP8266 remains active.
- All other subsystems (CPU, Wi-Fi, flash) are powered down.
- Wake-up is triggered by a **GPIO low-pulse** (RST pin) or a **timer-based** wake-up.
- Typical current: **10 µA**.
- Code snippet logic: `ESP.deepSleep(time_in_microseconds)`.

**Average Current Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
I_{avg} = (0.05 \times 70 \text{ mA}) + (0.95 \times 0.01 \text{ mA})
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 3.5 + 0.0095 = 3.5095 \text{ mA}
$$

**Battery Life Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
t_{battery} = \frac{2000 \text{ mAh}}{3.5095 \text{ mA}} \approx 569.9 \text{ hours} \approx 23.7 \text{ days}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Voltage level mistake:** Students often write that PcDino operates at **5 V** like Arduino. This is wrong — PcDino GPIO is **3.3 V logic**. Connecting a 5 V sensor directly damages the chip. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **ADC reference voltage error:** Many students assume $V_{ref} = 5$ V for PcDino. The correct value is **3.3 V**. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **Confusing PcDino with NodeMCU:** PcDino and NodeMCU use the *same* ESP8266 chip, but differ in **form factor, pin count, and USB interface**. Examiners expect you to highlight the difference. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 4. **Forgetting the floor function in ADC:** The digital output is always an **integer**. Writing $D = 263.52$ instead of $D = 263$ is a frequent error. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Deep-sleep current units:** Writing $I_{sleep} = 10$ mA instead of $10 \, \mu A$ leads to a wildly wrong battery life calculation. **Lose 2 marks.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **PcDino** = ESP8266-based, compact, Arduino-compatible, Wi-Fi-enabled IoT board.
- **Processor:** Tensilica L106 32-bit, **80 MHz** (default).
- **Memory:** ~50 KB SRAM, **1–4 MB** SPI flash.
- **Wi-Fi:** **2.4 GHz**, IEEE 802.11 b/g/n.
- **Operating Voltage:** **3.3 V** (do NOT feed 5 V to GPIO).
- **ADC:** **1× 10-bit** SAR, range 0–3.3 V, $V_{step} \approx 3.22$ mV.
- **USB Bridge:** **CH340** or **CP2102** for serial programming.
- **Power Modes:** Active, Modem-Sleep, Light-Sleep, **Deep-Sleep (~10 µA)**.
- **ADC Formula:** $D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 1023 \right\rfloor$
- **Reverse Formula:** $V_{in} = \frac{D}{1023} \times 3.3$
- **Duty-Cycle Current:** $I_{avg} = D_{active} \cdot I_{active} + D_{sleep} \cdot I_{sleep}$
- **Battery Life:** $t_{battery} = \frac{C \text{ (mAh)}}{I_{avg} \text{ (mA)}}$
- **Programming Tool:** **Arduino IDE** (with ESP8266 board package).
- **Key Differentiator:** PcDino combines **MCU + Wi-Fi + USB programmer** in a single tiny board.
- **Best Use Case:** Low-cost, low-power, compact Wi-Fi IoT sensor nodes.
- **Common Confusion:** PcDino ≠ NodeMCU (different form factor, pin layout, and USB interface — but same core chip).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the **3-layer IoT architecture** (Perception → Network → Application) and place PcDino in the **Perception + Network layer**. This visual representation scores well in 7-mark and 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BeagleBone Black
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** BeagleBone Black

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BeagleBone Black (BBB)** is a low-cost, community-supported single-board computer (SBC) developed by the BeagleBoard.org Foundation. It is powered by the **Texas Instruments Sitara AM335x ARM Cortex-A8 processor** and is engineered specifically for embedded applications, IoT prototyping, and real-time processing tasks. It is officially classified as a *credit-card-sized* development platform that bridges the gap between microcontroller boards (like Arduino) and full-fledged personal computers.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** (PCAMT501 - Module 4: *IoT Hardware*), BeagleBone Black is recognized as a foundational **edge-computing node** — a device capable of running a full operating system while simultaneously interfacing with the physical world through its extensive GPIO headers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the BeagleBone Black as a "mini-laptop brain without a screen" for physical-world gadgets.**

Imagine your **smart home hub**. It needs to:
- Run a small operating system to schedule tasks (like a laptop).
- Connect to Wi-Fi and talk to your phone.
- Read sensors like temperature, motion, and light.
- Turn on/off appliances.

A regular laptop is overkill, expensive, and bulky. A simple microcontroller (like Arduino) cannot run a full OS or handle complex networking. The **BeagleBone Black sits perfectly in the middle**:

| Real-World Analogy | Hardware Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Brain of a small robot | AM3358 Processor |
| Short-term memory (RAM) | 512 MB DDR3 RAM |
| Long-term memory (Hard Disk) | 4 GB eMMC Flash |
| Hands and Fingers (to touch/feel world) | 65 GPIO Pins |
| Ears and Mouth (to listen/speak to other chips) | I2C, SPI, UART Buses |
| Eyes (to see environment) | ADC Inputs (Analog Sensors) |

## 1.3 Why BeagleBone Black is Important for IoT

> [!TIP]
> **Key takeaway for exams:** BeagleBone Black is preferred over Raspberry Pi in many *industrial* and *real-time* IoT applications because of its **PRU (Programmable Real-time Units)** — a feature that Raspberry Pi does NOT have.

The BeagleBone Black is widely used in:
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: PLC replacement, motor control.
- **Edge Computing**: Running AI/ML inference at the sensor node.
- **Robotics**: Real-time sensor fusion using PRU.
- **Smart Agriculture**: Soil moisture, weather monitoring stations.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the **board-critical specifications** that are repeatedly asked in KTU exams:

- **Processor Clock Speed:** **1 GHz** (AM3358BZCZ100 ARM Cortex-A8)
- **RAM:** **512 MB** DDR3 (typical Rev C)
- **Onboard Flash Storage:** **4 GB eMMC**
- **Operating Voltage:** **5 V DC** (via barrel jack or mini-USB)
- **GPIO Voltage Levels:** **3.3 V** (NOT 5V tolerant!)
- **Number of GPIO Pins:** **65** accessible through P8 and P9 headers
- **Analog Inputs:** **7 channels, 12-bit ADC** (0 to 1.8 V)
- **Weight:** Approximately **39.68 g** (1.4 oz)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students wrongly assume BeagleBone Black GPIO is 5V tolerant like Arduino. The BBB is strictly **3.3V logic** — applying 5V will permanently damage the board. This is a frequently tested MCQ concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Specifications

## 2.1 Operational Architecture Breakdown

The BeagleBone Black architecture can be decomposed into **five functional blocks**:

### Block 1: Central Processing Unit (CPU)
- **Chip:** Texas Instruments **Sitara AM335x** (specifically AM3358BZCZ100).
- **Core:** **ARM Cortex-A8** (32-bit RISC architecture).
- **Clock:** **1 GHz** default; can be software-overclocked with cooling.
- **Architecture:** ARMv7-A with NEON SIMD extensions and VFPv3 floating-point unit.

### Block 2: Memory Subsystem
- **L1 Cache:** 32 KB Instruction + 32 KB Data.
- **L2 Cache:** 256 KB unified.
- **Main RAM:** 512 MB DDR3L @ 400 MHz.
- **Persistent Storage:** 4 GB eMMC (8-bit MMC interface).
- **External Storage:** microSD card slot (up to 32 GB+ supported).

### Block 3: Programmable Real-time Units (PRU-ICSS)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **USP (Unique Selling Point)** of BeagleBone Black over Raspberry Pi.
- **Two PRU cores** (PRU0 and PRU1), each running at **200 MHz**.
- **32-bit RISC architecture** per PRU.
- **Deterministic timing** — single-cycle execution.
- **Used for:** Bit-banging protocols (custom I2C/SPI/UART), motor control, precise PWM, quadrature encoder reading.

### Block 4: Connectivity & Peripherals
- **USB:** 1× USB 2.0 Type-A (host), 1× mini-USB (client/power).
- **Ethernet:** 10/100 Mbps RJ45.
- **HDMI:** micro-HDMI output (audio + video).
- **Debug:** Serial debug port via USB-to-TTL (4-pin header).

### Block 5: Expansion Headers (P8 and P9)
- Two 46-pin dual-row headers providing:
  - 65 GPIO pins (multiplexed functions).
  - 7 analog inputs (1.8V reference).
  - 8 PWM outputs.
  - 4 UART, 2 I2C, 2 SPI buses.
  - Multiple ground and power pins (3.3V, 5V, VDD_ADC).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Specifications Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **exam gold** — memorize these for short-answer questions.

| Specification | Value | Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Processor | AM3358BZCZ100 | SoC | Must know exact part number |
| Core Architecture | ARM Cortex-A8 | 32-bit | Distinguishes from RPi (ARMv8 64-bit) |
| Clock Speed | 1000 | MHz | Frequently asked |
| RAM | 512 | MB | Rev C onwards; Rev B had 256MB |
| Flash (eMMC) | 4 | GB | Internal non-volatile storage |
| GPIO Count | 65 | pins | Across P8 + P9 headers |
| ADC Resolution | 12 | bits | 4096 discrete levels |
| ADC Channels | 7 | channels | AIN0 to AIN6 |
| ADC Reference Voltage | 1.8 | V | Crucial for voltage calculations |
| Logic Level | 3.3 | V | NOT 5V tolerant |
| PRU Cores | 2 | cores | Real-time processing |
| PRU Clock | 200 | MHz | Independent of main CPU |
| Operating Voltage | 5 | V DC | Barrel jack input |
| Power Consumption | ~1.8 to 4.0 | W | Depends on load |

## 2.3 Communication Protocols Supported

The BeagleBone Black natively supports all major IoT protocols through its pin multiplexer:

- **I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit):** 2 buses (I2C1, I2C2). Used for sensors like BMP280, MPU6050.
- **SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface):** 2 buses (SPI0, SPI1). High-speed device communication.
- **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter):** 4 UARTs for serial debugging and GPS modules.
- **CAN (Controller Area Network):** 2 CAN buses (industrial/automotive use).
- **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation):** 8 outputs for motor speed control, LED dimming.
- **GPIO:** General-purpose digital I/O.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**, the BBB serves as:
1. A **soft-PLC** — replacing expensive traditional PLCs in small automation cells.
2. An **edge gateway** — aggregating data from multiple Modbus sensors before sending to the cloud via MQTT.
3. A **machine vision processor** — using the PRU for high-speed camera triggering in QC lines.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The PRU-ICSS allows the BBB to achieve **sub-microsecond timing precision** — something Linux on the main CPU cannot guarantee due to scheduling jitter. This is why BBB is preferred for *deterministic* IoT applications.

## 2.5 Operating Systems Supported

| OS | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Debian (official)** | Default; best community support |
| **Ubuntu** | Cloud-native IoT applications |
| **Android** | Touchscreen-based IoT interfaces |
| **Windows 10 IoT Core** | Limited support |
| **Yocto / Buildroot** | Custom embedded Linux builds |
| **FreeRTOS (on PRU)** | Real-time control loops |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 ADC Resolution and Step-Size Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most frequently asked calculation in KTU exams involving BeagleBone Black.

The BeagleBone Black features a **12-bit successive-approximation ADC** with a reference voltage of **1.8 V**. We can derive the step-size (also called the **Least Significant Bit voltage, $V_{LSB}$**).

### Given Information
- ADC Resolution: $n = 12$ bits
- Number of discrete levels: $2^n$
- Reference Voltage: $V_{REF} = 1.8$ V

### Step 1: Total Number of Output Codes
The number of distinct output levels a 12-bit ADC can produce is:

$$
N = 2^n = 2^{12}
$$

Evaluating this:

$$
N = 4096 \text{ discrete codes}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Step-Size
The step-size is the smallest voltage difference the ADC can distinguish. It is given by:

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n}
$$

Substituting the known values:

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{1.8 \text{ V}}{4096}
$$

### Step 3: Final Numerical Evaluation

$$
V_{LSB} = 0.0004394 \text{ V} = 439.45 \text{ }\mu\text{V} \approx 439 \text{ }\mu\text{V}
$$

This means **every change of 1 in the ADC output corresponds to a voltage change of ~0.439 mV** at the input.

### Step 4: Digital Output for a Given Analog Voltage
For an analog input voltage $V_{in}$, the 12-bit digital output is given by:

$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \times 2^{12}}{V_{REF}} \right\rfloor
$$

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example

**Problem:** A temperature sensor outputs 1.25 V. Calculate the 12-bit ADC digital value on BeagleBone Black.

**Step 1:** Apply the formula:

$$
D = \frac{1.25 \times 4096}{1.8}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator:

$$
1.25 \times 4096 = 5120
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by reference voltage:

$$
D = \frac{5120}{1.8} = 2844.44
$$

**Step 4:** Take the floor value:

$$
D = 2844
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to hex (KTU often asks this):

$$
2844_{10} = \text{B1C}_{16}
$$

So the 12-bit ADC registers the value **0xB1C (2844 decimal)**.

## 3.3 Power Consumption Estimation

The BBB typically draws current $I_{typ} = 360$ mA at 5 V under light load.

### Step 1: Calculate Power

$$
P = V \times I
$$

### Step 2: Substitute values

$$
P = 5 \text{ V} \times 360 \text{ mA} = 1.8 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 3: Under full load (CPU + peripherals stressed), current can reach 800 mA:

$$
P_{max} = 5 \text{ V} \times 0.8 \text{ A} = 4.0 \text{ W}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always mention the operating conditions (light load vs full load) when quoting power consumption. A bare minimum answer mentioning "1.8 W typical" gets full marks.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: BBB vs Other Boards

| Parameter | BeagleBone Black | Raspberry Pi 4 | Arduino Uno |
|---|---|---|---|
| Processor | ARM Cortex-A8 (1 GHz) | ARM Cortex-A72 (1.5 GHz) | ATmega328P (16 MHz) |
| OS Capable | Yes (Linux) | Yes (Linux) | No (bare-metal) |
| Real-time Units | Yes (PRU-ICSS) | No | Limited |
| GPIO Count | 65 | 40 | 14 digital + 6 analog |
| ADC | 7 ch, 12-bit | No native ADC | 6 ch, 10-bit |
| RAM | 512 MB | 1–8 GB | 2 KB |
| Power | 5V, ~1.8W | 5V, ~3W | 5V/USB |
| Best Use | Industrial/Real-time IoT | General IoT, ML | Hobbyist, simple sensors |

## 3.5 Pin Multiplexing Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> Each P8/P9 pin supports up to 8 different functions. This is called **pin muxing**.

If a pin is configured in **Mode 7**, it acts as a GPIO. Other modes activate peripherals (UART, I2C, SPI, etc.). The configuration is controlled by the **Control Module** registers in the AM3358 SoC, accessed through memory-mapped I/O.

For example, **P9_11** can serve as:
- Mode 0: UART4_RXD
- Mode 1: I2C4_SCL
- Mode 7: GPIO0_30 (default)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BeagleBone Black Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Power["5V DC Input\nBarrel Jack or USB"] --> PMIC["TPS65217C\nPower Management IC"]
    PMIC --> CPU["AM3358 SoC\nARM Cortex-A8 @ 1 GHz"]
    PMIC --> RAM["512 MB DDR3\n400 MHz"]
    PMIC --> eMMC["4 GB eMMC Flash\nInternal Storage"]
    PMIC --> HDMI["micro-HDMI\nAudio + Video Out"]
    CPU --> PRU1["PRU0\n200 MHz"]
    CPU --> PRU2["PRU1\n200 MHz"]
    CPU --> GPIO["P8 + P9 Headers\n65 GPIO Pins"]
    GPIO --> PwmOut["PWM Outputs x8"]
    GPIO --> AdcIn["ADC Inputs x7\n12-bit 1.8V"]
    GPIO --> I2C["I2C Bus x2"]
    GPIO --> SPI["SPI Bus x2"]
    GPIO --> UART["UART x4"]
    CPU --> Ethernet["10/100 Mbps\nEthernet PHY"]
    CPU --> USBHost["USB 2.0 Type-A\nHost Port"]
    CPU --> SD["microSD\nCard Slot"]
    PMIC --> LED["Status LEDs\nUSR0-USR3, PWR, Heartbeat"]
```

## 4.2 BeagleBone Black Boot Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PowerOn["Power On\n5V Applied"] --> RomCode["Read ROM Code\n(stage 0 bootloader)"]
    RomCode --> CheckBoot["Check Boot Order\nDefault: eMMC first"]
    CheckBoot --> MLO["Load MLO\n(stage 1 SPL bootloader)"]
    MLO --> Uboot["Load U-Boot\n(stage 2 bootloader)"]
    Uboot --> Kernel["Load Linux Kernel\n(Debian/Ubuntu)"]
    Kernel --> RootFS["Mount RootFS\n(ext4 partition)"]
    RootFS --> Services["Start systemd\nInit Services"]
    Services --> Userland["Userland Ready\nLogin Prompt / Cloud Agent"]
    Userland --> IoTApp["Run IoT Application\nMQTT, Sensors, Actuators"]
```

## 4.3 IoT Data Acquisition Pipeline using BBB

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sensor["Physical Sensor\nTemp, Humidity, Gas"] --> SignalCond["Signal Conditioning\nAmplifier / Filter"]
    SignalCond --> Adc["BBB ADC\n7 Channels 12-bit"]
    Adc --> PRU["PRU Real-time\nSampling"]
    PRU --> Linux["Linux User Space\nPython / C Application"]
    Linux --> MqttClient["MQTT Client\nPaho Library"]
    MqttClient --> Broker["Cloud MQTT Broker\nMosquitto / AWS IoT"]
    Broker --> Dashboard["Web Dashboard\nNode-RED / Grafana"]
    Dashboard --> User["End User\nMobile / PC"]
```

## 4.4 P8 and P9 Header Pin Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P8Header["P8 Header - 46 Pins (Top Row + Bottom Row)"]
        P8_1["P8_01: GND"]
        P8_2["P8_02: GND"]
        P8_3["P8_03: GPIO1_6 (Mode 7)"]
        P8_4["P8_04: GPIO1_7 (Mode 7)"]
        P8_5["P8_05: GPIO1_2"]
        P8_6["P8_06: GPIO1_3"]
    end
    subgraph P9Header["P9 Header - 46 Pins (Digital + Analog + Power)"]
        P9_1["P9_01: GND"]
        P9_2["P9_02: GND"]
        P9_3["P9_03: 3.3V DC"]
        P9_4["P9_04: 3.3V DC"]
        P9_5["P9_05: 5V VDD"]
        P9_6["P9_06: 5V VDD"]
        P9_33["P9_33: AIN4 (Analog)"]
        P9_35["P9_35: AIN6 (Analog)"]
        P9_36["P9_36: AIN5 (Analog)"]
        P9_37["P9_37: AIN2 (Analog)"]
        P9_38["P9_38: AIN3 (Analog)"]
        P9_39["P9_39: AIN0 (Analog)"]
        P9_40["P9_40: AIN1 (Analog)"]
    end
    P8Header --> P9Header
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a *representative* subset. The actual P8 and P9 headers each have 46 pins (23 + 23). Students are expected to know the *location of analog pins* and *power pins* specifically.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List any THREE features of the BeagleBone Black board.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **ARM Cortex-A8** processor @ **1 GHz** (Sitara AM3358 SoC).
2. **65 GPIO pins** distributed across P8 and P9 headers, with **3.3V** logic level.
3. **2× PRU (Programmable Real-time Units)** running at **200 MHz** for deterministic real-time tasks.
4. *(Optional additional point)*: 7-channel, 12-bit ADC with 1.8V reference voltage.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of PRU-ICSS in BeagleBone Black?**

**Model Answer:**
PRU-ICSS (Programmable Real-time Unit – Industrial Communication Sub-System) consists of **two 32-bit RISC cores** (PRU0 and PRU1) operating at **200 MHz**, independent of the main ARM CPU. Its significance is:
1. Provides **deterministic, single-cycle instruction execution** for real-time control.
2. Enables **bit-banging** of custom communication protocols (e.g., WS2812 LEDs, custom I2C timings).
3. Allows **parallel processing** — main CPU runs Linux, PRUs handle time-critical sensor reads.
4. Eliminates **Linux kernel jitter**, making BBB suitable for **industrial automation** and **motor control**.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never** mention "5V GPIO" — it is a 3.3V logic board. Marks deducted.
> - **Always** mention the PRU when differentiating from Raspberry Pi. Examiners award bonus marks for this.
> - **Voltage divider** is mandatory when connecting 5V sensors to ADC pins (1.8V max).
> - Sub-questions (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**. Ensure equal depth.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

#### **(a) Explain the architecture of BeagleBone Black with a neat block diagram. List any 5 hardware specifications.** [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
The BeagleBone Black (BBB) is a single-board computer developed by BeagleBoard.org, featuring the Texas Instruments **Sitara AM3358** system-on-chip (SoC) based on the **ARM Cortex-A8** core. It is designed for IoT, embedded, and real-time applications.

**Architecture Block Diagram (3 marks):**
*(See SECTION 4.1 for the Mermaid diagram — should be redrawn on paper as a block diagram with arrows showing:*

- **AM3358 SoC** as the central processing unit, connected to:
  - **512 MB DDR3 RAM** via a 32-bit memory bus.
  - **4 GB eMMC Flash** for internal storage.
  - **Power Management IC (TPS65217C)** providing 5V/3.3V/1.8V rails.
  - **P8 and P9 headers** (GPIO + ADC + PWM).
  - **PRU-ICSS** (PRU0 + PRU1) as co-processors.
  - **USB, Ethernet, HDMI, microSD** peripherals.

**Five Hardware Specifications (2 marks):**
1. CPU: ARM Cortex-A8, 1 GHz.
2. RAM: 512 MB DDR3.
3. GPIO: 65 pins @ 3.3V logic.
4. ADC: 7-channel, 12-bit, 1.8V reference.
5. PRU: 2 cores @ 200 MHz.

---

#### **(b) With a suitable example, explain how the ADC of BeagleBone Black can be used to read an analog sensor. Calculate the digital output for an input voltage of 1.2 V.** [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
The BBB has a **12-bit successive-approximation ADC** with **7 input channels (AIN0–AIN6)** accessible via the P9 header. The reference voltage is **1.8 V**. The ADC converts an analog voltage into a digital value between **0 and 4095**.

**Example (2 marks):**
A **LM35 temperature sensor** outputs **10 mV/°C**. To measure room temperature (~25°C), the sensor outputs **0.25 V**. This voltage is fed to **AIN0 (P9_39)**. The BBB samples it and produces a 12-bit digital value.

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Step 1: Calculate step-size:

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{12}} = \frac{1.8}{4096} = 0.0004394 \text{ V}
$$

Step 2: Calculate digital output for 1.2 V:

$$
D = \frac{1.2}{0.0004394} = 2730.99 \approx 2730
$$

Step 3: Convert to hex:

$$
2730_{10} = 0\text{xAAA}
$$

**Conclusion:** The ADC produces digital value **2730 (0xAAA)** for an input of 1.2 V.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

#### **(a) Compare BeagleBone Black with Raspberry Pi and Arduino Uno. Justify the use of BBB in real-time IoT applications.** [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | BeagleBone Black | Raspberry Pi 4 | Arduino Uno |
|---|---|---|---|
| Processor | ARM Cortex-A8 @ 1 GHz | ARM Cortex-A72 @ 1.5 GHz | ATmega328P @ 16 MHz |
| OS | Linux (Debian) | Linux (Raspbian) | None (bare metal) |
| Real-time | Yes (PRU @ 200 MHz) | No | Limited (interrupts) |
| GPIO | 65 @ 3.3V | 40 @ 3.3V | 14 digital + 6 analog @ 5V |
| ADC | 7 ch, 12-bit | None | 6 ch, 10-bit |
| RAM | 512 MB | 1–8 GB | 2 KB |
| Best For | Industrial, real-time | General purpose, ML | Hobbyist, education |

**Justification for Real-time IoT (3 marks):**
1. The **PRU-ICSS** enables sub-microsecond deterministic control — **impossible** on Raspberry Pi due to Linux scheduling jitter.
2. Industrial-grade **CAN bus** support makes BBB ideal for **automotive IoT**.
3. Low-level **pin muxing** allows precise timing for protocols like **DMX512, WS2812 LEDs, stepper motors**.
4. Real-world example: A **solar tracker** uses BBB's PRU to read a sun-position sensor and adjust motor angle in real-time.

---

#### **(b) Describe the boot process of BeagleBone Black from power-on to userland. What is the role of the MLO file?** [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Boot Process Stages (5 marks):**
1. **Power On:** 5V applied → PMIC (TPS65217C) generates voltage rails.
2. **ROM Code (Stage 0):** Internal ROM reads the SYSBOOT configuration pins to determine boot source (default: eMMC → microSD → USB).
3. **MLO Loading (Stage 1):** The first-stage bootloader (MLO = *Memory Loader*) is loaded into internal RAM. MLO initializes DDR memory and clocks.
4. **U-Boot (Stage 2):** MLO loads the second-stage bootloader, **U-Boot**, which presents an interactive command prompt (3-second delay by default).
5. **Kernel + RootFS:** U-Boot loads the **Linux kernel image (zImage)** and **device tree blob (DTB)**, then mounts the **root filesystem (rootfs)**.
6. **systemd init:** Linux kernel starts the **systemd** init system, launching services like SSH, network manager, and user IoT applications.
7. **Userland Ready:** Login prompt appears via HDMI/serial. User can now run Python/C apps for IoT.

**Role of MLO (2 marks):**
The **MLO (Memory Loader)** is the **first-stage bootloader** of BeagleBone Black. Its functions are:
- **Initialize DDR RAM** and configure the external memory controller.
- **Set up the system clocks** to bring the SoC to operational frequency.
- **Load the next-stage bootloader (U-Boot)** from boot media into RAM.
- It is named "MLO" because of the **MS-DOS 8.3 filename limitation** of the FAT boot partition.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (10 Bullets):**
>
> 1. ✅ BBB uses **AM3358 ARM Cortex-A8 @ 1 GHz** — NOT Raspberry Pi's ARM Cortex-A72.
> 2. ✅ GPIO is **3.3V logic** — applying 5V **damages** the board.
> 3. ✅ Has **65 GPIO** pins across **P8 and P9** headers.
> 4. ✅ ADC is **12-bit, 7-channel, 1.8V reference** → $V_{LSB} \approx 439 \text{ }\mu\text{V}$.
> 5. ✅ **PRU-ICSS** = 2 cores @ 200 MHz each = **the killer feature** for real-time IoT.
> 6. ✅ **PRU enables deterministic timing** impossible on stock Linux.
> 7. ✅ RAM = **512 MB DDR3**; eMMC = **4 GB** (Rev C).
> 8. ✅ **MLO** = First-stage bootloader; **U-Boot** = Second-stage bootloader.
> 9. ✅ Supports **I2C × 2, SPI × 2, UART × 4, CAN × 2, PWM × 8**.
> 10. ✅ **Voltage divider is mandatory** for sensors with output > 1.8V (e.g., 5V analog).
>
> **🔥 Frequent Numerical:** Calculate 12-bit ADC output for any given analog voltage using $D = \frac{V_{in} \times 4096}{1.8}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CubieBoard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CubieBoard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CubieBoard** is a family of **open-source, low-power, single-board computers (SBCs)** designed and manufactured by **CubieTech (Shenzhen, China)**, intended for embedded computing, IoT prototyping, edge gateways, and educational applications. The CubieBoard family is based on **Allwinner series System-on-Chips (SoCs)** built around **ARM Cortex-A series RISC processors**, and it executes standard operating systems such as **Linux (Ubuntu, Debian, Fedora, Arch)**, **Android**, and lightweight RTOS variants.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 syllabus for Internet of Things**, the CubieBoard is categorized as a **microprocessor-based edge computing platform** (as opposed to microcontroller boards like Arduino). It serves as an **IoT Gateway / Edge Node** that aggregates data from distributed sensors, performs local computation, and relays processed information to cloud back-ends via standard IP protocols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> CubieBoard is taught as a representative **microprocessor-based IoT hardware platform**. Students must distinguish it from microcontroller boards (Arduino, NodeMCU) and understand its role as a *Linux-capable edge device* capable of running full network stacks (TCP/IP, MQTT, HTTP, CoAP).

## 1.2 The CubieBoard Family — Generations at a Glance

| Version | SoC | CPU Architecture | Launch Era |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CubieBoard 1** | Allwinner A10 | Single-core ARM Cortex-A8 @ 1 GHz | 2012 |
| **CubieBoard 2** | Allwinner A20 | Dual-core ARM Cortex-A7 @ 1 GHz | 2013 |
| **CubieBoard 4 (CC-A80)** | Allwinner A80 | Quad-core ARM Cortex-A15/A7 (big.LITTLE) | 2014 |
| **CubieBoard 5 (CubieTruck)** | Allwinner H8 | Octa-core ARM Cortex-A7 @ 2 GHz | 2014 |
| **CubieBoard 6** | Allwinner H6 | Quad-core ARM Cortex-A53 @ 1.8 GHz | 2017 |
| **CubieBoard 7** | Allwinner H6 / H5 | Quad-core ARM Cortex-A53 | 2018+ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mini-Desktop" of IoT

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of CubieBoard as a **credit-card-sized personal computer (PC)** with a brain (CPU), memory (RAM), long-term storage (microSD/SATA), and communication ports (Ethernet, USB, WiFi). If an **Arduino** is like a calculator — excellent for one specific repetitive job — then a **CubieBoard is like a full laptop**: it can run a complete operating system, open web browsers, host servers, play video, and simultaneously talk to dozens of sensors. In IoT terms, **Arduino sits next to the sensor; CubieBoard sits at the gateway where the LAN meets the WAN.**

## 1.4 Position of CubieBoard in the IoT Architecture Stack

```
[ Sensor / Actuator ]  --->  [ Microcontroller (Arduino) ]
                                      |
                                      v
                          ┌──────────────────────┐
                          │   CUBIEBOARD (EDGE)  │  <-- THIS DEVICE
                          │  - Runs Linux         │
                          │  - MQTT Broker        │
                          │  - Local DB (SQLite)  │
                          └──────────┬───────────┘
                                     |
                                     v
                          [ Cloud / Dashboard ]
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture — Block-by-Block

The CubieBoard follows the **System-on-Chip (SoC)** design philosophy, where the CPU, GPU, memory controller, and I/O peripherals are integrated into a single silicon die. The reference architecture (exemplified by **CubieBoard 2 / Allwinner A20**) consists of:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU):** Dual-core ARM Cortex-A7. Implements the **ARMv7-A** instruction set, supports **NEON SIMD** extensions, hardware floating-point (VFPv4), and **Thumb-2** compressed instructions.
2. **Graphics Processing Unit (GPU):** ARM Mali-400 MP2 — handles 2D/3D acceleration, OpenGL ES 2.0.
3. **System Memory (RAM):** **1 GB DDR3** (some variants ship with 2 GB) clocked typically at **480 MHz**, providing a peak bandwidth of approximately **3.84 GB/s**.
4. **Storage Subsystem:**
   - microSD card slot (up to **64 GB** typically supported).
   - **SATA 2.0** interface (CubieBoard 1, 2, CubieTruck) — provides **3 Gbps** raw bandwidth, enabling connection of HDDs/SSDs. This is a major differentiator from Raspberry Pi (pre-3).
5. **Network Interfaces:**
   - **10/100 Mbps Ethernet** (via integrated PHY on A20).
   - Optional **WiFi 802.11 b/g/n** via USB dongle or soldered module.
6. **USB Subsystem:** Two **USB 2.0 Host** ports + one **USB 2.0 OTG** port.
7. **Display/Video:** **HDMI 1.4** (1080p @ 60 Hz), VGA (CubieBoard 2 only), composite video.
8. **Audio:** 3.5 mm headphone jack, microphone input, SPDIF optical out.
9. **GPIO Header:** A 54-pin dual-row header exposing I2C, SPI, UART, PWM, CAN (on certain SoCs), and general-purpose digital I/O.
10. **Power Input:** **5 V DC** via barrel jack or micro-USB; typical consumption **700 mA – 2 A** depending on load.

## 2.2 GPIO Pin Map — Engineering Reference

The 54-pin GPIO header on CubieBoard 2 is organized as:

| Pin Range | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **1 – 2** | 5 V power out |
| **3 – 4** | 3.3 V power out |
| **5 – 6** | Ground (GND) |
| **7 – 18** | GPIO (general-purpose digital) |
| **19, 21, 23, 24** | I2C-1 (TWI) — SCL, SDA |
| **22, 26, 36, 38, 40** | UART (serial debug) |
| **25, 27** | SPI-0 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, CS) |
| **28, 29, 31, 33** | PWM outputs |
| **37, 39** | ADC inputs (1.8 V tolerant) |
| **41 – 46** | LCD / LVDS signals |
| **47 – 54** | CSI (camera serial interface) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Caveat:** CubieBoard GPIO is **3.3 V logic-level** (NOT 5 V tolerant). Connecting a 5 V signal directly to a GPIO pin will permanently damage the SoC. Always use a logic-level converter when interfacing with 5 V devices (e.g., legacy Arduino Uno).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Electrical Power Budget | $P = V \cdot I$ | $P = 5\text{ V} \cdot 2\text{ A} = 10\text{ W}$ | Power-supply sizing |
| 2 | DDR3 Peak Bandwidth | $BW = 2 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot \tfrac{n_{bytes}}{n_{cycles}}$ | $\approx 3.84$ GB/s | Memory throughput estimation |
| 3 | SATA-2 Raw Throughput | $R_{SATA2} = 3$ Gbps $\approx 300$ MB/s (after 8b/10b) | $300$ MB/s | Storage I/O ceiling |
| 4 | GPIO Sink/Source Current | $I_{GPIO}$ | $\pm 8$ mA (typical), $\pm 16$ mA (max) per pin | LED / relay drive design |
| 5 | ADC Resolution | $V_{LSB} = \tfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | $\tfrac{1.8\text{ V}}{1023} \approx 1.76$ mV | Analog sensor quantization |
| 6 | Sampling Theorem (ADC) | $f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | Anti-aliasing design |
| 7 | Ethernet Frame Throughput | $R = \tfrac{L_{payload}}{t_{frame}}$ | $\leq 11.9$ MB/s @ 100 Mbps | Network capacity planning |
| 8 | CPU Utilization (Little's Law) | $U = \lambda \cdot T_{s}$ | dimensionless | Real-time scheduling margin |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pipe-Safe Convention:** For absolute-value expressions inside any row, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never the bare pipe `|` — to prevent markdown-table syntax corruption.

## 2.4 Why and How — Operational Logic

**Why use CubieBoard as an IoT gateway?**
- **Full OS support** enables installation of standard network daemons (`mosquitto` for MQTT, `nginx` for HTTP, `Node-RED` for flow-based programming).
- **SATA interface** allows local database hosting (PostgreSQL, InfluxDB) for buffered storage during cloud outages.
- **Hardware floating-point** accelerates numeric IoT workloads (FFT, edge ML inference).

**How does data flow inside a CubieBoard-based IoT node?**

1. **Sensor acquisition** — A peripheral (e.g., DHT22) sends a digital pulse train on a GPIO pin; kernel driver reads it via `sysfs` or character device.
2. **User-space processing** — A Python/Node.js daemon interprets the raw value, applies calibration, and timestamps it.
3. **Local persistence** — Data is written to SQLite/InfluxDB on the SATA drive.
4. **Network publish** — Daemon connects to local `mosquitto` broker via `mqtt://localhost:1883`, then the broker fans out to cloud subscribers via TLS on port `8883`.
5. **Actuator feedback** — Cloud-issued commands arrive on a subscribed topic; daemon toggles a relay via the `libgpiod` library.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Smart Agriculture** | Edge gateway collecting soil-moisture & climate data, running local irrigation control logic |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | OPC-UA / Modbus-TCP aggregation in factory cells |
| **Smart Home** | Home Assistant / OpenHAB hub |
| **Retail Analytics** | In-store people counting with on-device anonymization |
| **Education** | Low-cost Linux teaching platform for embedded-systems labs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Power Budget for a CubieBoard IoT Node

A CubieBoard 2 (CubieBoard 2 = Allwinner A20) is being deployed as an edge gateway connected to: **3 × DHT22 sensors, 1 × USB WiFi dongle, 1 × 2.5-inch SATA HDD, 1 × HDMI monitor (during setup only)**. Verify the power budget if powered by a **5 V, 3 A SMPS**.

**Given Data:**
- CubieBoard 2 idle current: **$I_{idle} = 0.45$ A**
- CubieBoard 2 loaded CPU + SATA: **$I_{load} = 1.20$ A**
- DHT22 each: **$I_{DHT} = 2.5$ mA**
- USB WiFi dongle: **$I_{wifi} = 0.30$ A**
- 2.5-inch HDD idle: **$I_{HDD-idle} = 0.40$ A**, spin-up: **$I_{HDD-su} = 1.00$ A**
- HDMI monitor (setup only): **$I_{mon} = 0.50$ A**
- Supply: **$V_{s} = 5$ V**, rated **$I_{rated} = 3$ A**

**Step 1 — Sum worst-case continuous load (excluding monitor):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{total} &= I_{load} + 3 \cdot I_{DHT} + I_{wifi} + I_{HDD\text{-}su} \\
&= 1.20 + 3 \cdot 0.0025 + 0.30 + 1.00 \\
&= 1.20 + 0.0075 + 0.30 + 1.00 \\
&= 2.5075 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute required power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{required} &= V_s \cdot I_{total} \\
&= 5 \cdot 2.5075 \\
&= 12.54 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare against the 5 V × 3 A supply:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{supply} &= 5 \cdot 3 = 15 \text{ W} \\
\text{Margin} &= P_{supply} - P_{required} = 15 - 12.54 = 2.46 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
Margin $\approx$ 16 % — acceptable, but tight. **Recommendation:** Use a 5 V / **4 A** SMPS to provide a **> 30 % safety margin** for HDD inrush and temperature derating.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — ADC Quantization for a Soil-Moisture Sensor

The CubieBoard 2 ADC reference is **$V_{ref} = 1.8$ V**, resolution **$n = 10$ bits**. A capacitive soil sensor outputs **$V_{out} = 1.32$ V** for a moist sample. Find the **digital code** and the **quantization step**.

**Step 1 — Quantization step (LSB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LSB} &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} \\
&= \frac{1.8}{2^{10} - 1} \\
&= \frac{1.8}{1023} \\
&\approx 1.7595 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \\
&\approx 1.76 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Digital code (round-down / floor for truncation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \left\lfloor \frac{V_{out}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor \\
&= \left\lfloor \frac{1.32}{1.7595 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor \\
&= \left\lfloor 750.21 \right\rfloor \\
&= 750
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Reconstructed voltage from code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{recon} &= D \cdot V_{LSB} = 750 \cdot 1.7595 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1.3196 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Quantization error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_q &= V_{out} - V_{recon} = 1.32 - 1.3196 = 0.4 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

The maximum possible quantization error is bounded by $\pm \tfrac{V_{LSB}}{2} \approx \pm 0.88$ mV, which for a soil sensor is negligible.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Throughput Ceiling for MQTT Telemetry

The gateway publishes one JSON message of **$L = 256$ bytes** per sensor per second. There are **$N = 10$ sensors**. The MQTT broker runs locally over **TCP**. Compute the minimum Ethernet bandwidth required, and check whether the 100 Mbps link is sufficient.

**Step 1 — Raw application data rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{app} &= N \cdot L \cdot 8 \text{ bits} \\
&= 10 \cdot 256 \cdot 8 \\
&= 20{,}480 \text{ bps} \approx 20.5 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Account for TCP/IP overhead:**
Ethernet preamble + SFD = 8 bytes, IP header = 20 bytes, TCP header = 20 bytes, Ethernet trailer (FCS) = 4 bytes. Total L2 overhead per frame $\approx$ 54 bytes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{wire} &= R_{app} \cdot \frac{L + 54}{L} \\
&= 20{,}480 \cdot \frac{256 + 54}{256} \\
&= 20{,}480 \cdot 1.211 \\
&\approx 24{,}801 \text{ bps} \\
&\approx 24.8 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Utilization check on 100 Mbps link:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{R_{wire}}{R_{link}} = \frac{24.8 \times 10^{3}}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 2.48 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\Rightarrow 0.0248 \text{ \% utilization}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Plenty of headroom — Ethernet is not the bottleneck. The CPU and MQTT broker queue will dominate latency at this scale.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Map

| Engineering Decision | Option A | Option B | Trade-off |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Storage | microSD | SATA HDD | Speed vs. capacity vs. vibration tolerance |
| OS | Debian Server | Ubuntu Core | LTS stability vs. snap ecosystem |
| Connectivity | Wired Ethernet | WiFi dongle | Reliability vs. mobility |
| MQTT library | Mosquitto C | Paho Python | Performance vs. development speed |
| Security | TLS only | TLS + device cert | Compute cost vs. mutual auth |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
