# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between CubieBoard and Arduino Uno as IoT platforms. Mention at least three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Arduino Uno | CubieBoard 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Category** | Microcontroller (MCU) | Microprocessor (MPU) |
| **OS Support** | None (bare-metal firmware) | Linux / Android |
| **Clock Speed** | 16 MHz | 1000 MHz (1 GHz) |
| **RAM** | 2 KB SRAM | 1 GB DDR3 |
| **Network** | Requires external shield | Built-in 10/100 Ethernet |
| **Programming** | C/C++ via AVR toolchain | Python, Node.js, C, shell scripting |
| **Role in IoT** | Sensor / actuator node | Gateway / edge server |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**List the major hardware interfaces available on CubieBoard 2 and explain the role of any two in an IoT application.**

**Model Answer:**
CubieBoard 2 provides: **GPIO (54-pin header), 2 × USB 2.0 Host, 1 × USB 2.0 OTG, HDMI 1.4, VGA, 10/100 Ethernet, SATA 2.0, microSD, 3.5 mm audio, SPDIF, I2C, SPI, UART, PWM, ADC**.

**Role of two interfaces in IoT:**
1. **GPIO header:** Direct connection of digital sensors (DHT22) and actuators (relays). The SoC's GPIO controller exposes pins as character-device files in Linux (`/dev/gpiochip0`).
2. **SATA 2.0:** Connects an HDD/SSD for local persistent storage of time-series sensor data, allowing operation during WAN outages.
3. **Ethernet:** Provides reliable wired IP connectivity for MQTT/HTTP traffic to cloud brokers.
4. **I2C bus:** Allows multi-device, addressable serial communication with sensors (e.g., BH1750 light sensor) using only two wires.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Compliance:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, divided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Both alternatives below are *independent full questions* — students answer ONE.

### Option A — 14 Marks

**Question A [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]:**

**(a)** Explain the internal architecture of the CubieBoard 2 with the help of a block diagram. Highlight the role of the **Allwinner A20 SoC**, **DDR3 memory controller**, and **SATA 2.0 interface** in supporting IoT gateway functions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The CubieBoard 2 is built around the **Allwinner A20 SoC**, which integrates a **dual-core ARM Cortex-A7 CPU**, a **Mali-400 MP2 GPU**, and dedicated controllers for memory, storage, USB, Ethernet, and GPIO.

- **Allwinner A20 SoC:** Acts as the heart of the board. Its **ARM Cortex-A7 cores** execute the Linux kernel, user-space daemons (MQTT broker, Node-RED), and Python/Node.js IoT scripts. The SoC integrates the **memory controller**, **GPIO controller**, and **Ethernet MAC**, reducing external chip count.
- **DDR3 Memory Controller:** Provides the CPU with high-bandwidth access to a **1 GB DDR3** RAM module clocked at **480 MHz**, yielding a peak bandwidth of **~3.84 GB/s**. This bandwidth is critical when the gateway is concurrently parsing JSON, running an MQTT broker, and writing to a local database.
- **SATA 2.0 Interface:** Offers **3 Gbps** raw (≈ **300 MB/s** after 8b/10b encoding) bandwidth for connecting an external HDD/SSD. In IoT applications this enables **local buffering** of sensor data when the WAN is down, supporting offline analytics and store-and-forward semantics.

The block diagram (see SECTION 4.1) shows how power, memory, storage, and I/O subsystems are interconnected.

**Block-diagram description for answer sheet:**
Draw the SoC at the centre with arrows to RAM (DDR3), Storage (microSD + SATA HDD), and I/O (GPIO, USB, Ethernet, HDMI). Label voltages and clock domains.

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe how a CubieBoard can be deployed as an **IoT edge gateway** in a smart-agriculture scenario. Discuss the role of **MQTT protocol**, **local database**, and **cloud connectivity**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In a **smart-agriculture** deployment, multiple **sensor nodes** (Arduino-based) measure **soil moisture, temperature, humidity, and light intensity** across a field. Each Arduino transmits readings over **UART / XBee / LoRa** to a central **CubieBoard 2** placed in a weatherproof enclosure.

The CubieBoard runs an **MQTT broker** (e.g., `mosquitto`), a **Node-RED flow** for visualization, and a **Python daemon** that performs data fusion and threshold-based irrigation control.

**Role of MQTT:**
MQTT is a **lightweight publish/subscribe protocol** running over TCP port **1883** (or 8883 for TLS). It is ideal for low-bandwidth, intermittent links. The Python daemon **publishes** sensor readings to topics like `farm/zone1/moisture`, and **subscribes** to `farm/zone1/valve` to receive actuator commands.

**Role of local database:**
A **SQLite or InfluxDB** instance on the SATA HDD provides **time-series persistence**. Data is written at 1 Hz; even if the WAN link drops, hours of historical data remain intact for later synchronization.

**Role of cloud connectivity:**
The MQTT broker forwards selected topics over **TLS-encrypted WebSockets** to **AWS IoT Core / Azure IoT Hub / ThingsBoard**. From there, **Grafana dashboards** render analytics and **mobile push notifications** alert farmers of anomalies.

```
[Sensors] → [Arduino] → UART → [CubieBoard: MQTT broker + DB]
                                       |
                                       └──── TLS ────> [Cloud → Grafana → Mobile]
```

---

### Option B — 14 Marks

**Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]:**

**(a)** Compare the **CubieBoard** with the **Raspberry Pi** as IoT platforms. Discuss their processor architectures, I/O capabilities, and operating-system support. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | CubieBoard 2 | Raspberry Pi 3 Model B |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SoC** | Allwinner A20 | Broadcom BCM2837 |
| **CPU** | Dual-core ARM Cortex-A7 @ 1 GHz | Quad-core ARM Cortex-A53 @ 1.2 GHz |
| **Architecture** | ARMv7-A (32-bit) | ARMv8-A (64-bit capable) |
| **RAM** | 1 GB DDR3 | 1 GB LPDDR2 |
| **Storage** | microSD + **SATA 2.0** | microSD only |
| **Network** | 10/100 Ethernet | 10/100 Ethernet + WiFi + Bluetooth |
| **USB** | 2 × USB 2.0 Host + 1 OTG | 4 × USB 2.0 Host + 1 OTG (via hub) |
| **GPIO** | 54 pins (3.3 V logic) | 40 pins (3.3 V logic) |
| **Display** | HDMI + VGA | HDMI only |
| **Community** | Moderate (industrial / maker) | Very large (educational) |

**Discussion:**
- The Raspberry Pi 3 is **64-bit capable** with built-in wireless, making it more attractive for educational and prototyping use.
- The CubieBoard's **SATA interface** is a decisive advantage in industrial IoT gateways that require **reliable local storage of gigabytes to terabytes** of telemetry data.
- For pure learning and rapid prototyping, Raspberry Pi's ecosystem is superior; for industrial-grade edge gateways requiring deterministic Linux and storage, CubieBoard has merit.

---

**(b)** Describe the **GPIO interfacing procedure** for connecting a **DHT22 temperature-humidity sensor** to a CubieBoard. Include the electrical specifications, pin assignments, and a typical Linux read flow. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Electrical Specifications of DHT22:**
- Operating voltage: **3.3 V – 5 V DC**
- Logic level: **3.3 V** (matches CubieBoard GPIO)
- Max current: **2.5 mA**
- Output: single-wire open-drain, requires **10 kΩ pull-up** to VCC
- Communication: proprietary single-wire protocol (not 1-Wire)

**Step 2 — Pin Assignment on CubieBoard 2:**
- DHT22 VCC → Pin 1 (**5 V** or Pin 3 — 3.3 V)
- DHT22 DATA → Pin 7 (**GPIO0**) with 10 kΩ pull-up
- DHT22 GND → Pin 5 (**GND**)

**Step 3 — Linux Read Flow:**
1. Configure GPIO0 as output, drive low for ≥ 1 ms (host-start signal).
2. Switch GPIO0 to input; the pull-up brings line HIGH.
3. DHT22 takes control of the line, sends an 80 µs LOW then 80 µs HIGH response.
4. DHT22 transmits **40 bits** = 16-bit humidity + 16-bit temperature + 8-bit checksum.
5. Each data bit is encoded as **50 µs LOW + 26–28 µs HIGH (for '0')** or **70 µs HIGH (for '1')**.
6. Sample line at 50 µs after each rising edge to discriminate 0 from 1.
7. Compute checksum; if valid, decode humidity and temperature.

**Step 4 — User-Space Code (Pseudo-logic, not Python-only):**
A C program uses `mmap()` on `/dev/gpiomem` to obtain direct register access, achieving **< 10 µs timing jitter** — far better than Python's `RPi.GPIO` equivalent on Linux. Alternatively, the **libgpiod** library exposes command-line tools `gpioset` and `gpioget` for shell scripting.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on CubieBoard Questions:**
> 1. **Voltage mismatch (–2 marks):** Writing that CubieBoard GPIO is 5 V tolerant. **It is NOT** — it is **3.3 V** only.
> 2. **Confusing with microcontroller (–1 mark):** Calling CubieBoard a "microcontroller board". The correct term is **microprocessor-based single-board computer** because it runs a full OS.
> 3. **Missing the OS context (–2 marks):** Describing CubieBoard as "just hardware". You must always mention that it runs **Linux (Debian/Ubuntu)** and supports **Python, Node.js, MQTT brokers, SQL databases**.
> 4. **No SoC mention (–1 mark):** Forgetting the **Allwinner** series SoC. Always specify "Allwinner A10 / A20 / H8" for the corresponding CubieBoard version.
> 5. **Drawing wrong block diagram (–2 marks):** Omitting the **memory controller** or misplacing the **SATA** block in the SoC diagram.
> 6. **Power calculation errors (–2 marks):** Forgetting HDD spin-up current. Always account for the **worst-case inrush**, not just the steady-state value.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **CubieBoard** is an **open-source Linux SBC** by **CubieTech**, based on **Allwinner SoCs** with **ARM Cortex-A** CPUs.
- Popular variants: **CubieBoard 1 (A10)**, **CubieBoard 2 (A20)**, **CubieBoard 5 / CubieTruck (H8)**.
- It is a **microprocessor-based** (not microcontroller) platform — runs full **Linux** or **Android**.
- Key features: **1 GB DDR3 RAM**, **microSD + SATA 2.0 storage**, **10/100 Ethernet**, **2 × USB 2.0 + OTG**, **54-pin GPIO** at **3.3 V logic**.
- **GPIO voltage = 3.3 V only** — never apply 5 V to GPIO pins.
- Functions as an **IoT edge gateway**: hosts **MQTT broker (mosquitto)**, **Node-RED**, **SQLite/InfluxDB**, and forwards data to **AWS IoT / Azure / ThingsBoard** via **TLS**.
- **Power input = 5 V DC**, typical current **0.45 – 2 A**; provision **≥ 30 %** margin for HDD spin-up.
- **ADC reference = 1.8 V, 10-bit**, $V_{LSB} \approx 1.76$ mV.
- **SATA-2 raw bandwidth = 3 Gbps**, useful ≈ **300 MB/s** after encoding.
- Distinct advantage over Raspberry Pi (≤ 3): **SATA + VGA**; disadvantage: **smaller community**.
- Standard application stack: **Linux → Python/Node.js → MQTT broker → Cloud → Dashboard**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Analytics for IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT501 - INTRODUCTION TO INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Analytics for IoT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Analytics for IoT** is defined as the systematic computational process of inspecting, cleansing, transforming, and modelling the massive volumes of heterogeneous data generated by connected IoT devices, sensors, actuators, and edge nodes, in order to discover meaningful patterns, extract actionable insights, support real-time decision-making, and enable autonomous machine-driven actions across the IoT ecosystem.

The formal KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCAMT501, Module 4) frames **Data Analytics for IoT** as the convergence of three engineering disciplines:

1. **Statistical Mathematics** — for pattern discovery and probability modelling.
2. **Machine Intelligence (AI/ML)** — for prediction, classification, and anomaly detection.
3. **Distributed Systems Engineering** — for handling the **4 V's of Big Data**: **Volume**, **Velocity**, **Variety**, and **Veracity**.

## 1.2 The Four Paradigms of Analytics

| Paradigm | Core Question Answered | IoT Example |
|----------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Descriptive Analytics** | "What happened?" | Dashboard showing last 24 h temperature of 500 boilers. |
| **Diagnostic Analytics** | "Why did it happen?" | Root-cause analysis of a turbine vibration spike. |
| **Predictive Analytics** | "What will happen?" | Forecast motor failure in 72 hours using LSTM. |
| **Prescriptive Analytics** | "What should we do?" | Auto-adjust cooling fan RPM to prevent overheating. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: A direct 5–7 mark ESE question is frequently framed on distinguishing these four paradigms with **IoT-specific use cases**. Memorize one industrial example per paradigm.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital ICU

Imagine a **smart hospital Intensive Care Unit (ICU)**:
- **IoT Sensors (Devices)** = Patient monitoring instruments (ECG, BP, SpO₂).
- **IoT Gateway** = The nurse station aggregating readings.
- **Data Lake** = The hospital's centralised patient records database.
- **Analytics Engine** = The senior cardiologist reading live vitals.
- **Prescriptive Output** = Adjusting the ventilator parameters automatically.

Just as the cardiologist never looks at *one single heartbeat* but rather *trends and statistical anomalies*, IoT Data Analytics discards isolated noise and focuses on **temporal patterns, statistical correlation, and machine-learned predictions**.

## 1.4 IoT Data Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate**: Students must categorise IoT data into the following three classes:

- **Structured Data**: Tabular sensor readings (temperature, humidity, GPS coords). Stored in SQL DBs.
- **Semi-Structured Data**: JSON / XML payloads from MQTT brokers or CoAP responses.
- **Unstructured Data**: CCTV footage, audio streams, vibration spectrograms. Requires deep learning.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IoT Analytics Pipeline (Operational Logic)

The **end-to-end IoT Analytics Stack** operates in five structured layers:

1. **Data Ingestion Layer** — Kafka / MQTT brokers collect raw telemetry.
2. **Data Pre-processing Layer** — Cleansing, normalisation, missing-value imputation.
3. **Data Storage Layer** — Time-Series DBs (InfluxDB), NoSQL (MongoDB), Data Lakes (HDFS).
4. **Analytics & ML Layer** — Spark MLlib, TensorFlow, scikit-learn (offline); River, MOA (streaming).
5. **Visualisation & Action Layer** — Grafana, Kibana, AWS QuickSight, automated control actuators.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Layer

- **Why ingestion first?** IoT devices push data at high **velocity** (often >10,000 events/sec) and we cannot afford synchronous processing — hence asynchronous message brokers.
- **Why pre-processing?** Raw sensor data suffers from **noise, drift, outliers**, and missing values due to hardware faults. ML accuracy collapses without cleansing.
- **Why a separate storage layer?** The **3-tier storage strategy** (Edge → Fog → Cloud) balances latency vs. storage cost.
- **Why ML?** Humans cannot manually inspect millions of events. ML models extract **latent features** automatically.
- **Why visualisation?** Decision-makers need **interpretable, real-time dashboards** to act.

## 2.3 Statistical Foundations for IoT Analytics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight**: Even if a question appears "computer science", it is **mathematically grounded**. Memorise the formulae below — they are routinely asked in Part A (3 marks).

### 2.3.1 Central Tendency & Dispersion

| Metric | Formula | Engineering Utility |
|--------|---------|--------------------|
| Arithmetic Mean | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Baseline sensor value |
| Median | $\tilde{x}$ = middle value of sorted $x$ | Robust to outliers (motor vibration) |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}$ | Sensor noise floor |
| Variance | $\sigma^{2}$ | Statistical variance bound |
| Coefficient of Variation | $C_v = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu}$ | Cross-sensor comparison (unit-less) |

### 2.3.2 Probability & Hypothesis Testing

| Concept | Formula | IoT Use |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Bayes' Theorem | $P(A\,\vert\,B) = \dfrac{P(B\,\vert\,A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Fault classification |
| Z-Score (Anomaly) | $z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Outlier detection (threshold $\vert z_i \vert > 3$) |
| Moving Average (Window $k$) | $MA_t = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=t-k+1}^{t} x_i$ | Smoothing noisy telemetry |
| Exponential Smoothing | $S_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha)S_{t-1}$ | Trend forecasting (CPU load) |
| Root Mean Square Error | $RMSE = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | ML model accuracy |

### 2.3.3 Linear Regression (Supervised Baseline)

$$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$$

The optimal coefficients are obtained by **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)**:

$$\beta_1 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}, \quad \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$$

## 2.4 Machine Learning Taxonomy for IoT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note**: A 14-mark question may ask you to map a given IoT problem to one of the four ML categories below. Understand each with at least **two examples**.

| ML Category | Algorithm Examples | IoT Application |
|-------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Supervised** | Decision Tree, SVM, Random Forest, LSTM | Predictive maintenance, energy forecasting |
| **Unsupervised** | K-Means, DBSCAN, PCA | Anomaly clustering, sensor grouping |
| **Reinforcement** | Q-Learning, DQN | HVAC optimisation, autonomous robotics |
| **Deep Learning** | CNN, RNN, Autoencoders | Image recognition on edge cameras |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing**: Reduce unplanned downtime by **30–50%** using predictive maintenance analytics.
- **Smart Agriculture**: ML-based soil moisture analytics save up to **40%** water usage.
- **Smart Healthcare**: Anomaly detection on ECG streams predicts arrhythmia **6 hours in advance**.
- **Smart Grid**: Load forecasting reduces peak power wastage by **15%**.
- **Connected Vehicles**: Real-time analytics on CAN-bus data prevents brake failure.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Linear Regression Coefficients via OLS

We aim to find the straight line $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ that **minimises the sum of squared errors (SSE)**:

$$SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\bigr)^{2}$$

**Step 1**: Take the partial derivative of SSE with respect to $\beta_0$ and set it to zero (stationary point).

$$\frac{\partial SSE}{\partial \beta_0} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i) = 0$$

**Step 2**: Solve for $\beta_0$.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i = N\beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

**Step 3**: Divide throughout by $N$ to express the means.

$$\bar{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \bar{x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$$

**Step 4**: Take the partial derivative of SSE with respect to $\beta_1$ and set it to zero.

$$\frac{\partial SSE}{\partial \beta_1} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i) = 0$$

**Step 5**: Substitute $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ into Step 4 and simplify.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i y_i = (\bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x})\sum x_i + \beta_1 \sum x_i^{2}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i y_i = \bar{y}\sum x_i - \beta_1 \bar{x}\sum x_i + \beta_1 \sum x_i^{2}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i y_i - \bar{y}\sum x_i = \beta_1\Bigl(\sum x_i^{2} - \bar{x}\sum x_i\Bigr)$$

**Step 6**: Recognise the identity $\sum x_i = N\bar{x}$ and rewrite both numerator and denominator in **centred** form.

$$\beta_1 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$$

This is the canonical OLS slope formula.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Z-Score Anomaly Detection Bound

Suppose a temperature sensor reports readings $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N$ with sample mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

**Step 1**: Define the Z-score transformation.

$$z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Step 2**: Compute the expected value of $z_i$.

$$\mathbb{E}[z_i] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}\right] = \frac{1}{\sigma}\bigl(\mathbb{E}[x_i] - \mu\bigr) = \frac{1}{\sigma}(\mu - \mu) = 0$$

**Step 3**: Compute the variance of $z_i$.

$$\mathrm{Var}(z_i) = \mathbb{E}[z_i^{2}] - (\mathbb{E}[z_i])^{2} = \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}\mathbb{E}[(x_i - \mu)^{2}] = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\sigma^{2}} = 1$$

**Step 4**: Apply the empirical **68–95–99.7 rule** of the Normal Distribution.

$$P(\vert z_i \vert \leq 1) \approx 0.6827,\; P(\vert z_i \vert \leq 2) \approx 0.9545,\; P(\vert z_i \vert \leq 3) \approx 0.9973$$

**Step 5**: Conclude the threshold rule.

$$x_i \text{ is an anomaly} \iff \vert z_i \vert > 3 \quad (\text{i.e., } x_i > \mu + 3\sigma \text{ or } x_i < \mu - 3\sigma)$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Linear Regression on IoT Data

A smart-bridge vibration sensor logs the following **5 readings** of vehicle load ($x$, in tonnes) versus bridge deflection ($y$, in mm):

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| 1 | 2 | 1.0 |
| 2 | 4 | 1.8 |
| 3 | 6 | 3.1 |
| 4 | 8 | 4.0 |
| 5 | 10 | 5.2 |

**Step 1**: Compute the means.

$$\bar{x} = \frac{2+4+6+8+10}{5} = 6.0, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1.0+1.8+3.1+4.0+5.2}{5} = 3.02$$

**Step 2**: Compute the centred products.

$$\begin{aligned}
(x_1-\bar{x})(y_1-\bar{y}) &= (-4)(-2.02) &= 8.08 \\
(x_2-\bar{x})(y_2-\bar{y}) &= (-2)(-1.22) &= 2.44 \\
(x_3-\bar{x})(y_3-\bar{y}) &= (0)(0.08)   &= 0.00 \\
(x_4-\bar{x})(y_4-\bar{y}) &= (2)(0.98)   &= 1.96 \\
(x_5-\bar{x})(y_5-\bar{y}) &= (4)(2.18)   &= 8.72
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5}(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y}) = 8.08 + 2.44 + 0 + 1.96 + 8.72 = 21.20$$

**Step 3**: Compute the centred squared $x$ terms.

$$\begin{aligned}
(x_1-\bar{x})^2 &= 16,\; (x_2-\bar{x})^2 = 4,\; (x_3-\bar{x})^2 = 0,\; (x_4-\bar{x})^2 = 4,\; (x_5-\bar{x})^2 = 16 \\
\sum_{i=1}^{5}(x_i-\bar{x})^2 &= 16 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 16 = 40
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4**: Compute $\beta_1$.

$$\beta_1 = \frac{21.20}{40} = 0.53$$

**Step 5**: Compute $\beta_0$.

$$\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x} = 3.02 - (0.53)(6.0) = 3.02 - 3.18 = -0.16$$

**Step 6**: Final regression equation.

$$\hat{y} = -0.16 + 0.53\,x$$

**Step 7**: Predict deflection for a 12-tonne vehicle.

$$\hat{y}(12) = -0.16 + 0.53 \times 12 = -0.16 + 6.36 = 6.20 \text{ mm}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Z-Score Anomaly Detection

Suppose 6 temperature readings (°C) from a smart boiler: $x = \{80, 82, 79, 81, 145, 80\}$.

**Step 1**: Compute mean.

$$\mu = \frac{80+82+79+81+145+80}{6} = \frac{547}{6} = 91.17$$

**Step 2**: Compute standard deviation.

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{(80-91.17)^2 + (82-91.17)^2 + \cdots + (145-91.17)^2}{6}} \approx 24.66$$

**Step 3**: Z-score of 145 °C.

$$z = \frac{145 - 91.17}{24.66} \approx 2.18$$

**Step 4**: Since $\vert z \vert = 2.18 < 3$, it lies within the 95% confidence band — but is suspiciously high and warrants a warning alert in production.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the four paradigms of data analytics with an IoT example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
The four paradigms of data analytics are:
1. **Descriptive Analytics** (1 mark) — Tells *what happened*. Example: A Grafana dashboard displays the last 24 hours of average PM2.5 readings from 50 air-quality sensors deployed across a smart city.
2. **Diagnostic Analytics** (1 mark) — Tells *why it happened*. Example: After a sudden PM2.5 spike, correlation analysis with traffic and wind data reveals vehicular pollution as the root cause.
3. **Predictive Analytics** (0.5 marks) — Tells *what will happen*. Example: An LSTM model forecasts AQI levels 6 hours ahead based on historical patterns.
4. **Prescriptive Analytics** (0.5 marks) — Tells *what to do*. Example: The system automatically issues a "stay indoors" advisory and triggers traffic-signal rerouting.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six characteristics of Big Data (the 6 V's) in the context of IoT.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
1. **Volume** (0.5) — Petabytes of telemetry from millions of devices.
2. **Velocity** (0.5) — Real-time streams at >10,000 events/sec.
3. **Variety** (0.5) — Structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data.
4. **Veracity** (0.5) — Trustworthiness of noisy sensor data.
5. **Value** (0.5) — Business insights extracted from raw data.
6. **Variability** (0.5) — Fluctuations in data rate and format over time.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse *Predictive* with *Prescriptive* analytics — this is the **single most common mistake** costing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Always** justify your ML algorithm choice with a one-line IoT reason. A bare algorithm name without context loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Always** include a real-time vs batch processing distinction in stream analytics answers.
> 4. **Avoid** writing generic ML definitions. Tie every sentence to an **IoT sensor / actuator scenario**.
> 5. **Show** the OLS derivation steps explicitly if regression appears — partial marks are awarded per step.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **IoT Data Analytics Pipeline** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer and differentiate between **batch processing** and **stream processing**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat architecture diagram, describe how **Apache Kafka and Spark Streaming** can be combined to perform real-time analytics on IoT data. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Introduction (1 mark)**: An IoT analytics pipeline is a layered software architecture that ingests, processes, stores, analyses, and visualises data from connected devices. It is designed to handle the **4 V's of Big Data**.

**Layer-wise Explanation (5 marks)**:
1. **Ingestion Layer**: Tools like **Apache Kafka**, **RabbitMQ**, or **MQTT brokers** receive high-velocity telemetry from IoT devices in publish-subscribe fashion.
2. **Pre-processing Layer**: Performs data cleansing (noise removal using filters), normalisation (min-max scaling), and imputation of missing values (mean/median substitution or KNN-imputation).
3. **Storage Layer**: Hybrid storage strategy using **InfluxDB** (time-series), **MongoDB** (semi-structured), and **HDFS** (raw data lake).
4. **Analytics Layer**: Hosts ML models — supervised (Random Forest, SVM), unsupervised (K-Means, DBSCAN), and deep learning (LSTM for time-series).
5. **Visualisation & Action Layer**: Tools like **Grafana** and **Kibana** render real-time dashboards; **alert engines** push notifications via SMS or MQTT.

**Batch vs Stream Processing (1 mark)**:
- **Batch**: Data accumulated over hours/days, processed in bulk (e.g., Hadoop MapReduce) — used for daily energy billing.
- **Stream**: Data processed record-by-record in milliseconds (e.g., Apache Flink, Spark Streaming) — used for real-time intrusion detection.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Kafka + Spark Streaming Architecture (4 marks)**:
- **IoT Devices** publish JSON payloads to **Kafka topics** (e.g., `topic_sensor_temp`).
- **Kafka Broker** partitions the topic across multiple brokers for fault tolerance and parallel consumption.
- **Spark Streaming** subscribes to these topics using a **Direct Stream** or **Structured Streaming API**.
- Spark applies transformations: `map`, `filter`, `reduceByKey`, `window`, and MLlib models.
- Processed results are written to **Cassandra/InfluxDB** and pushed to **Grafana** dashboards.

**Why this combination is ideal for IoT (2 marks)**:
- **Kafka** decouples producers (devices) from consumers (analytics), absorbing traffic bursts.
- **Spark** provides in-memory distributed computation with **exactly-once semantics**.
- Latency achievable: **sub-second to 2 seconds**, suitable for industrial IoT control loops.

**Use Case (1 mark)**: Real-time anomaly detection in a wind turbine farm — Kafka streams vibration data; Spark applies a trained Isolation-Forest model; alerts trigger turbine shutdown within 1 second.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss **supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning** algorithms suitable for IoT analytics. Provide one IoT application for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, derive the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression coefficients** for a linear model and apply it to a 5-point IoT dataset. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Supervised Learning (2 marks)**: Algorithms trained on labelled (input, output) data.
- *Algorithms*: Decision Tree, Random Forest, SVM, LSTM.
- *IoT Application*: Predicting compressor failure 48 hours in advance using historical sensor logs and failure labels.

**Unsupervised Learning (2 marks)**: Algorithms that discover hidden structure in unlabelled data.
- *Algorithms*: K-Means clustering, DBSCAN, PCA.
- *IoT Application*: Clustering 10,000 smart-meter customers into "high", "medium", "low" usage profiles without pre-defined labels.

**Reinforcement Learning (2 marks)**: An agent learns an optimal policy by interacting with the environment and receiving rewards/penalties.
- *Algorithms*: Q-Learning, Deep Q-Network (DQN), SARSA.
- *IoT Application*: An HVAC controller learns to minimise energy cost while maintaining room temperature by adjusting fan speed based on occupancy and electricity price signals.

**Comparison Table (1 mark)**:
- Supervised requires labelled data; unsupervised does not; reinforcement learns via trial-and-error.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation (3 marks)**: Already fully derived in **Section 3.1** of these notes — students must reproduce the steps leading to:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}, \quad \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (4 marks)**: Reproduce the bridge-deflection example from **Section 3.3**, step by step, showing:
1. Means: $\bar{x}=6.0$, $\bar{y}=3.02$ (1 mark).
2. Numerator $\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y}) = 21.20$ (1 mark).
3. Denominator $\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2 = 40$ (1 mark).
4. Final equation $\hat{y} = -0.16 + 0.53x$ and prediction for $x=12$: $\hat{y}=6.20$ mm (1 mark).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist** — Read this the morning of your exam.

- ✅ **Data Analytics for IoT** = Inspection + Cleansing + Transformation + Modelling of IoT-generated data.
- ✅ **Four Paradigms**: Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive — *never* confuse the last two.
- ✅ **6 V's of Big Data**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value, Variability.
- ✅ **5-Layer Pipeline**: Ingestion → Pre-processing → Storage → Analytics → Visualisation/Action.
- ✅ **Batch vs Stream**: Hadoop = batch; Spark Streaming / Flink = stream.
- ✅ **Z-Score Anomaly Rule**: $\vert z_i \vert > 3$ ⇒ likely anomaly.
- ✅ **OLS Slope**: $\beta_1 = \dfrac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}$.
- ✅ **ML Taxonomy**: Supervised (labelled), Unsupervised (unlabelled), Reinforcement (reward-based), Deep Learning (neural nets).

- ✅ **Real-Time Tools**: Kafka + Spark Streaming + InfluxDB + Grafana.
- ✅ **Always** state latency, throughput, or accuracy in numeric terms — examiners reward quantitative thinking.
- ✅ **Pitfall**: Do not write generic ML answers without an **IoT sensor context**.
